**(b)** Design a **positive clamper circuit** to shift a 6 V peak sine wave such that its positive peak becomes +10 V. Show all calculations and the final output waveform. Assume $f = 1$ kHz, ideal diode. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Biased Positive Shunt Clipper (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A biased positive shunt clipper consists of:
- AC voltage source $v_{in}(t) = 10 \sin(\omega t)$ V
- Series resistor R (1 kΩ, current limiter)
- Diode D in **shunt** (parallel with the output), with **anode at ground** and **cathode at the output node**
- DC reference battery $V_{REF} = 5$ V in **series with the diode** (cathode side, raising the clipping threshold)
- Load resistor R_L (10 kΩ) in parallel with the output

**Step 2 — Threshold Calculation (2 marks):**
For the diode to conduct, the cathode-to-anode voltage must reach the diode's cut-in voltage. With the reference battery inserted (cathode connected to $+V_{REF}$ relative to the anode when the diode is OFF):

$$V_{TH} = V_{REF} + V_\gamma = 5 + 0 = 5 \text{ V (ideal)}$$

**Step 3 — Output Waveform Analysis (2 marks):**
- **When $v_{in} < 5$ V:** Diode is reverse-biased (OFF) → $v_{out} = v_{in}$ (linear region).
- **When $v_{in} \geq 5$ V:** Diode is forward-biased (ON) → $v_{out} = 5$ V (clipped flat top).

**Step 4 — Final Output Range (1 mark):**
- Output maximum: $+5$ V (clipped).
- Output minimum: $-10$ V (full negative half-cycle passes through).
- Positive half-cycle duration above 5 V is "clipped off."

**Sketch (1 mark — full credit only if both halves shown correctly):**
- Input: full sine wave from $-10$ V to $+10$ V.
- Output: sine wave from $-10$ V to $+5$ V, with a flat region at $+5$ V during the upper portion of the positive half-cycle.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Positive Clamper Design (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify Required Shift (2 marks):**
The original positive peak is $V_m = 6$ V. We want the new positive peak to be $+10$ V. So the required **upward DC shift** is:

$$\Delta V = 10 \text{ V} - 6 \text{ V} = 4 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Verify Topology (1 mark):**
For an unbiased positive clamper, the natural DC shift is $+V_m = +6$ V, which would move the positive peak to $+12$ V (not $+10$ V). So we need a **biased** positive clamper with a reference voltage that **reduces the shift** by 2 V.

**Step 3 — Apply Formula (2 marks):**
For a positive biased clamper (with the reference battery oriented to reduce the shift):

$$V_{out,peak} = V_m + (V_m - V_{REF}) = 2V_m - V_{REF}$$

Setting this equal to 10 V:

$$2(6) - V_{REF} = 10 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{REF} = 2 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Output Range (1 mark):**
With $V_{REF} = 2$ V and ideal diode:
- Positive peak of output: $+10$ V ✓ (as required)
- Negative peak of output: $V_{out,min} = -V_m + (V_m - V_{REF}) = -2$ V
- DC shift applied: $+4$ V
- Peak-to-peak amplitude: $10 - (-2) = 12$ V = $2V_m$ ✓ (preserved)

**Step 5 — Component Selection (1 mark):**
For $f = 1$ kHz, $T = 1$ ms, $T/2 = 0.5$ ms.
Choose $C = 1$ μF and $R_L = 100$ kΩ → $\tau_d = 100$ ms ≫ 0.5 ms ✓.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the operation of a **negative clamper** with a neat circuit diagram. Derive the expression for the steady-state output voltage. Sketch the input-output waveforms for $v_{in}(t) = 8 \sin(\omega t)$ V. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the **combinational clipper** (double-diode) circuit shown below, determine the output waveform for an input sine wave of 12 V peak. The reference voltages are $V_1 = +4$ V (positive clip level) and $V_2 = -3$ V (negative clip level). Assume ideal diodes. **(7 marks)**

*Combinational clipper topology:*
- Diode D1 (anode at output, cathode at $+V_1 = +4$ V) clips the positive excursion at $+4$ V.
- Diode D2 (cathode at output, anode at $-V_2 = -3$ V) clips the negative excursion at $-3$ V.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Negative Clamper (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A negative clamper consists of:
- AC source $v_{in}(t) = 8\sin(\omega t)$ V
- Capacitor C in series with the signal path
- Diode D with **anode at the output node** and **cathode at ground** (this orientation makes the diode conduct on the **positive** half-cycle, charging the capacitor to clamp the output downward)
- Load resistor R_L at the output

**Step 2 — Operation During First Positive Half-Cycle (2 marks):**
As $v_{in}$ rises from 0 to $+8$ V, the output node tries to follow. The diode becomes **forward-biased** (anode positive, cathode at 0 V) and conducts. The output is clamped to 0 V (ideal diode). The capacitor charges to:

$$v_C = v_{in} - v_{out} = +8 - 0 = +8 \text{ V}$$

(Left plate positive, right plate at 0 V.)

**Step 3 — Steady-State Output (2 marks):**
After the positive peak, $v_{in}$ begins to decrease. The diode becomes reverse-biased and turns OFF. The capacitor is now trapped with $v_C = +8$ V. KVL gives:

$$v_{out} = v_{in} - v_C = v_{in} - 8$$

**Step 4 — Waveform Sketch (1 mark):**
- Original input: ranges from $-8$ V to $+8$ V.
- Clamped output: ranges from $-16$ V to $0$ V.
- DC shift: $-8$ V (downward).
- Peak-to-peak: $16$ V (unchanged).

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Combinational Clipper (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify Clipping Levels (2 marks):**
- **Upper clipping level:** Determined by D1. When the output tries to exceed $+V_1 = +4$ V, D1 becomes forward-biased and clamps the output at $+4$ V.
- **Lower clipping level:** Determined by D2. When the output tries to go below $-V_2 = -3$ V, D2 becomes forward-biased and clamps the output at $-3$ V.

**Step 2 — Diode State Regions (2 marks):**
- **Region 1 ($-3 \text{ V} < v_{in} < +4$ V):** Both diodes are reverse-biased (OFF). Output follows input: $v_{out} = v_{in}$.
- **Region 2 ($v_{in} \geq +4$ V):** D1 is ON, D2 is OFF. Output clamped at $+4$ V.
- **Region 3 ($v_{in} \leq -3$ V):** D2 is ON, D1 is OFF. Output clamped at $-3$ V.

**Step 3 — Output Waveform Description (2 marks):**
For an input sine wave of 12 V peak:
- The input is **clipped on both sides**: positive peaks above $+4$ V are flattened to $+4$ V, and negative peaks below $-3$ V are flattened to $-3$ V.
- The middle portion (between $-3$ V and $+4$ V) passes through linearly.
- The resulting waveform resembles a **trapezoid with rounded sides** (or a sine wave with both top and bottom flattened asymmetrically).

**Step 4 — Sketch Verification (1 mark):**
Input: sine wave from $-12$ V to $+12$ V.
Output: trapezoidal-like wave with top flat at $+4$ V (clipped from $+4$ V to $+12$ V, a 8 V region removed) and bottom flat at $-3$ V (clipped from $-12$ V to $-3$ V, a 9 V region removed).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 EXAMINER'S MARKS-LOSS ALERT — READ BEFORE WRITING YOUR EXAM 🚨**
>
> **1. Forgetting the diode cut-in voltage $V_\gamma$:**
> In **waveform-sketching** questions, assume $V_\gamma = 0$ V (ideal diode). In **numerical/design** questions where $V_\gamma$ is explicitly mentioned (especially 0.7 V for Si), you **must include it** in the threshold calculation: $V_{TH} = V_{REF} + V_\gamma$. Missing this costs 1–2 marks per question.
>
> **2. Confusing clipper and clamper polarity conventions:**
> A "positive clipper" removes the **positive** half-cycle (output is purely negative or zero-clipped). A "positive clamper" **shifts the waveform upward** (positive direction). The naming refers to what is being **acted upon**, not the output's polarity. Many students invert this logic — DO NOT BE ONE OF THEM.
>
> **3. Wrong capacitor polarity in clamper steady-state diagrams:**
> Always show the **capacitor voltage polarity** (left plate + or −, right plate the opposite) in your steady-state circuit diagram. KTU examiners award 1 mark specifically for the correct polarity mark on the capacitor symbol.
>
> **4. Sketching the clamper output on the wrong time axis:**
> The output waveform must be drawn **time-aligned** with the input waveform (same time scale, same x-axis). If the input's positive peak is at $t = T/4$, the output's positive peak must also be at $t = T/4$. Misaligned waveforms lose 1 mark.
>
> **5. Not specifying the time constant condition in clamper design:**
> For a clamper to function properly, you **must mention** the condition $R_L C \gg T/2$. Examiners test this explicitly in design questions. Omitting it costs 1 mark.
>
> **6. Calling a clamper a "DC source":**
> A clamper does NOT add external DC — it **redistributes** the existing DC component by charging a capacitor to the input peak. Writing "the clamper adds 6 V DC from a battery" loses 1 mark for conceptual error.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST (Read this 5 minutes before the exam) 📋**

- **Clipper = LIMIT; Clamper = LIFT.** This is the #1 mantra.

- **Clipper changes the peak amplitudes; clamper preserves the peak-to-peak amplitude.**

- **Series clipper:** Diode in series with the load. When diode is OFF, output = 0. When diode is ON, output = V_in.

- **Shunt clipper:** Diode in parallel with the load. When diode is OFF, output = V_in. When diode is ON, output = V_γ (or 0 V ideal).

- **Positive clipper:** Diode conducts on positive half-cycle, removing the positive peak. Output is the negative half-cycle plus a flat region at 0 V (or V_γ).

- **Negative clipper:** Diode conducts on negative half-cycle, removing the negative peak. Output is the positive half-cycle plus a flat region at 0 V (or V_γ).

- **Biased clipper threshold:** $V_{TH} = V_{REF} + V_\gamma$ (for positive bias); $-V_{TH} = -(V_{REF} + V_\gamma)$ (for negative bias).

- **Positive clamper output:** $v_{out} = v_{in} + V_m$ — shifts the waveform so the **negative peak sits at 0 V** and the **positive peak is at $+2V_m$**.

- **Negative clamper output:** $v_{out} = v_{in} - V_m$ — shifts the waveform so the **positive peak sits at 0 V** and the **negative peak is at $-2V_m$**.

- **Biased positive clamper:** $v_{out} = v_{in} + (V_m - V_{REF})$ — positive peak becomes $+2V_m - V_{REF}$.

- **Time constant rule for clampers:** $R_L C \gg T/2$ (so capacitor does not discharge significantly between cycles); $R_F C \ll T/2$ (so capacitor charges quickly when diode conducts).

- **Combinational clipper:** Two diodes with two reference voltages create a **window** (or band) of unclipped output — values between the two reference levels pass through, values outside are clipped.

- **Zener clipper:** Two back-to-back zener diodes produce a **symmetric** clipper with clipping levels $\pm(V_Z + V_\gamma)$.

- **Practical application of clampers:** DC restoration in TV receivers (the clamper reinserts the lost DC component of the composite video signal after AC coupling).

- **Practical application of clippers:** Overvoltage protection in sensitive measurement instruments; AM noise limiters; function-generator output level shifting.

- **Ideal diode vs. practical Si diode:** For waveform-sketching questions, use $V_\gamma = 0$ V. For numerical problems, use $V_\gamma = 0.7$ V unless otherwise stated.

- **KTU Viva-Voce Favorite Question:** "What happens if you replace the diode in a positive clamper with a resistor?" — Answer: No clamping action occurs. The circuit degenerates into a **high-pass filter** (CR differentiator at low frequencies) because there is no nonlinear element to charge the capacitor asymmetrically. The output would be a sine wave with a slight phase shift and amplitude attenuation, but no DC shift.

- **Common Lab Observation (PCEOL307):** In the actual hardware experiment, you will observe a **small tilt** in the clamper's flat regions (the output is not perfectly flat) because the capacitor discharges slightly through the load during the non-conducting half-cycle. This is normal and is a hallmark of a real (non-ideal) clamper. Theoretically, the tilt is zero.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design and Testing of Series Voltage Regulator Using Zener Diode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL307 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Design and Testing of Series Voltage Regulator Using Zener Diode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

A **Series Voltage Regulator using Zener Diode** is a closed-loop (in its discrete form, an open-loop feed-forward) linear voltage regulation circuit in which a **Zener diode** provides a stable reference voltage, and a **series pass transistor (BJT/FET)** acts as a variable resistance element connected in *series* between the unregulated DC input and the regulated load. The transistor automatically adjusts its collector-emitter voltage ($V_{CE}$) to absorb fluctuations in input voltage or load current, thereby maintaining a **constant DC output voltage** at the load terminals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCEOL307):** The experiment aims to design a regulator for a specified output voltage (e.g., **+9 V** or **+5 V**) from a varying unregulated DC input (e.g., **12 V – 18 V**), fabricate it on a breadboard/PCB, and verify parameters such as **Line Regulation (LR)**, **Load Regulation (LDR)**, and **Ripple Rejection**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Water Tank and Faucet Analogy"**
> Imagine a water tank supplying water to your house. The water level in the tank fluctuates (unregulated input), and the demand from various taps keeps changing (load variation). To ensure a *constant, steady stream* of water at a fixed pressure:
> 1. A **Zener Diode** is like a **calibrated pressure gauge** — it constantly monitors the reference pressure (voltage).
> 2. A **Series Pass Transistor** is like an **automatic pressure-adjusting valve** placed *in series* with the pipeline. If tank pressure rises, the valve closes slightly; if more taps open, it opens more.
> 3. The **Series Resistor ($R_S$)** supplies a small biasing current to keep the pressure gauge (Zener) awake in its breakdown region.
>
> The net result: No matter how wildly the source pressure or the load demand changes, the user always gets a constant, smooth flow. This is *exactly* what a series voltage regulator does with electrons!

### Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Unregulated Input Voltage ($V_{in}$):** The raw, fluctuating DC supply (e.g., from a bridge rectifier + filter capacitor).
- **Regulated Output Voltage ($V_{out}$):** The stable, constant DC voltage delivered to the load.
- **Zener Voltage ($V_Z$):** The reverse breakdown voltage of the Zener diode, used as the reference.
- **Series Pass Element:** The BJT/FET in series with the load.
- **Quiescent Current ($I_Q$):** The current drawn by the regulator itself, with no load connected.
- **Line Regulation (LR):** The change in output voltage per unit change in input voltage (measured in %/V or mV/V).
- **Load Regulation (LDR):** The change in output voltage from no-load to full-load condition.
- **Ripple Rejection (PSRR):** The ability of the regulator to suppress AC ripple present in the input DC.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Principle — The "Why" and "How"

The series regulator operates on a fundamental principle: **Excess voltage is dropped across the series pass transistor**, keeping the output constant.

1. **The Zener Diode as Reference:** When reverse-biased beyond its breakdown voltage ($V_Z$), the Zener diode maintains a nearly constant voltage across itself, regardless of the current flowing through it (within its rated range, $I_{Z(min)}$ to $I_{Z(max)}$). This is the *reference*.

2. **Biasing the Zener ($R_S$ Selection):** A resistor $R_S$ is connected between the unregulated input $V_{in}$ and the Zener cathode. This resistor drops the excess voltage and supplies a current $I_Z$ to keep the Zener in breakdown:
   $$I_Z = \frac{V_{in} - V_Z}{R_S}$$
   $R_S$ must be chosen such that even at the *minimum* $V_{in}$ and *maximum* load current, $I_Z \geq I_{Z(min)}$.

3. **The Emitter Follower Configuration:** An NPN transistor $Q_1$ is wired as an **emitter follower** (common-collector). Its **base** is tied to the Zener, its **collector** receives the unregulated $V_{in}$, and its **emitter** supplies $V_{out}$.
   - The base voltage $V_B = V_Z$.
   - The emitter voltage $V_E = V_B - V_{BE} = V_Z - 0.7\text{ V}$.
   - Therefore, **$V_{out} = V_Z - V_{BE}$**.

4. **Automatic Regulation Mechanism:**
   - **If $V_{in}$ increases:** $V_{out}$ tries to rise. This raises $V_B$ (Zener is constant, so this is small), but more importantly, it increases $V_{CE}$ of $Q_1$, which absorbs the extra voltage. $V_{out}$ remains pinned at $V_Z - V_{BE}$.
   - **If load current $I_L$ increases:** $V_{out}$ tries to drop. The base voltage is held by the Zener, so $V_{BE}$ increases, driving the transistor harder into conduction, restoring $V_{out}$.

5. **Why "Series" and not "Shunt"?** In a shunt regulator, the Zener itself carries the *entire* excess current to ground, wasting power. In a **series regulator**, the transistor carries only the *load current* and dissipates the *excess voltage*, leading to **better efficiency** and **higher current capability**.

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Zener Current | $I_Z = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_Z}{R_S}$ | A / mA | Must satisfy $I_{Z(min)} \leq I_Z \leq I_{Z(max)}$ |
| 2 | Output Voltage | $V_{out} = V_Z - V_{BE}$ | V | For NPN emitter follower; $V_{BE} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$ |
| 3 | Emitter (Load) Current | $I_L = I_E = \dfrac{V_{out}}{R_L}$ | A | Equals load current |
| 4 | Transistor Power Dissipation | $P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L$ | W | Critical for heatsink design |
| 5 | Line Regulation | $LR = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \times 100\,\%$ | %/V | Smaller is better |
| 6 | Load Regulation | $LDR = V_{NL} - V_{FL}$ | mV or V | Difference between no-load and full-load $V_{out}$ |
| 7 | Ripple Rejection | $RR = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{ripple(in)}}{V_{ripple(out)}}\right)$ | dB | Higher is better (typically 40–60 dB) |
| 8 | Series Resistor Design | $R_S = \dfrac{V_{in(min)} - V_Z}{I_{Z(min)} + I_B(max)}$ | $\Omega$ | Use worst-case minimums |
| 9 | Maximum Zener Current | $I_{Z(max)} = \dfrac{P_{Z(rated)}}{V_Z}$ | A | From Zener datasheet (e.g., 1N4733 = 1 W) |
| 10 | Base Current | $I_B = \dfrac{I_L}{h_{FE}}$ | A | Must be supplied via $R_S$ |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Legacy Linear Regulators (78xx series):** The conceptual ancestor of IC regulators like the **LM7805, LM7812, LM317**. Every modern low-dropout (LDO) regulator traces its design DNA back to this Zener + pass transistor topology.
- **On-board local regulation** in mixed-signal PCBs to power noise-sensitive analog stages (e.g., ADC reference inputs, op-amp rails) from a noisy digital supply rail.
- **Reference voltage generation** for instrumentation amplifiers and sensor excitation.
- **Bench power supplies** where moderate current (< 1 A) and low noise are required.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Worked Design Example (KTU Standard Problem)

> **Design Statement:** Design a series voltage regulator using a Zener diode to deliver **$V_{out} = +9\text{ V}$** at a maximum load current of **$I_L = 100\text{ mA}$** from an unregulated input that varies between **$V_{in(min)} = 12\text{ V}$** and **$V_{in(max)} = 18\text{ V}$**. Use a Zener diode with $V_Z = 9.7\text{ V}$, $I_{Z(min)} = 5\text{ mA}$, $P_Z = 1\text{ W}$, and a transistor with $V_{BE} = 0.7\text{ V}$, $h_{FE} = 50$.

#### Step 1: Verify the Zener Voltage Selection

The design requires $V_{out} = 9\text{ V}$. The output is given by:
$$V_{out} = V_Z - V_{BE}$$

Substituting the standard $V_{BE} = 0.7\text{ V}$:
$$V_Z = V_{out} + V_{BE} = 9 + 0.7 = 9.7\text{ V}$$

The selected Zener (9.7 V) is **correct** ✓.

#### Step 2: Compute the Required Base Current

The base current needed to drive the pass transistor is:
$$I_B = \frac{I_L}{h_{FE}} = \frac{100\text{ mA}}{50} = 2\text{ mA}$$

#### Step 3: Determine the Worst-Case Zener Current

Under worst-case conditions ($V_{in} = V_{in(min)} = 12\text{ V}$), the Zener current must still be at least $I_{Z(min)} = 5\text{ mA}$ *after* supplying the base current:
$$I_{R_S} = I_Z + I_B$$
$$I_{R_S(min)} = I_{Z(min)} + I_B = 5 + 2 = 7\text{ mA}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *minimum* current that must flow through $R_S$. We size $R_S$ based on this worst case.

#### Step 4: Calculate the Series Resistor $R_S$

Using Ohm's Law at the worst case ($V_{in} = 12\text{ V}$):
$$R_S = \frac{V_{in(min)} - V_Z}{I_{R_S(min)}}$$

$$
R_S = \frac{12 - 9.7}{7 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2.3}{0.007} \approx 328.57\,\Omega
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Practice:** Choose the nearest **standard E12/E24 value**, typically **$R_S = 330\,\Omega$** (or $390\,\Omega$ for a small safety margin).

#### Step 5: Verify the Zener Power Rating

The maximum Zener current occurs at $V_{in(max)} = 18\text{ V}$ (assuming no load, worst-case power):
$$I_{Z(max)} = \frac{V_{in(max)} - V_Z}{R_S} = \frac{18 - 9.7}{330} = \frac{8.3}{330} \approx 25.15\text{ mA}$$

Zener power dissipation:
$$P_Z = V_Z \cdot I_{Z(max)} = 9.7 \times 0.02515 \approx 0.244\text{ W}$$

Since $P_Z < 1\text{ W}$ (the rated power), the Zener is **safe** ✓.

#### Step 6: Transistor Power Dissipation Check

The worst-case dissipation on $Q_1$ occurs at **maximum $V_{in}$** and **maximum $I_L$**:
$$P_D = (V_{in(max)} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L$$
$$P_D = (18 - 9) \times 0.1 = 0.9\text{ W}$$

> [!WARNING]
> The transistor dissipates **0.9 W** — this requires a **small clip-on heatsink** or a transistor in a TO-220 package (e.g., **BD139**, $P_D$ rating ≥ 1.25 W).

#### Step 7: Line Regulation Calculation

Suppose on the bench, $V_{out}$ changes from **9.02 V** (at $V_{in} = 12\text{ V}$) to **8.98 V** (at $V_{in} = 18\text{ V}$):
$$\Delta V_{out} = 9.02 - 8.98 = 0.04\text{ V} = 40\text{ mV}$$
$$\Delta V_{in} = 18 - 12 = 6\text{ V}$$

$$LR = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} = \frac{0.04}{6} \approx 6.67\text{ mV/V}$$

As a percentage:
$$LR\,\% = 6.67 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 = 0.667\,\%/\text{V}$$

#### Step 8: Load Regulation Calculation

With $V_{in}$ held constant at 15 V:
- No-load output: $V_{NL} = 9.05\text{ V}$
- Full-load output: $V_{FL} = 8.95\text{ V}$

$$LDR = V_{NL} - V_{FL} = 9.05 - 8.95 = 0.10\text{ V} = 100\text{ mV}$$

Expressed as a percentage of $V_{out}$:
$$LDR\,\% = \frac{0.10}{9.0} \times 100 \approx 1.11\,\%$$

#### Step 9: Efficiency Estimation

$$\eta = \frac{V_{out} \cdot I_L}{V_{in} \cdot (I_L + I_Q)} \times 100\,\%$$

Assuming $I_Q \approx 7\text{ mA}$, at $V_{in} = 15\text{ V}$, $I_L = 100\text{ mA}$:
$$
\eta = \frac{9 \times 0.1}{15 \times 0.107} \times 100\,\% = \frac{0.9}{1.605} \times 100\,\% \approx 56.07\,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This efficiency is *typical* for linear regulators. It is the major reason switching regulators (SMPS) are used for high-current/high-voltage applications.

### Final Bill of Materials (BOM)

| Component | Value/Part Number | Specification |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------|
| $Q_1$ (NPN BJT) | BD139 | $V_{CEO} \geq 25\text{ V}$, $I_C \geq 1.5\text{ A}$ |
| Zener $D_Z$ | 1N4739 (9.1 V) or 1N4742 (12 V, depending on design) | $1\text{ W}$, $I_{Z(min)} = 5\text{ mA}$ |
| $R_S$ | 330 Ω, 0.25 W | Carbon film |
| Heatsink | Clip-on for TO-220 | Thermal resistance ≤ 20 °C/W |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Circuit Schematic (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    VinNode["UNREGULATED DC INPUT V_in 12V TO 18V"] --> RsNode["R_S 330 OHM BIAS RESISTOR"]
    VinNode --> CollectorNode["COLLECTOR Q1 BD139 NPN BJT"]
    RsNode --> ZenerNode["ZENER DIODE 1N4739 V_Z 9.1V REFERENCE"]
    ZenerNode --> GndZ["GROUND 0V"]
    ZenerNode --> BaseNode["BASE OF Q1"]
    CollectorNode --> PassTransistor["Q1 BD139 EMITTER FOLLOWER"]
    PassTransistor --> EmitterNode["EMITTER V_out REGULATED OUTPUT"]
    EmitterNode --> LoadNode["LOAD R_L VARIABLE 0 TO 100 OHM"]
    EmitterNode --> VoutMeter["OUTPUT VOLTMETER V_out 9V NOMINAL"]
    VinNode --> VinMeter["INPUT VOLTMETER V_in MONITOR"]
    LoadNode --> GndL["GROUND 0V"]
    GndZ --- GndL
```

### 4.2 Functional Signal-Flow Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph STAGE_A["STAGE 1 UNREGULATED DC SOURCE"]
        A1["TRANSFORMER 230V TO 15V AC"] --> A2["BRIDGE RECTIFIER 4 X 1N4007"]
        A2 --> A3["FILTER CAPACITOR 1000 uF 25V"]
        A3 --> A4["RIPPLE CONTAINING DC V_in"]
    end

    subgraph STAGE_B["STAGE 2 SERIES VOLTAGE REGULATOR"]
        A4 --> B1["R_S 330 OHM"]
        A4 --> B2["Q1 BD139 COLLECTOR"]
        B1 --> B3["ZENER 1N4739 BREAKDOWN REGION"]
        B3 --> B4["BASE OF Q1 REFERENCE 9.1V"]
        B4 --> B2
        B2 --> B5["EMITTER OF Q1 V_out 8.4V TO 9V"]
    end

    subgraph STAGE_C["STAGE 3 OUTPUT AND LOAD"]
        B5 --> C1["OUTPUT TERMINAL POSITIVE"]
        B5 --> C2["LED INDICATOR WITH 1K RESISTOR"]

```

### 4.3 Regulation Mechanism — Cause-and-Effect Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    Event1["DISTURBANCE 1 V_in INCREASES"] --> Effect1["V_ce OF Q1 INCREASES TO ABSORB EXCESS"]
    Event1 --> Effect2["I_through_R_S INCREASES"]
    Effect2 --> Effect3["I_z INCREASES ZENER STAYS IN BREAKDOWN"]
    Effect3 --> Result1["V_base HELD CONSTANT AT V_Z"]
    Effect1 --> Result1
    Result1 --> FinalOutput["V_out CONSTANT AT V_Z MINUS V_BE"]

    Event2["DISTURBANCE 2 I_load INCREASES"] --> Effect4["V_out TRIES TO DROP"]
    Effect4 --> Effect5["V_be INCREASES BJT DRIVES HARDER"]
    Effect5 --> Result2["I_collector INCREASES RESTORING V_out"]
    Result2 --> FinalOutput
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State the function of a Zener diode in a series voltage regulator. Why is it connected in reverse bias?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Zener diode provides a **stable reference voltage** ($V_Z$) for the regulator circuit. It is connected in **reverse bias** because Zener diodes are designed to operate in their **reverse breakdown region**, where the voltage across them remains nearly constant ($V_Z$) over a wide range of reverse currents. If connected in forward bias, it would behave like an ordinary silicon diode (~0.7 V) and could not serve as a voltage reference. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Line Regulation and Load Regulation. What are their ideal values for a perfect voltage regulator?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Line Regulation (LR):** The change in regulated output voltage ($V_{out}$) for a corresponding change in unregulated input voltage ($V_{in}$), typically expressed in **mV/V** or **%/V**.
- **Load Regulation (LDR):** The change in output voltage as the load current varies from **no-load to full-load**, expressed in **mV** or **%**.
- For an **ideal** voltage regulator, both **Line Regulation = 0 mV/V** and **Load Regulation = 0 mV**. (3 marks)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **series voltage regulator using a Zener diode and a pass transistor**. Derive the expression for the regulated output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Diagram:** *(Refer to Section 4.1 schematic above)*

**Working Principle:**
The unregulated DC input $V_{in}$ is applied to the collector of the NPN pass transistor $Q_1$ and to the series resistor $R_S$. The Zener diode $D_Z$ is reverse-biased through $R_S$ and is maintained in its breakdown region, providing a constant reference voltage $V_Z$ at the base of $Q_1$.

Since $Q_1$ is wired as an **emitter follower (common-collector)**, the output voltage at the emitter is:
$$V_{out} = V_B - V_{BE} = V_Z - V_{BE}$$

For a standard silicon transistor, $V_{BE} \approx 0.7\text{ V}$, hence $V_{out} = V_Z - 0.7\text{ V}$.

**Regulation Action:**
1. When $V_{in}$ rises, the collector-emitter voltage $V_{CE}$ of $Q_1$ increases to absorb the excess voltage, keeping $V_{out}$ constant.
2. When load current $I_L$ increases, $V_{out}$ tends to fall, increasing $V_{BE}$ and driving $Q_1$ harder into conduction to restore $V_{out}$.

**Components:**
- $R_S$ = Bias resistor for the Zener
- $D_Z$ = Voltage reference
- $Q_1$ = Series pass element

**(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for working, 1 for output expression, 1 for regulation action)**

---

**(b)** Design a Zener diode–transistor series regulator to deliver a regulated output of **9 V at 80 mA** from an input that varies from **12 V to 18 V**. Assume a Zener of 9.1 V, $I_{Z(min)} = 5\text{ mA}$, $V_{BE} = 0.7\text{ V}$, and $h_{FE} = 50$. Calculate the value of $R_S$ and the transistor power dissipation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Output Equation Check**
$$V_{out} = V_Z - V_{BE} = 9.1 - 0.7 = 8.4\text{ V}$$

To get exactly 9 V, we would need $V_Z = 9.7\text{ V}$ (using a different Zener, e.g., 1N4739A or 1N4740). **For this problem, we accept $V_{out} \approx 8.4\text{ V}$ using the given 9.1 V Zener** (academic simplification). *(2 marks)*

**Step 2: Base Current**
$$I_B = \frac{I_L}{h_{FE}} = \frac{80\text{ mA}}{50} = 1.6\text{ mA}$$

**Step 3: Worst-Case $R_S$**
At $V_{in(min)} = 12\text{ V}$, current through $R_S$ must be at least $I_{Z(min)} + I_B = 5 + 1.6 = 6.6\text{ mA}$.
$$R_S = \frac{V_{in(min)} - V_Z}{I_{Z(min)} + I_B} = \frac{12 - 9.1}{6.6\text{ mA}} = \frac{2.9}{0.0066} \approx 439.4\,\Omega$$

Choose standard value: **$R_S = 470\,\Omega$** *(2 marks)*

**Step 4: Transistor Power Dissipation (Worst Case)**
$$P_D = (V_{in(max)} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L = (18 - 8.4) \times 0.080 = 9.6 \times 0.080 = 0.768\text{ W}$$

A transistor with **$P_D \geq 1\text{ W}$** (e.g., BD139 with heatsink) must be used. *(2 marks)*

**Step 5: Zener Power Check**
$$I_{Z(max)} = \frac{V_{in(max)} - V_Z}{R_S} = \frac{18 - 9.1}{470} \approx 18.94\text{ mA}$$
$$P_Z = 9.1 \times 0.01894 \approx 0.172\text{ W} \ll 1\text{ W} \;\text{(safe)}$$ *(1 mark)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** Compare **series and shunt voltage regulators** based on efficiency, current capability, and complexity. When is a series regulator preferred over a shunt regulator? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Series Regulator | Shunt Regulator |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------|
| **Efficiency** | **High** (pass element drops only $V_{in} - V_{out}$) | **Low** (Zener/element carries full excess current to ground) |
| **Current Capability** | **High** (BJT can supply large $I_L$) | **Limited** (Zener current rating limits $I_L$) |
| **Complexity** | Moderate (requires pass transistor + bias) | Simple (Zener + series resistor only) |
| **Short-Circuit Protection** | Poor (requires external current limit) | Inherent (Zener is in parallel, not series) |
| **Load Regulation** | Good | Moderate |

**Preference for Series Regulator:** A series regulator is preferred when the load current is **high (> 50 mA)**, when **efficiency is critical** (e.g., battery-powered devices), and when the **input-output voltage differential is moderate** (e.g., 3–6 V). It is the standard topology for all IC linear regulators (LM78xx, LM317, LDO). *(7 marks: 3 for table, 2 for explanation, 2 for application conditions)*

---

**(b)** In a series regulator with a 10 V Zener, $V_{BE} = 0.7\text{ V}$, $R_S = 330\,\Omega$, and $V_{in} = 15\text{ V}$, calculate the **no-load output voltage** and the **output voltage when a 100 Ω load is connected**. Assume $h_{FE} = 100$ and $I_{Z(min)} = 5\text{ mA}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: No-Load Output Voltage**
$$V_{out(NL)} = V_Z - V_{BE} = 10 - 0.7 = 9.3\text{ V}$$ *(2 marks)*

**Step 2: Zener Current Under No-Load**
$$I_{R_S} = \frac{V_{in} - V_Z}{R_S} = \frac{15 - 10}{330} = \frac{5}{330} \approx 15.15\text{ mA}$$
$$I_B(NL) = \frac{I_L(NL)}{h_{FE}} = 0 \quad (\text{no load})$$
$$I_Z(NL) = 15.15 - 0 \approx 15.15\text{ mA} \;>\; 5\text{ mA} \;\text{(Zener healthy ✓)}$$ *(1 mark)*

**Step 3: Load Current with $R_L = 100\,\Omega$**
$$I_L = \frac{V_{out}}{R_L} \approx \frac{9.3}{100} = 93\text{ mA}$$ *(1 mark)*

**Step 4: Base Current Required**
$$I_B = \frac{I_L}{h_{FE}} = \frac{93}{100} = 0.93\text{ mA}$$ *(1 mark)*

**Step 5: Check Zener Current Under Load**
$$I_Z(FL) = I_{R_S} - I_B = 15.15 - 0.93 = 14.22\text{ mA} \;>\; 5\text{ mA} \;\text{(Zener still in breakdown ✓)}$$

Hence $V_{out(FL)} \approx 9.3\text{ V}$. The load regulation is theoretically **nearly zero** in this idealized emitter-follower case. *(2 marks)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $V_{BE}$ drop:** Many students write $V_{out} = V_Z$ directly. Always remember $V_{out} = V_Z - V_{BE}$ for an NPN emitter follower. (**−1 mark**)
> 2. **Ignoring base current:** $R_S$ must supply both the Zener current *and* the base current. Using only $I_{Z(min)}$ in the $R_S$ formula is a common error. (**−1 mark**)
> 3. **Wrong worst-case selection:** Some students use $V_{in(max)}$ to find $R_S$. The correct worst case is $V_{in(min)}$ because that's when the Zener current is lowest. (**−1.5 marks**)
> 4. **Units mismatch:** Writing $R_S = 439$ (without $\Omega$) or $I_Z = 6.6$ (without mA) is a deduction. (**−0.5 marks**)
> 5. **Neglecting transistor heatsink:** Forgetting to mention the need for a heatsink when $P_D > 0.5\text{ W}$ loses practical-design marks. (**−1 mark**)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **Zener diode** must always be operated in **reverse breakdown** to act as a voltage reference.
- ✅ Output voltage formula: **$V_{out} = V_Z - V_{BE}$** (NPN emitter follower).
- ✅ Series resistor $R_S$ is sized using the **worst case**: $V_{in(min)}$ and the sum of $I_{Z(min)} + I_B$.
- ✅ The **series pass transistor** absorbs the voltage difference between $V_{in}$ and $V_{out}$, providing efficient regulation.
- ✅ **Line Regulation** is measured by varying $V_{in}$ (with load fixed); **Load Regulation** is measured by varying $R_L$ (with $V_{in}$ fixed).
- ✅ Transistor power dissipation: **$P_D = (V_{in(max)} - V_{out}) \cdot I_L$** — this is the key parameter for heatsink selection.
- ✅ Zener power dissipation: **$P_Z = V_Z \cdot I_{Z(max)}$** — must be less than the rated $P_Z$ of the diode.
- ✅ Efficiency of a linear regulator is always **less than $V_{out}/V_{in}$** — it is fundamentally limited by the linear (ohmic) drop.
- ✅ Always include an **input filter capacitor** ($C_{in}$) and an **output filter capacitor** ($C_{out}$) to improve ripple rejection and transient response.
- ✅ For higher output current, **Darlington pairs** (e.g., TIP122) replace the single BJT to multiply the effective $h_{FE}$.
- ✅ For variable output, replace the fixed Zener with an **adjustable reference** (e.g., TL431) — this is the basis of the LM317.
- ✅ Ripple rejection of a simple Zener + BJT regulator is typically **40–60 dB**, inferior to IC regulators (which use feedback to achieve > 70 dB).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RC Coupled Amplifier Using BJT in CE Configuration - Measurement of Gain, BW and Plotting of Frequency Response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL307 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** RC Coupled Amplifier Using BJT in CE Configuration - Measurement of Gain, BW and Plotting of Frequency Response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **RC Coupled Amplifier (CE Configuration):** A multistage amplifier in which two or more Common-Emitter (CE) amplifier stages are connected in cascade using a **Resistor-Capacitor (RC) network** as the inter-stage coupling element. The RC network allows AC signals to pass from one stage to the next while blocking the DC bias of one stage from disturbing the bias of the succeeding stage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This is the **most common and most important** small-signal analog experiment in the Analog and Digital Electronics Lab. Students are expected to: (1) build the circuit on a breadboard/PCB, (2) measure voltage gain $A_v$ at mid-band, (3) identify the **lower cutoff frequency $f_L$** and the **upper cutoff frequency $f_H$**, and (4) plot the frequency response on a **semi-log graph paper** (frequency on log scale, gain in dB on linear scale).

**Key Terminology — At a Glance:**

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **CE Configuration** | Common Emitter — input at base, output at collector, emitter is common (often through a bypass capacitor) |
| **RC Coupling** | Coupling between stages using a series **Capacitor** $C_c$ and a load **Resistor** $R_C$ at each stage |
| **Voltage Gain $A_v$** | Ratio of output AC voltage to input AC voltage, $A_v = V_{out}/V_{in}$ |
| **Bandwidth $BW$** | The range of frequencies over which the amplifier gain is within **70.7%** of mid-band gain (i.e., within $-3$ dB) |
| **Frequency Response** | A plot of gain magnitude (in dB) versus input signal frequency (in Hz, on log scale) |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!TIP]
> **The "Bucket-Brigade" Analogy:** Imagine a row of workers passing buckets of water down a line. Each worker is a *stage* of the amplifier. The water (your AC signal) must be handed from one worker to the next without spilling. The **RC coupling capacitor** acts like a small "valve" between workers — it only allows the *moving water* (AC) to pass and stops the *static water pressure* (DC bias) from one worker from interfering with the next. The **resistor** at the collector is like the "shoulder" of the worker — the bigger the resistor, the more "oomph" (voltage swing) the worker can pass along. When the signal is very low frequency (slow waves), the valve barely lets it through (capacitor acts open). When the signal is very high frequency, the worker's own reflexes slow down (transistor internal capacitances), and the gain drops. Only in the **middle range** does the worker transfer water efficiently — this is the **mid-band region**.

### 1.3 Why Common-Emitter and Why RC?

- **CE provides high voltage gain** and **$180^\circ$ phase inversion** between input and output.
- **RC coupling is cheap, lightweight, and compact** compared to transformer coupling, and gives excellent **flat mid-band response** — making it the de-facto choice for audio amplifiers.
- The **DC blocking** by $C_c$ allows each stage to have its **own independent bias point (Q-point)**, designed using the **voltage divider bias** rule of $V_{CE} \approx V_{CC}/2$ for maximum undistorted swing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Misconception:** "RC Coupled = the entire circuit has only RC components." Wrong! The BJT is the *active* device. The **R** in "RC Coupled" refers to the **collector resistor $R_C$** and the **C** refers to the **coupling capacitor $C_c$** that interlinks stages. There is also a **base resistor $R_B$**, an **emitter resistor $R_E$** (often bypassed by $C_E$), and biasing resistors $R_1, R_2$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Circuit Topology (Single CE Stage + Coupling Network)

A single RC-coupled CE stage consists of:

1. **Voltage Divider Bias Network** — $R_1$ (upper) and $R_2$ (lower) set the base bias $V_B$.
2. **Base Resistor** $R_B$ — sometimes used instead of $R_1, R_2$ for simplicity in lab circuits.
3. **Collector Resistor** $R_C$ — converts collector current variation to voltage variation.
4. **Emitter Resistor** $R_E$ — provides DC negative feedback for thermal stability.
5. **Emitter Bypass Capacitor** $C_E$ — short-circuits $R_E$ for AC signals so that AC gain is not reduced.
6. **Input Coupling Capacitor** $C_{in}$ — blocks DC from the source and passes AC to the base.
7. **Output Coupling Capacitor** $C_{out}$ — blocks DC from the next stage load and passes AC.
8. **AC Equivalent Load** $R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L$ where $R_L$ is the next-stage input or external load.

For a **two-stage** RC-coupled amplifier, the output of stage-1 is coupled to the input of stage-2 through a second coupling capacitor $C_{c2}$.

### 2.2 The Three Frequency Regions

The frequency response of any RC-coupled amplifier is divided into three regions:

| Region | Frequency Range | Controlling Elements | Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low-Frequency Region** | $f < f_L$ | Coupling caps $C_{in}, C_{out}$ and bypass cap $C_E$ | Gain **rolls off at +20 dB/decade** (falls) as frequency decreases |
| **Mid-Frequency Region** | $f_L \le f \le f_H$ | All capacitors act as **short circuits**; internal transistor capacitances act as **open circuits** | Gain is **maximum and constant** (flat), phase shift $\approx 180^\circ$ |
| **High-Frequency Region** | $f > f_H$ | Internal transistor capacitances $C_{bc}$ (Miller effect) and $C_{be}$ | Gain **rolls off at -20 dB/decade** as frequency increases |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this happens physically:**
> - At **low $f$**, the impedance of capacitors $X_C = 1/(2\pi f C)$ becomes **very large**, so they no longer act as short circuits. They form a **voltage divider** with the input resistance of the stage, reducing the voltage reaching the base.
> - At **high $f$**, the transistor's internal junction capacitances (especially the **base-collector capacitance $C_{bc}$**, which is amplified by the Miller effect) act as **low-impedance shunts** to ground, draining away signal current.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Mandatory for Exam)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are EXTREMELY HIGH-YIELD for KTU exams. Memorize the boundary conditions and units carefully.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Meaning / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mid-band Voltage Gain (CE) | $$A_{v(\text{mid})} = -g_m \cdot R_{ac} = -\frac{\beta \cdot R_{ac}}{r_e}$$ | $R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L$ for one stage. Sign "$-$" indicates $180^\circ$ phase inversion. |
| 2 | Gain in decibels | $$A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10} \vert A_v \vert$$ | Always report gain in dB on the graph paper. |
| 3 | Bandwidth | $$BW = f_H - f_L$$ | $f_H \gg f_L$, so often $BW \approx f_H$ for rough estimates. |
| 4 | $-3$ dB Cutoff Condition | $$A_v(\text{cutoff}) = \frac{A_{v(\text{mid})}}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.707 \cdot A_{v(\text{mid})}$$ | $f_L$ and $f_H$ are the frequencies at which gain falls to $70.7\%$ of mid-band value. |
| 5 | Lower Cutoff (input coupling) | $$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi (R_S + R_{in}) \cdot C_{in}}$$ | $R_S$ = source resistance, $R_{in}$ = input resistance of stage. |
| 6 | Lower Cutoff (emitter bypass) | $$f_{LE} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_E \cdot C_E}$$ | The bypass capacitor effect. |
| 7 | Miller Input Capacitance | $$C_{M} = C_{bc}(1 - A_v) = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$ | $C_{bc}$ is amplified by $\approx (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$. |
| 8 | Upper Cutoff | $$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} \cdot C_{eq}}$$ | $R_{eq}$ and $C_{eq}$ are the Thevenin equivalents seen at the base, **after** applying Miller's theorem. |
| 9 | Transconductance | $$g_m = \frac{I_C(\text{mA})}{25\,\text{mV}} = \frac{I_C}{V_T}$$ | At room temperature, $V_T \approx 25\text{ mV}$. |
| 10 | Thermal Voltage | $$V_T = \frac{kT}{q} \approx 25\text{ mV} @ 300\text{ K}$$ | Boltzmann constant $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. |

**Boundary Conditions to ALWAYS remember:**
- At $f = f_L$ and $f = f_H$: gain drops by exactly **$-3$ dB** (≈ $0.707$).
- In mid-band: all capacitive reactances $\to 0$ (short) and internal capacitive reactances $\to \infty$ (open).
- Units: $f$ in Hz, $R$ in $\Omega$, $C$ in Farads, $A_v$ is dimensionless.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio amplifiers** (radios, TVs, public-address systems) use RC-coupled multistage amplifiers because of their **wide bandwidth** (typically 20 Hz to 20 kHz for voice/music).
- **Instrumentation pre-amplifiers** use cascaded CE stages for high, stable mid-band gain.
- **Op-amp internal gain stages** are essentially multi-stage RC-coupled differential amplifiers.
- Understanding gain roll-off is **critical** in **filter design**, **feedback amplifier stability** (Bode plots), and **high-frequency communication circuits**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All derivations below are **exam-grade**, with every step written out. No skipping.

### 3.1 Derivation of Mid-Band Voltage Gain $A_v$

**Step 1 — Replace the transistor with its small-signal AC equivalent.**

In the mid-band region, the coupling and bypass capacitors are short circuits, and the internal capacitances are open. The BJT is replaced by its **simplified hybrid-$\pi$ model**: a base-emitter resistance $r_\pi = \beta/g_m$ and a **current-controlled current source** $\beta \cdot i_b$ from collector to emitter.

**Step 2 — Apply KVL to the input loop (base-emitter side).**

The input AC voltage $v_{in}$ appears across $r_\pi$ (since $C_E$ shorts $R_E$ in mid-band). Hence the small-signal base current is:

$$i_b = \frac{v_{in}}{r_\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL at the collector node.**

The collector current is $i_c = \beta \cdot i_b$. This current flows through the **AC equivalent load resistance** $R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L$, producing an output voltage:

$$v_{out} = -i_c \cdot R_{ac} = -\beta \cdot i_b \cdot R_{ac}$$

The minus sign is because increasing $i_c$ causes a larger voltage drop across $R_C$, lowering the collector voltage — this is the $180^\circ$ phase inversion.

**Step 4 — Form the ratio $v_{out}/v_{in}$.**

Substitute $i_b$ from Step 2 into Step 3:

$$v_{out} = -\beta \cdot \frac{v_{in}}{r_\pi} \cdot R_{ac}$$

Dividing both sides by $v_{in}$:

$$\boxed{A_v = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -\frac{\beta \cdot R_{ac}}{r_\pi}}$$

**Step 5 — Replace $r_\pi$ with $\beta/g_m$.**

Since $r_\pi = \beta/g_m$, we get the cleaner form:

$$\boxed{A_v = -g_m \cdot R_{ac}}$$

**Final Result:**

$$A_{v(\text{mid})} = -\frac{\beta \cdot R_{ac}}{r_\pi} = -g_m \cdot R_{ac}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the $-3$ dB Cutoff Condition

By definition, the cutoff frequencies are those at which the **power** delivered to a load drops to **half** of its mid-band value. Since power $P = V^2/R$, this corresponds to:

$$V_{out}^2(f_L) = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V_{out}^2(\text{mid})$$

Taking the square root of both sides:

$$V_{out}(f_L) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot V_{out}(\text{mid}) = 0.7071 \cdot V_{out}(\text{mid})$$

Converting to decibels:

$$A_v(\text{dB})|_{f=f_L} = 20 \log_{10}(0.7071) = -3.0103 \approx -3\,\text{dB}$$

Therefore the **cutoff frequencies are the frequencies at which the gain drops by $3$ dB** below the mid-band value.

### 3.3 Derivation of Lower Cutoff Frequency $f_L$ (Coupling Capacitor)

**Step 1 — Identify the low-frequency equivalent circuit.**

At low $f$, the coupling capacitor $C_{in}$ has a **finite, non-negligible** impedance $X_{Cin} = 1/(2\pi f C_{in})$. The bypass capacitor $C_E$ is similarly finite. Each of these forms an RC high-pass filter with the surrounding resistances.

**Step 2 — Input side: $C_{in}$ in series with the input resistance.**

The Thevenin resistance seen by $C_{in}$ is $R_{th1} = R_S + R_{in}$, where $R_{in} = R_1 \parallel R_2 \parallel r_\pi$ is the input resistance of the stage.

The transfer function of this RC high-pass section is:

$$\frac{V_b}{V_s} = \frac{j 2\pi f C_{in} (R_S + R_{in})}{1 + j 2\pi f C_{in} (R_S + R_{in})}$$

**Step 3 — Find the cutoff (when imaginary part = real part = 1).**

At the cutoff, the magnitude of the denominator equals $\sqrt{2}$ times the real part, giving:

$$1 = 2\pi f_L C_{in} (R_S + R_{in})$$

Solving for $f_L$:

$$\boxed{f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi C_{in} (R_S + R_{in})}}$$

**Step 4 — Output side and emitter side give similar formulas:**

$$f_{L2} = \frac{1}{2\pi C_{out} (R_C + R_L)}$$

$$f_{LE} = \frac{1}{2\pi C_E R_E}$$

**Step 5 — The overall $f_L$ is determined by the section with the LARGEST individual cutoff:**

$$f_L \approx \max(f_{L1}, f_{L2}, f_{LE})$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Upper Cutoff Frequency $f_H$ (Miller's Theorem)

**Step 1 — Recognize that at high frequency, the internal capacitance $C_{bc}$ dominates.**

The base-collector capacitance $C_{bc}$ (typically a few pF) connects the input and output nodes. Its effect on the input is **amplified** by the gain of the stage — this is **Miller's Effect**.

**Step 2 — Apply Miller's Theorem.**

Miller's theorem states that a capacitor $C_{bc}$ connected between input and output, with voltage gain $A_v$ from input to output, can be replaced by two capacitors to ground:

- **Input side:** $C_{M1} = C_{bc}(1 - A_v)$
- **Output side:** $C_{M2} = C_{bc}\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{A_v}\right)$

Since $A_v$ is **negative** for CE configuration, $(1 - A_v) = (1 + \vert A_v \vert)$. With $\vert A_v \vert$ typically 50–200, the input-side Miller capacitance is **huge**:

$$C_M = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert) \approx C_{bc} \cdot \vert A_v \vert$$

**Step 3 — Form the input RC low-pass filter.**

The total input capacitance is $C_{in,\text{total}} = C_{be} + C_M$. The total input resistance is $R_{in,\text{total}} = (R_S \parallel R_1 \parallel R_2) \parallel r_\pi$. This forms an **RC low-pass filter** at the input:

$$f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{in,\text{total}} \cdot C_{in,\text{total}}}$$

**Step 4 — Combine the Miller and base-emitter capacitances.**

$$\boxed{f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{eq} \cdot (C_{be} + C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert))}}$$

where $R_{eq}$ is the Thevenin resistance seen at the base node.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (For Lab Viva Preparation)

> [!TIP]
> **Sample Calculation Question:** A single-stage CE amplifier has $g_m = 40$ mA/V, $R_C = 4.7\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_L = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_{in} = 1\,\mu\text{F}$, $C_E = 100\,\mu\text{F}$, $R_E = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$, $R_S = 600\,\Omega$, $R_{in} = 1.5\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C_{bc} = 5\,\text{pF}$, $C_{be} = 50\,\text{pF}$. Find: (a) Mid-band gain in dB, (b) $f_L$, (c) $f_H$, (d) Bandwidth.

**Solution:**

**Step (a):** $R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L = (4.7 \times 10)/(4.7 + 10) = 3.2\,\text{k}\Omega$.
$A_v = -g_m \cdot R_{ac} = -0.040 \times 3200 = -128$.
$A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}(128) = 42.14\,\text{dB}$.

**Step (b):** $f_{L1} = 1/[2\pi \times 1\,\mu\text{F} \times (600 + 1500)] = 1/(2\pi \times 10^{-6} \times 2100) \approx 75.8\,\text{Hz}$.
$f_{LE} = 1/(2\pi \times 100\,\mu\text{F} \times 1000) = 1/(2\pi \times 0.1) \approx 1.59\,\text{Hz}$.
Overall $f_L \approx \max(75.8, 1.59) = 75.8\,\text{Hz}$ (input coupling dominates).

**Step (c):** $C_M = 5\,\text{pF} \times (1 + 128) = 645\,\text{pF}$.
$C_{in,\text{total}} = 50 + 645 = 695\,\text{pF}$.
Assuming $R_{eq} \approx 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ (rough estimate), $f_H = 1/(2\pi \times 1000 \times 695 \times 10^{-12}) \approx 229\,\text{kHz}$.

**Step (d):** $BW = f_H - f_L = 229{,}000 - 75.8 \approx 228.9\,\text{kHz} \approx f_H$.

**Observation:** Notice how the **Miller effect** multiplies the tiny $5\,\text{pF}$ base-collector capacitance into a **dominating $645\,\text{pF}$** at the input — this is precisely why CE amplifiers have a **limited upper cutoff** and why the bandwidth is much smaller than the theoretical $f_T$ of the transistor.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Since physical circuit schematics cannot be rendered in Mermaid syntax, the diagrams below provide the **block-level functional architecture** of a two-stage RC-coupled amplifier and the **characteristic frequency response curve** as a Mermaid `xychart-beta` plot.

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Two-Stage RC-Coupled Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputAC[AC Input Signal Vs] --> Cin1[Coupling Capacitor Cin]
    Cin1 --> stage1[Stage 1 CE BJT]
    stage1 --> Cc1[Inter-stage Coupling Capacitor Cc]
    Cc1 --> stage2[Stage 2 CE BJT]
    stage2 --> Cout[Output Coupling Capacitor Cout]
    Cout --> loadAC[AC Load RL]
    stage1 --> bias1[DC Bias Network R1 R2]
    stage2 --> bias2[DC Bias Network R3 R4]
    bias1 -.blocked by Cc.-> bias2
```

**Interpretation:**
- The AC signal flows left to right through capacitors $C_{in} \to C_c \to C_{out}$.
- The DC bias networks are **isolated** from each other by the coupling capacitors — this is the core purpose of RC coupling.
- Each stage amplifies the signal further; overall gain $A_{v,\text{total}} = A_{v1} \cdot A_{v2}$ (in linear scale) or sum in dB.

### 4.2 Frequency Response — Bode Plot (Gain vs. Frequency)

```mermaid
xychart-beta
    title "Frequency Response of RC-Coupled CE Amplifier"
    x-axis "Frequency f (log scale, Hz)" [10, 100, 1000, 10000, 100000, 1000000, 10000000]
    y-axis "Gain in dB" 0 --> 50
    line [10, 25, 39, 42, 42, 39, 25]
```

**How to read this plot for your lab record:**

| Feature on the Curve | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Flat top portion** (40–42 dB) | **Mid-band region** — gain is maximum and nearly constant. |
| **Left side slope (rising at +20 dB/decade)** | Low-frequency region — gain falls as $f$ decreases. |
| **Right side slope (falling at -20 dB/decade)** | High-frequency region — gain falls as $f$ increases. |
| **Where left curve meets flat top** | $f_L$ (lower cutoff), gain = mid-band $-$ 3 dB. |
| **Where right curve meets flat top** | $f_H$ (upper cutoff), gain = mid-band $-$ 3 dB. |
| **Horizontal distance between $f_L$ and $f_H$ on the log axis** | **Bandwidth $BW$** (in decades). |

### 4.3 Functional Block Architecture — Signal Path with Critical Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph dc[DC Bias Block per stage]
        R1[R1] --> R2[R2]
        R2 --> gnd[Ground]
    end
    subgraph ac[AC Signal Path per stage]
        sigin[Input AC] --> Cc[Coupling Cap Cc]
        Cc --> rpi[r_pi base-emitter]
        rpi --> ccnode[Collector node]
        ccnode --> RC[RC]
        RC --> vcc[Vcc]
        ccnode --> Cbc[Cbc to base]
        ccnode --> CL[CL load]
        CL --> nextstage[Next stage or output]
    end
    ccnode -.Miller amplified.-> Cbc
```

**Engineering takeaway:** The Miller-amplified feedback capacitance $C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ between the collector node and the base node is the **primary bandwidth limiter** in any CE stage. Reducing it requires either (a) using a transistor with smaller $C_{bc}$, (b) reducing stage gain, or (c) using a **cascode** configuration.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the minus sign in $A_v$.** CE is inverting; if you write $A_v = +\beta R_C/r_\pi$, you will lose a mark on phase shift.
> 2. **Mixing $f_L$ and $f_H$ formulas.** $f_L$ is dominated by **external coupling capacitors** (large $\mu$F range). $f_H$ is dominated by **internal transistor capacitances** (pF range). Confusing them yields a wildly wrong BW.
> 3. **Forgetting the Miller factor.** In $f_H$ calculations, students often write $f_H = 1/(2\pi R_{in} C_{bc})$ instead of including $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ — this gives a $f_H$ that is **100× too high**, which the examiner will catch.
> 4. **Plotting on linear graph paper.** KTU strictly requires **semi-log** paper: $f$ on log axis, $A_v$ in dB on linear axis. Linear plots lose the wide frequency range.
> 5. **Not stating Q-point.** In the experiment, you must mention the Q-point $(V_{CE}, I_C)$ measured in your circuit.

### Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]**
**What is an RC coupled amplifier? Why is CE configuration preferred for voltage amplification in such amplifiers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An **RC coupled amplifier** is a multistage amplifier in which successive CE stages are coupled using a **resistor-capacitor network** — the capacitor $C_c$ blocks the DC bias of one stage from the next while passing the AC signal. The **CE configuration is preferred** because: (1) it provides **high voltage gain** (typically 50–500 per stage), (2) it has **moderate current gain** ($\beta$), and (3) it produces a **$180^\circ$ phase inversion** which is useful in many feedback and oscillator circuits. The RC network is also **compact, cheap, and gives a wide flat mid-band response**, making it ideal for audio-frequency amplification.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define bandwidth of an RC coupled amplifier. What is the significance of the $-3$ dB point?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Bandwidth $BW$** of an RC coupled amplifier is the range of frequencies over which the gain remains within **70.7%** (i.e., $-3$ dB) of its maximum mid-band value. Mathematically, $BW = f_H - f_L$, where $f_H$ is the upper cutoff and $f_L$ is the lower cutoff. **Significance of the $-3$ dB point:** It marks the frequency at which the **output power drops to half** of its mid-band value (since $10 \log_{10}(0.5) = -3$ dB). These are called the **half-power frequencies** or **cutoff frequencies**, and they define the useful operating range of the amplifier.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> Per KTU 2024 scheme, every Part-B question carries **14 marks** split as **7 + 7** in two sub-parts. You are given an internal choice between two questions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2 Pattern]**
**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **single-stage RC coupled CE amplifier**. Derive an expression for its **mid-band voltage gain**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive expressions for the **lower cutoff frequency $f_L$** and the **upper cutoff frequency $f_H$** of the amplifier. Explain the **Miller effect** and its impact on bandwidth. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Working and Mid-Band Gain (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A single-stage RC coupled CE amplifier consists of:
- A **voltage divider bias** ($R_1, R_2$) setting the base bias.
- **$R_E$ and $C_E$** providing DC stability and AC bypassing respectively.
- **$R_C$** as the collector load converting current variation to voltage.
- **$C_{in}$** and **$C_{out}$** as input and output coupling capacitors.
- The transistor **Q** in **CE configuration** (input at base, output at collector, emitter common).

**Step 2 — DC Operating Point (1 mark):**
Using KVL on the base-emitter loop and the voltage divider rule:
$$V_B = V_{CC} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}, \quad V_E = V_B - V_{BE}, \quad I_E = \frac{V_E}{R_E}, \quad I_C \approx I_E$$
$$V_{CE} = V_{CC} - I_C(R_C + R_E)$$
For maximum swing, design the Q-point such that $V_{CE} \approx V_{CC}/2$.

**Step 3 — AC Equivalent Circuit and Gain Derivation (4 marks):**
Replace the BJT with its simplified hybrid-$\pi$ model ($r_\pi$ and dependent source $g_m v_{be}$). In mid-band, all capacitors are short circuits and the internal $C_{bc}, C_{be}$ are open.

Applying KVL at the input: $v_{in} = i_b \cdot r_\pi$, so $i_b = v_{in}/r_\pi$.
The collector current: $i_c = g_m v_{be} = g_m v_{in}$.
The output voltage across $R_{ac} = R_C \parallel R_L$:
$$v_{out} = -i_c R_{ac} = -g_m v_{in} R_{ac}$$
Therefore:
$$\boxed{A_{v(\text{mid})} = \frac{v_{out}}{v_{in}} = -g_m R_{ac} = -\frac{\beta R_{ac}}{r_\pi}}$$

The negative sign indicates a **$180^\circ$ phase shift** between input and output, which is characteristic of the CE configuration.

---

**(b) Lower and Upper Cutoff Frequencies & Miller Effect (7 marks):**

**Lower Cutoff Frequency $f_L$ (3 marks):**
At low frequencies, the impedances of $C_{in}, C_{out},$ and $C_E$ are no longer negligible. Each forms a **high-pass RC filter** with the surrounding resistances. For the input coupling capacitor, the Thevenin resistance seen by $C_{in}$ is $R_S + R_{in}$:
$$f_{L1} = \frac{1}{2\pi C_{in}(R_S + R_{in})}$$
For the emitter bypass capacitor:
$$f_{LE} = \frac{1}{2\pi C_E R_E}$$
The **overall $f_L$ is the maximum** of these individual cutoffs, since that section attenuates the signal the most.

**Upper Cutoff Frequency $f_H$ (3 marks):**
At high frequencies, the internal **base-collector capacitance $C_{bc}$** becomes the dominant factor. Using **Miller's theorem**, $C_{bc}$ between input and output nodes is replaced by an equivalent input capacitance:
$$C_{M} = C_{bc}(1 - A_v) = C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$$
This **Miller-multiplied capacitance** forms a low-pass filter at the input with $R_{in,\text{eq}}$, giving:
$$\boxed{f_H = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{in,\text{eq}} (C_{be} + C_{bc}(1 + \vert A_v \vert))}}$$

**Impact of Miller Effect on Bandwidth (1 mark):**
Since $A_v$ is typically 50–200, the factor $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ magnifies a tiny $C_{bc}$ of a few pF into a few hundred pF at the input. This **drastically lowers $f_H$**, severely limiting the high-frequency response. Hence the Miller effect is the **primary bandwidth-limiting factor** in CE amplifiers.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2 Pattern]**
**(a)** Describe the **experimental procedure** to measure the **voltage gain** and **bandwidth** of an RC coupled CE amplifier in the laboratory. List the **instruments and components** required. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain how the **frequency response curve** is plotted. Discuss the shape of the curve in the **low, mid, and high frequency regions**, with reference to the controlling reactive elements. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Experimental Procedure & Apparatus (7 marks):**

**Apparatus Required (2 marks):**
- BJT (BC107 / 2N2222 / SL100 — depending on lab kit)
- Resistors: $R_1, R_2$ (for voltage divider), $R_C, R_E$ (as per design)
- Capacitors: $C_{in}, C_{out}, C_E$ (electrolytic, $\mu$F range)
- DC power supply: **0–30 V dual regulated supply**
- **Function generator** (1 Hz – 1 MHz, sine wave)
- **Dual-trace CRO** (20 MHz or higher) or **AC voltmeter / DMM**
- Breadboard and connecting wires
- **Component values** (example): $V_{CC} = +12$ V, $R_1 = 33$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 10$ k$\Omega$, $R_C = 4.7$ k$\Omega$, $R_E = 1$ k$\Omega$, $C_{in} = C_{out} = 10\,\mu$F, $C_E = 100\,\mu$F.

**Procedure (5 marks):**
1. **Build the circuit** on the breadboard as per the schematic. Identify base, emitter, collector pins of the BJT.
2. **Switch on the DC supply** ($V_{CC}$) and verify the Q-point by measuring $V_{CE}$ and $I_C$ using the DMM. Adjust resistors if necessary to get $V_{CE} \approx V_{CC}/2$.
3. **Connect the function generator** to the input (with $C_{in}$) and set it to a **mid-band frequency** (e.g., 1 kHz) with a small amplitude (say, 50 mV peak).
4. **Connect the CRO** to the output (after $C_{out}$) and observe the amplified sine wave. Measure the output peak-to-peak voltage.
5. **Calculate mid-band gain:** $A_v = V_{out}/V_{in}$ and $A_v(\text{dB}) = 20 \log_{10}\vert A_v \vert$.
6. **Vary the frequency** of the function generator in steps (e.g., 10 Hz, 50 Hz, 100 Hz, 500 Hz, 1 kHz, 10 kHz, 100 kHz, 500 kHz, 1 MHz) keeping $V_{in}$ constant. At each frequency, note $V_{out}$ and compute the gain in dB.
7. **Identify $f_L$ and $f_H$** as the frequencies at which the gain drops by 3 dB below the mid-band value.
8. **Compute $BW = f_H - f_L$**.
9. **Plot the frequency response** on semi-log graph paper.

---

**(b) Frequency Response Curve — Shape and Controlling Elements (7 marks):**

**Plotting Procedure (2 marks):**
- Use **semi-log graph paper**: $x$-axis is frequency in Hz on **log scale** (decades), $y$-axis is gain in **dB on linear scale**.
- Mark the mid-band flat region. Mark $f_L$ and $f_H$ at $-3$ dB points.
- Draw smooth asymptotic lines: a **+20 dB/decade slope** to the left of $f_L$ and a **$-20$ dB/decade slope** to the right of $f_H$.
- The actual response curve **rounds off** smoothly at $f_L$ and $f_H$ (it does not have sharp corners — the asymptotes are approximations).

**Low-Frequency Region (2 marks):**
For $f < f_L$, the gain **rises at +20 dB/decade** as frequency increases. This region is controlled by the **external coupling and bypass capacitors** $C_{in}, C_{out}, C_E$. As $f \to 0$, their reactances $X_C = 1/(2\pi f C) \to \infty$, and they form **voltage dividers** with the surrounding resistances, attenuating the signal. At very low $f$ (e.g., 10 Hz), the gain may drop by 20–40 dB compared to mid-band.

**Mid-Frequency Region (1 mark):**
For $f_L \le f \le f_H$, the gain is **maximum and constant (flat)**. All external capacitors act as **ideal short circuits**, and internal transistor capacitances act as **ideal open circuits**. The amplifier behaves like an ideal **resistive** network with a flat response.

**High-Frequency Region (2 marks):**
For $f > f_H$, the gain **falls at -20 dB/decade** as frequency increases. This region is controlled by the **internal transistor capacitances** $C_{be}$ and **$C_{bc}$ (the base-collector junction capacitance)**. Miller's effect multiplies $C_{bc}$ by $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$, making it the dominant factor. As $f \to \infty$, these capacitances act as **short circuits to ground**, shunting the signal away and reducing the gain sharply.

> [!TIP]
> **The +20 dB/decade / -20 dB/decade slopes come directly from the fact that each RC section contributes a single-pole roll-off**, giving an asymptotic slope of $\pm 6$ dB per octave ($\equiv 20$ dB per decade).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **RC Coupled Amplifier**: Multistage CE amplifier with **R-C inter-stage coupling** (capacitor $C_c$ blocks DC, passes AC).
- [x] **CE Configuration** is preferred for its **high voltage gain** and **$180^\circ$ phase inversion**.
- [x] **Mid-band gain** $A_v = -g_m R_{ac} = -\beta R_{ac}/r_\pi$. In dB: $20 \log_{10}\vert A_v \vert$.
- [x] **Bandwidth $BW = f_H - f_L$**. The $-3$ dB points define $f_L$ and $f_H$.
- [x] **$f_L$** is set by **external coupling caps** ($C_{in}, C_{out}, C_E$) — large $\mu$F values.
- [x] **$f_H$** is set by **internal transistor caps** ($C_{be}, C_{bc}$) — small pF values.
- [x] **Miller's theorem** magnifies $C_{bc}$ by $(1 + \vert A_v \vert)$ at the input — this is the **main bandwidth limiter**.
- [x] **Roll-off slopes**: **+20 dB/decade** (low $f$), **flat** (mid), **$-20$ dB/decade** (high $f$).
- [x] **Always plot on semi-log paper**: $f$ log, $A_v$(dB) linear.
- [x] **Q-point must be near $V_{CE} \approx V_{CC}/2$** for maximum symmetric swing.
- [x] **Phase shift** in mid-band is **$180^\circ$** (inverting amplifier).
- [x] **Two-stage gain** = product of individual stage gains (linear) or sum (dB).
- [x] **Common lab BJT**: BC107, 2N2222, SL100, BC547.
- [x] **Common lab values**: $V_{CC} = 12$ V, $R_C = 4.7$ k$\Omega$, $R_E = 1$ k$\Omega$, $C_E = 100\,\mu$F.
- [x] **For lab viva**: Know what $r_\pi$, $g_m$, $\beta$, $V_T$, and $h_{fe}$ mean and how they relate.
- [x] **KCL/KVL analysis at the Q-point** is essential to compute $I_B, I_C, V_{CE}$ before powering up.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Op-amp Circuits - Design and Set Up of Inverting and Non-inverting Amplifier, Adder Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL307 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Op-amp Circuits - Design and Set Up of Inverting and Non-inverting Amplifier, Adder Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a high-gain, direct-coupled, differential input electronic voltage amplifier that uses external feedback components to control its transfer function. The IC 741 is the most widely used general-purpose op-amp featuring **two inputs** (inverting, denoted by `–` and non-inverting, denoted by `+`) and **a single output**. In this lab experiment, we design, build, and verify three fundamental linear applications of the op-amp:

- **Inverting Amplifier**
- **Non-Inverting Amplifier**
- **Adder Circuit (Summing Amplifier)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCEOL307):** The student must be able to *design, set up, and practically demonstrate* the inverting amplifier, non-inverting amplifier, and adder circuits using the **IC 741 op-amp** on a breadboard, with measurements taken using a CRO/DSO and function generator.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **see-saw (lever) on a playground**. The two children sitting on either end represent the two inputs of the op-amp. The **pivot point** is the internal amplifier, and the **adult pushing from behind** to keep the see-saw balanced represents the **feedback network**. Depending on where the adult stands, the see-saw tilts in a predictable way — either amplifying or reducing motion from one side.

In real terms:
- The op-amp continuously **senses the difference** between its two inputs.
- Through an external **feedback resistor (Rf)**, it forces its output to a level that makes this difference almost **zero** (the **virtual short** concept).
- By choosing the ratio of the input and feedback resistors, you decide **how much amplification** happens.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in Bold:**
> - **Open-loop gain (A):** typically **2 × 10⁵ (200,000)** for IC 741.
> - **Input impedance:** approximately **2 MΩ**.
> - **Output impedance:** approximately **75 Ω**.
> - **Supply voltage (±Vcc):** typically **±12 V** or **±15 V** in lab.
> - **Slew rate:** **0.5 V/μs** for IC 741.
> - **Bandwidth:** approximately **1 MHz** (unity-gain).

### 1.3 IC 741 Pin Configuration (Must Memorize)

The IC 741 is an **8-pin DIP (Dual In-line Package)**. Below is the standard pinout that every KTU student must know by heart:

- **Pin 1:** Offset Null
- **Pin 2:** Inverting Input (V⁻)
- **Pin 3:** Non-Inverting Input (V⁺)
- **Pin 4:** V⁻ (Negative supply, typically −12 V or −15 V)
- **Pin 5:** Offset Null
- **Pin 6:** Output (V_out)
- **Pin 7:** V⁺ (Positive supply, typically +12 V or +15 V)
- **Pin 8:** NC (No Connection)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Lab Mistake:** Reversing the polarity of Pin 7 and Pin 4 is the most common cause of op-amp circuits "not working" on a breadboard. Always connect Pin 7 to **+Vcc** and Pin 4 to **−Vcc (GND is between them)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Ideal Op-Amp Assumptions (Golden Rules)

For all practical analysis in the KTU lab and exam, the **ideal op-amp model** is used:

- **Infinite open-loop gain (A → ∞)**
- **Infinite input impedance (Z_in → ∞)** → no current flows into the input terminals.
- **Zero output impedance (Z_out = 0)** → output voltage is independent of load.
- **Infinite bandwidth**
- **Zero offset voltage**

From these, two **Golden Rules of Ideal Op-Amp Analysis** are derived:

1. **Rule 1 (Virtual Short):** V⁺ = V⁻ (the differential input voltage is zero)
2. **Rule 2 (No Current into Inputs):** I⁺ = I⁻ = 0

> [!NOTE]
> These two rules are the **foundation** for deriving every op-amp circuit formula. In the KTU exam, you will be expected to apply these in your derivations.

### 2.2 Circuit-by-Circuit Theory Breakdown

#### **A) Inverting Amplifier**

- The input signal **Vin** is applied through resistor **R1** to the **inverting (−) input**.
- The non-inverting (+) input is connected to **ground**.
- A feedback resistor **Rf** is connected between the output and the inverting input.

**Operation Logic:**
1. Due to the virtual short (V⁻ = V⁺ = 0 V), the inverting input is at a "virtual ground."
2. Since no current flows into the op-amp, all current through R1 must flow through Rf.
3. This creates the relationship: **I_in = I_f**, leading to a precise gain equation.

#### **B) Non-Inverting Amplifier**

- The input signal **Vin** is applied **directly to the non-inverting (+) input**.
- A resistor R1 connects the inverting input to ground.
- A feedback resistor Rf connects the output to the inverting input.

**Operation Logic:**
1. By the virtual short: V⁻ = V⁺ = V_in.
2. The voltage divider formed by R1 and Rf at the inverting input gives V⁻ = V_out × [R1 / (R1 + Rf)].
3. Equating and solving yields the non-inverting gain.

#### **C) Inverting Adder (Summing Amplifier)**

- An extension of the inverting amplifier where **multiple input signals** (V1, V2, V3, ...) are applied through individual input resistors (R1, R2, R3, ...) to the inverting input.
- A single feedback resistor Rf is used.
- The non-inverting input is grounded.

**Operation Logic:**
1. The inverting node is a virtual ground.
2. By KCL at the inverting node, the sum of all input currents equals the feedback current.
3. The output is the **weighted negative sum** of all inputs.

#### **D) Non-Inverting Adder**

- A non-inverting configuration where multiple input signals are applied through resistors to the **non-inverting input**, and a feedback network sets the gain.
- The output is the **sum (positive) of the inputs**, scaled by the non-inverting gain factor.
- **Note:** Designing a non-inverting adder requires satisfying a specific resistor matching condition (covered in Section 3).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Circuit Type | Output Voltage Formula | Closed-Loop Gain (A_CL) | Key Design Rule |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Inverting Amplifier** | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} \cdot V_{in}$ | $A_{CL} = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | Input impedance = $R_1$ |
| **Non-Inverting Amplifier** | $V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) \cdot V_{in}$ | $A_{CL} = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}$ | Input impedance = **very high** |
| **Inverting Adder (n inputs)** | $V_{out} = -R_f \left(\dfrac{V_1}{R_1} + \dfrac{V_2}{R_2} + \cdots + \dfrac{V_n}{R_n}\right)$ | $A_i = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$ for i-th input | If $R_1 = R_2 = R = R_f$, then $V_{out} = -(V_1 + V_2 + \cdots + V_n)$ |
| **Non-Inverting Adder (3 inputs)** | $V_{out} = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}\right) \cdot (V_a \cdot k_a + V_b \cdot k_b + V_c \cdot k_c)$ | Sum of weighted inputs | $\dfrac{1}{R_a} + \dfrac{1}{R_b} + \dfrac{1}{R_c} = \dfrac{1}{R_1}$ (matching condition) |
| **Unity Gain Buffer** | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ | $A_{CL} = 1$ | $R_f = 0$, $R_1 = \infty$ |
| **Virtual Ground Voltage** | $V^- = V^+ = 0$ V (inverting config) | — | Only for inverting configurations |
| **Virtual Short** | $V^- = V^+$ | — | Universal op-amp property |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Op-amp amplifiers and adders are the **building blocks** of every analog signal processing chain. They are used in:
> - **Audio mixing consoles** (summing multiple microphone inputs)
> - **Instrumentation amplifiers** (medical ECG/EEG machines)
> - **Active filters** (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass)
> - **DAC output stages** (summing binary-weighted currents)
> - **Servo motor controllers** and **robotics signal conditioning**
