## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**State the two ideal characteristics of an op-amp used in the analysis of inverting and non-inverting amplifier circuits.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The two ideal characteristics are:
1. **Infinite open-loop voltage gain (A → ∞):** This means the difference between the two input voltages (V⁺ − V⁻) approaches zero, leading to the **virtual short concept (V⁺ = V⁻)**.
2. **Infinite input impedance (Z_in → ∞):** This means **no current flows into either of the input terminals** of the op-amp, leading to the **current rule (I⁺ = I⁻ = 0)**.

These are the two **Golden Rules** used to analyze all op-amp circuits.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for each rule, 1 mark for the consequence/application.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**List any three linear applications of the IC 741 op-amp relevant to this lab experiment.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three linear applications covered in this lab are:
1. **Inverting Amplifier** — Produces an output that is 180° out of phase with the input, with gain $A_{CL} = -R_f / R_1$.
2. **Non-Inverting Amplifier** — Produces an output that is in phase with the input, with gain $A_{CL} = 1 + R_f / R_1$.
3. **Adder (Summing Amplifier)** — Produces a weighted sum of multiple input voltages, given by $V_{out} = -R_f (V_1/R_1 + V_2/R_2 + \cdots)$.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for each application with its corresponding gain/formula.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**
**Question A:**
**(a)** Design an **inverting amplifier** using IC 741 op-amp to achieve a closed-loop gain of **−20**. Assume the input signal is a sine wave of 100 mV peak at 1 kHz. Calculate the expected output voltage peak. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **3-input inverting adder circuit** and derive the expression for the output voltage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Design Choice**

Choose input resistor R1 = **1 kΩ** (a standard practical value).

**Step 2: Calculate Rf**

$$R_f = -A_{CL} \cdot R_1 = -(-20) \cdot 1 \text{ k}\Omega = 20 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 3: Calculate output voltage peak**

$$V_{out, peak} = |A_{CL}| \cdot V_{in, peak} = 20 \cdot 100 \text{ mV} = 2000 \text{ mV} = 2 \text{ V peak}$$

**Step 4: Verify against supply rails**

Since |V_out| = 2 V < 15 V supply, the op-amp will not saturate. The sine wave will appear undistorted at the output, inverted by 180°.

**Step 5: Practical implementation**

- R1 = 1 kΩ from Vin to Pin 2.
- Rf = 20 kΩ from Pin 6 to Pin 2.
- Pin 3 grounded.
- ±15 V supplies on Pins 7 and 4.

**Expected CRO observation:** A sine wave of 2 V peak, 180° out of phase with the input.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A(b) (7 marks):**

**Circuit Diagram (Mermaid representation):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    nV1["V1"] --> nR1["R1"]
    nV2["V2"] --> nR2["R2"]
    nV3["V3"] --> nR3["R3"]
    nR1 --> nNode["Pin 2 - Virtual Ground"]
    nR2 --> nNode
    nR3 --> nNode
    nNode --> nOp["IC 741"]
    nGND["GND"] --> nP3["Pin 3"]
    nP3 --> nOp
    nOp --> nOut["Vout - to CRO"]
    nOut --> nRf["Rf"]
    nRf --> nNode
```

**Working Explanation:**

In the inverting adder, three input signals V1, V2, V3 are applied through input resistors R1, R2, R3 respectively to the inverting input (Pin 2). The non-inverting input (Pin 3) is grounded. A feedback resistor Rf is connected between the output and the inverting input.

**Derivation (3 marks of part b):**

Applying the virtual ground concept: V⁻ = V⁺ = 0 V.

Applying **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the inverting node:

$$\frac{V_1 - 0}{R_1} + \frac{V_2 - 0}{R_2} + \frac{V_3 - 0}{R_3} + \frac{V_{out} - 0}{R_f} = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{V_{out} = -R_f \left(\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3}\right)}$$

**Special Case:** If R1 = R2 = R3 = Rf = R, then V_out = −(V1 + V2 + V3), which is a pure summing amplifier with unity weights.

**Practical Use:** Audio mixers, DAC circuits, and signal combination in instrumentation systems.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper, Module 2]**
**Question B:**
**(a)** Design and explain the working of a **non-inverting amplifier** with a gain of **11**. Mention the phase relationship between input and output. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **internal block diagram of IC 741** and list its **pin functions**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Design Equation**

For a non-inverting amplifier:

$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 11$$

**Step 2: Solve for the ratio**

$$\frac{R_f}{R_1} = 10$$

**Step 3: Choose standard values**

Let R1 = **1 kΩ**, then Rf = **10 kΩ** (both standard E12 series values).

**Step 4: Calculate output**

For Vin = 50 mV peak sine wave:

$$V_{out} = A_{CL} \cdot V_{in} = 11 \cdot 50 \text{ mV} = 550 \text{ mV peak}$$

**Step 5: Phase relationship**

The output is **in phase** with the input (0° phase shift), unlike the inverting amplifier which has a 180° phase shift.

**Step 6: Verify the high input impedance advantage**

Unlike the inverting amplifier (where input impedance = R1), the non-inverting amplifier offers **very high input impedance** (≈ 2 MΩ for IC 741), making it ideal for buffering high-impedance sources.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B(b) (7 marks):**

**Internal Block Diagram of IC 741 (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
graph LR
    nVneg["Pin 2 - Inverting"] --> nDA["Differential Amplifier - Q1 Q2 Q3 Q4"]
    nVpos["Pin 3 - Non-Inverting"] --> nDA
    nDA --> nG2["High Gain Stage - Darlington Q15 Q16 Q17"]
    nG2 --> nLev["Level Shifter - Q11 Q12"]
    nLev --> nOut["Output Stage - Class AB Q14 Q20"]
    nOut --> nVout["Pin 6 - Vout"]
    nBias["Bias Network - Q5 Q6 Q7"] --> nDA
    nBias --> nG2
```

**Pin Functions (Memorize for KTU Viva):**

| Pin | Function |
|---|---|
| 1 | Offset Null (used to trim DC offset with a 10 kΩ potentiometer) |
| 2 | Inverting Input (V⁻) |
| 3 | Non-Inverting Input (V⁺) |
| 4 | V⁻ Supply (typically −15 V) |
| 5 | Offset Null (paired with Pin 1) |
| 6 | Output (V_out) |
| 7 | V⁺ Supply (typically +15 V) |
| 8 | No Connection (NC) |

**Key Features Mentioned (1 mark):**
- Internally frequency compensated
- Short-circuit protection
- Large common-mode and differential voltage range

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to ground Pin 3 in an inverting configuration** — This causes the op-amp to float and the output saturates to either +Vsat or −Vsat. Always ensure Pin 3 is grounded for inverting amplifier and inverting adder.
>
> 2. **Confusing the virtual ground (V⁻ = 0 V) with actual ground** — Virtual ground is a **node that is held at 0 V by feedback**, not a physical connection to ground. Don't short Pin 2 to ground in the inverting amplifier!
>
> 3. **Sign error in the output formula** — The inverting amplifier output is **negative** (V_out = −(Rf/R1)·Vin). Students often drop the negative sign, losing 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Not checking the saturation condition** — Always verify |V_out| < |V_sat| ≈ |V_cc| − 2V. If the calculated V_out exceeds this, the op-amp saturates and the experiment fails.
>
> 5. **Resistor selection in the adder** — The ratio of resistors determines the **weight** of each input. If asked to "sum" the inputs with equal weight, all input resistors **and** Rf must be equal.
>
> 6. **Skipping the KCL derivation steps** — In Part B, marks are awarded for **showing KCL and the algebraic simplification**. Don't jump from the circuit directly to the formula.
>
> 7. **Not mentioning phase relationship** — In KTU viva, you are expected to state that the inverting amplifier produces a **180° phase shift** and the non-inverting amplifier produces a **0° phase shift**.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Op-amp IC 741** is a general-purpose, 8-pin DIP op-amp with **open-loop gain of 2 × 10⁵**, **input impedance of 2 MΩ**, and **bandwidth of 1 MHz**.
- **Two Golden Rules:** (1) V⁺ = V⁻ (virtual short), (2) I⁺ = I⁻ = 0 (no input current).
- **Inverting Amplifier:** V_out = −(Rf/R1)·V_in; 180° phase shift; input impedance = R1.
- **Non-Inverting Amplifier:** V_out = (1 + Rf/R1)·V_in; 0° phase shift; input impedance ≈ 2 MΩ.
- **Inverting Adder:** V_out = −Rf(V1/R1 + V2/R2 + V3/R3); if all resistors are equal, V_out = −(V1 + V2 + V3).
- **Non-Inverting Adder:** Requires the matching condition 1/R_a + 1/R_b + 1/R_c = 1/R_1; output is a positive weighted sum scaled by (1 + Rf/R1).
- **Pin Configuration (MUST MEMORIZE):** Pin 2 = (−) input, Pin 3 = (+) input, Pin 4 = V⁻ supply, Pin 6 = V_out, Pin 7 = V⁺ supply.
- **Always check saturation:** |V_out| < V_cc − 2V (typically < 13 V for ±15 V supply).
- **Lab precautions:** Use ±15 V dual supply, ground Pin 3 for inverting circuits, apply input through R1, connect Rf from output to inverting input.
- **Measurement instruments:** Function generator (input signal source), CRO/DSO (waveform observation), Digital Multimeter (DC voltage verification).
- **Design procedure for the lab record:** (1) State the required gain/equation, (2) Choose R1, (3) Calculate Rf, (4) Verify output is within supply range, (5) Draw the circuit diagram, (6) List components, (7) Tabulate expected vs. measured values.
- **Standard resistor values used in lab:** 1 kΩ, 10 kΩ, 100 kΩ (for inverting/non-inverting); 10 kΩ for all resistors in unity-weight adder.
- **Frequency of operation:** Use 1 kHz sine wave — well within the 1 MHz bandwidth of IC 741, so gain remains constant.
- **Equation of an inverting amplifier is the foundation** for understanding filters, integrators, differentiators, and DAC circuits in higher semesters.
- **The non-inverting amplifier is the basis** of the voltage follower (unity gain buffer) — used as an impedance transformer.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Tip:** In the lab record, always include a **comparison table** of theoretical vs. measured gain and a **graphical plot** of input vs. output waveforms. The viva panel will specifically ask you to explain the **virtual ground** concept and the **180° phase shift** observed in the inverting amplifier.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Op-amp Circuits - Design and Set Up of Integrator and Differentiator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL307 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Op-amp Circuits - Design and Set Up of Integrator and Differentiator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Spirit):**
> An **Op-amp Integrator** is an active linear circuit built around an inverting operational amplifier whose feedback element is a **capacitor** (C), producing an output voltage proportional to the **time integral of the input voltage**. Conversely, an **Op-amp Differentiator** uses a **capacitor at the input** and a **resistor in the feedback path**, producing an output proportional to the **time derivative of the input voltage**. Both belong to the family of *Linear Wave-Shaping Circuits using Op-amps*.

In the 2024 scheme lab module, the experiment is designed to:
- Verify the input–output mathematical relationship of both circuits experimentally.
- Observe the effect of square-wave and triangular-wave inputs on the output.
- Compute the **time constant** $T = RC$ and compare theoretical versus observed responses.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Water Tank Analogy:**
> - **Integrator** = Imagine pouring water into a tank. The water *level* (output) builds up over *time* as water (input) is added continuously. The output represents the **accumulated history** of the input. Rate of filling = input; Tank level = integral.
> - **Differentiator** = Imagine a speedometer in a car. It shows the **rate of change of position** (input = distance, output = velocity = derivative). The output reacts *strongly* to *sudden changes* and *ignores* steady (DC) input.
> 
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Integrate smooth signals; differentiate sharp signals. Engineers use integrators in **ADC ramp generators, PID controllers, and analog computers**; differentiators in **edge detectors, FM demodulators, and lead compensators**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Op-amp supply $V_{CC}, -V_{EE}$ | **±15 V** | Standard dual supply for IC 741 |
| IC used | **µA741 / LM741** | 8-pin DIP, single op-amp |
| Slew rate of 741 | **0.5 V/µs** | Limits max signal frequency |
| Input impedance | **~2 MΩ** | Very high for ideal op-amp |
| Ideal op-amp open-loop gain $A$ | **∞ (ideally), 2×10⁵ (741)** | Drives virtual ground concept |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Working Logic

### 2.1.1 Ideal Op-amp Integrator (Miller Integrator)

**Circuit Topology:** Inverting configuration where feedback resistor $R_f$ is replaced by capacitor $C$.

- **Step 1 — Virtual Ground:** Because the non-inverting terminal is grounded and $A \to \infty$, the inverting terminal is also at $0\,\text{V}$ (virtual ground).
- **Step 2 — No Current into Op-amp:** The input bias current is negligible ($\approx 0$), so the entire current through $R_1$ must flow through the capacitor $C$.
- **Step 3 — Capacitor I-V Relation:** The current through the capacitor equals $C \cdot \dfrac{dV_C}{dt}$. Since $V_C = V_{out} - 0 = V_{out}$, the discharging current is $-C\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt}$.
- **Step 4 — KCL at Inverting Node:** $\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1} + C\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt} = 0$.
- **Step 5 — Integrate Both Sides:** $V_{out}(t) = -\dfrac{1}{R_1 C}\displaystyle\int V_{in}(t)\,dt + V_{out}(0)$.

### 2.1.2 Ideal Op-amp Differentiator

**Circuit Topology:** Inverting configuration where the input resistor $R_1$ is replaced by capacitor $C$, and feedback is a resistor $R_f$.

- **Step 1 — Virtual Ground** again at the inverting node.
- **Step 2 — Current into C:** $i_{in} = C\dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ (since the other plate of the capacitor is at virtual ground).
- **Step 3 — Current through $R_f$:** $i_f = -\dfrac{V_{out}}{R_f}$.
- **Step 4 — KCL:** $C\dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt} + \dfrac{V_{out}}{R_f} = 0$.
- **Step 5 — Solve:** $V_{out}(t) = -R_f C \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$.

## 2.2 Why the Differentiator is Unstable — The Practical Issue

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Noise Amplification Problem:**
> The differentiator's gain *increases linearly* with frequency ($|H(j\omega)| = \omega R_f C$). At high frequencies, **noise spikes get amplified massively**, and the 741's slew rate is exceeded — causing **triangular-wave output to clip into a sine/distorted waveform**. Therefore, a *practical differentiator* adds a small series resistor $R_1$ and a parallel capacitor $C_f$ to limit high-frequency gain.

Similarly, a **practical (lossy) integrator** adds a large resistor $R_f$ in **parallel** with $C$ to provide a **DC feedback path**, preventing output saturation due to input offset voltage and bias current.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 HIGH-YIELD EQUATIONS — Memorize This Table for the Lab Exam Viva & Record.**

| # | Parameter | Equation | Units | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Integrator output | $V_{out}(t) = -\dfrac{1}{R_1 C}\int V_{in}\,dt$ | Volt | $R_1$ in Ω, $C$ in F |
| 2 | Differentiator output | $V_{out}(t) = -R_f C\,\dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ | Volt | $R_f C$ is the time constant |
| 3 | Integrator time constant | $T_i = R_1 C$ | second | Choose $T_i \gg$ input period for integration |
| 4 | Differentiator time constant | $T_d = R_f C$ | second | Choose $T_d \ll$ input period for differentiation |
| 5 | Integrator cutoff freq. (lossy) | $f_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C}$ | Hz | $R_f$ is the added feedback resistor |
| 6 | Differentiator cutoff freq. | $f_b = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C}$ | Hz | $R_1$ added in series to limit gain |
| 7 | Ideal op-amp gain | $A = \infty$ | — | Approximation in DC analysis |
| 8 | Practical 741 gain | $A = 2 \times 10^5$ | — | Open-loop DC gain |
| 9 | Integrator for square input | $V_{out}$ = Triangular wave | — | Amplitude = $V_{in} \cdot T/2R_1C$ |
| 10 | Differentiator for triangular input | $V_{out}$ = Square wave | — | Amplitude = $V_{peak} \cdot 4 f R_f C$ |
| 11 | Frequency response integrator | $\vert H(j\omega)\vert = \dfrac{1}{\omega R_1 C}$ | — | -20 dB/decade roll-off |
| 12 | Frequency response differentiator | $\vert H(j\omega)\vert = \omega R_f C$ | — | +20 dB/decade rise |

> **Engineering Utility:**
> Integrators are the heart of **analog computers, ramp generators in oscilloscopes, dual-slope ADCs, and active filters**. Differentiators find use in **PID controllers (D-term), FM demodulators, and high-frequency edge-trigger circuits** in instrumentation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Ideal Integrator

Starting from the inverting amplifier node and applying Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the inverting terminal (node $V^-$):

$$
i_{R_1} = i_C
$$

**Step 1 — Express current through input resistor:**
Since the non-inverting terminal is grounded, and by virtual short $V^- = V^+ = 0$, the voltage across $R_1$ is $V_{in} - 0 = V_{in}$.

$$
i_{R_1} = \frac{V_{in} - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_{in}}{R_1}
$$

**Step 2 — Express current through feedback capacitor:**
The voltage across the capacitor is $V^- - V_{out} = 0 - V_{out} = -V_{out}$. For a capacitor, $i = C\,\dfrac{dV}{dt}$.

$$
i_C = C \cdot \frac{d(-V_{out})}{dt} = -C\,\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply KCL:**

$$
\frac{V_{in}}{R_1} = -C\,\frac{dV_{out}}{dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange for $V_{out}$:**

$$
\frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{in}}{R_1 C}
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$:**

$$
V_{out}(t) - V_{out}(0) = -\frac{1}{R_1 C}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

**Step 6 — Final expression (assuming initial capacitor voltage = 0):**

$$
V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{R_1 C}\int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(\tau)\,d\tau
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative sign confirms **inverting** action. The factor $\dfrac{1}{R_1 C}$ is called the **integrator gain coefficient** $K_i$ with units of $\text{s}^{-1}$.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Ideal Differentiator

**Step 1 — Current through input capacitor:**
Voltage across $C$ at the input is $V_{in} - V^- = V_{in} - 0 = V_{in}$.

$$
i_C = C\,\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Current through feedback resistor $R_f$:**

$$
i_{R_f} = \frac{V^- - V_{out}}{R_f} = \frac{0 - V_{out}}{R_f} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}
$$

**Step 3 — KCL at the inverting node (no current enters the op-amp):**

$$
C\,\frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $V_{out}$:**

$$
V_{out}(t) = -R_f C\,\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The product $R_f C$ is the **differentiator time constant** $T_d$. The negative sign indicates **180° phase inversion** between input and derivative.

## 3.3 Design Example (Typical KTU Lab Numerical)

**Problem:** Design an op-amp integrator for a square wave of **1 kHz, ±5 V** such that the triangular output amplitude is **2 V peak**. Find $R_1$ and $C$.

**Given:** $f = 1\,\text{kHz} \Rightarrow T = 1\,\text{ms}$; $V_{in} = 5\,\text{V}$; $V_{out,\text{peak}} = 2\,\text{V}$.

**Step 1 — During the positive half-cycle ($T/2 = 0.5\,\text{ms}$), the integrator output ramps linearly:**

$$
V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{R_1 C}\int_{0}^{T/2} 5\,dt = -\frac{5 \cdot T/2}{R_1 C}
$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude equation:**

$$
2 = \frac{5 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{R_1 C} \Rightarrow R_1 C = 1.25 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 3 — Choose $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F} = 10^{-7}\,\text{F}$:**

$$
R_1 = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-7}} = 12.5\,\text{k}\Omega \quad (\text{use } 12\,\text{k}\Omega \text{ standard})
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Integrator | Differentiator |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical operation | $\int V_{in}\,dt$ | $\dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ |
| Square-wave input | Triangular output | Spike output (impulse train) |
| Triangular-wave input | Parabolic output | Square-wave output |
| Sine-wave input | Cosine output (90° lag) | Cosine output (90° lead) |
| Frequency response slope | -20 dB/decade | +20 dB/decade |
| Stability | Stable if lossy | Unstable at high frequencies |
| Noise behaviour | Attenuates high-freq noise | Amplifies high-freq noise |
| Real-world use | ADC, filter, computer | Edge detector, FM demod |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Op-amp Integrator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT V_in"] -->|Through R1| B["INVERTING NODE V_minus"]
    B -->|Virtual Ground| C["OPAMP 741"]
    D["GROUND 0V"] --> E["NON-INVERTING NODE V_plus"]
    E --> C
    C -->|Output| F["OUTPUT V_out"]
    B -->|Feedback via C| F
    C -->|Power supply| G["Plus Minus 15V DC"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Op-amp Differentiator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["INPUT V_in"] -->|Through C| B["INVERTING NODE V_minus"]
    B -->|Virtual Ground| C["OPAMP 741"]
    D["GROUND 0V"] --> E["NON-INVERTING NODE V_plus"]
    E --> C
    C -->|Output| F["OUTPUT V_out"]
    B -->|Feedback via Rf| F
    C -->|Power supply| G["Plus Minus 15V DC"]
```

## 4.3 Practical (Lossy) Integrator Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["V_in Square Wave"] -->|R1| B["Summing Junction V_minus"]
    B -->|Cf parallel Rf| C["Opamp 741"]
    D["GND"] -->|plus input| C
    C --> E["V_out Triangular Wave"]
    F["DC Stabilizing Path Rf"] -.->|Prevents saturation| B
    G["AC Integrating Path Cf"] -.->|Integrates| B
```

## 4.4 Signal Transformation Flow (Input-to-Output Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Square Wave Input"] -->|Integrator| B["Triangular Output"]
    A -->|Differentiator| C["Impulse Spike Output"]
    D["Triangular Input"] -->|Integrator| E["Parabolic Output"]
    D -->|Differentiator| F["Square Wave Output"]
    G["Sinusoidal Input"] -->|Integrator| H["Cosine 90 deg Lag"]
    G -->|Differentiator| I["Cosine 90 deg Lead"]
```

## 4.5 Frequency Response Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Frequency Axis f"] --> B["Integrator Response"]
    A --> C["Differentiator Response"]
    B -->|"Slope minus 20 dB per decade"| D["Drops at high f"]
    C -->|"Slope plus 20 dB per decade"| E["Rises at high f"]
    D --> F["Acts as Low Pass Filter"]
    E --> G["Acts as High Pass Filter"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> Define an op-amp integrator. Derive the expression for its output voltage.
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> An op-amp integrator is an active circuit using an op-amp in inverting configuration with a **capacitor $C$ as the feedback element** and resistor $R_1$ at the input. **(1 mark)**
> Applying KCL at the virtual ground node: $\dfrac{V_{in}}{R_1} = -C\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt}$. **(1 mark)**
> Integrating: $V_{out}(t) = -\dfrac{1}{R_1 C}\int V_{in}(t)\,dt + V_{out}(0)$. **(1 mark)**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> Differentiate between an ideal and a practical (lossy) integrator. Why is the resistor $R_f$ added in parallel with $C$ in the practical integrator?
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The ideal integrator has only $C$ in feedback; it suffers from **output saturation** due to op-amp input offset voltage and bias current being continuously integrated. **(1 mark)**
> The practical (lossy) integrator adds a high-value feedback resistor $R_f$ (typically $10 R_1$) in parallel with $C$. **(1 mark)**
> This $R_f$ provides a **DC feedback path**, limiting the DC gain to $A_0 = -R_f/R_1$ (finite) and prevents drift/saturation while preserving integration at frequencies $f \gg 1/(2\pi R_f C)$. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Question A (14 Marks)**
> 
> **(a)** Design an op-amp integrator to integrate a **1 kHz square wave of amplitude ±5 V**, producing a **triangular output of amplitude 2 V peak**. Choose suitable values of $R_1$ and $C$. Show the circuit. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Explain the **practical integrator circuit** with a neat sketch. State the role of the additional feedback resistor $R_f$ and derive the expression for its lower cutoff frequency $f_a$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Q3(a)

**Step 1 — Write the integrator output equation:**

$$
V_{out}(t) = -\frac{1}{R_1 C}\int V_{in}\,dt
$$

**Step 2 — For a square wave, $V_{in} = +5$ V for half-period $T/2$. The output ramps linearly. At $t = T/2$:**

$$
V_{out,\text{peak}} = \frac{V_{in} \cdot T/2}{R_1 C} = \frac{5 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{R_1 C} = 2\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve:**

$$
R_1 C = 1.25 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Choose $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ (standard value):**

$$
R_1 = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-7}} = 12.5\,\text{k}\Omega \approx 12\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 5 — Verification:** Time constant $T_i = R_1 C = 1.25$ ms; Input period $T = 1$ ms. Since $T_i \gg T$ is not strictly true, the circuit acts as a **good integrator** (integration occurs when $T \gg T_i$, but here triangular shape is still obtained with slight non-linearity). For better integration, increase $C$ to $0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ and $R_1$ to $15\,\text{k}\Omega$. **(2 marks for circuit drawing + 5 marks for design steps)**

### Model Solution — Q3(b)

**Step 1 — Practical Integrator Circuit:**
A large resistor $R_f$ is connected in **parallel** with the feedback capacitor $C$ to form a lossy integrator.

**Step 2 — Derivation of Lower Cutoff Frequency:**
Using nodal analysis with feedback impedance $Z_f = R_f \Vert (1/sC)$:

$$
Z_f = \frac{R_f \cdot \frac{1}{sC}}{R_f + \frac{1}{sC}} = \frac{R_f}{1 + sR_f C}
$$

The transfer function:

$$
H(s) = -\frac{Z_f}{R_1} = -\frac{R_f/R_1}{1 + sR_f C}
$$

**Step 3 — Substituting $s = j\omega$ and finding the -3 dB frequency:**

$$
f_a = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C}
$$

**Step 4 — Role of $R_f$:** It limits the DC gain to a finite value $A_0 = -R_f/R_1$, preventing the integration of DC offset and bias current, which would otherwise cause the output to saturate. The integration action is preserved for $f \gg f_a$. **(7 marks complete)**

---

> **Q4. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**
> 
> **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an **op-amp differentiator**. Derive the expression for its output voltage. State one limitation. **(7 marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Design a practical op-amp differentiator to differentiate a **triangular wave of 2 kHz, ±4 V peak**, so that the square-wave output has an amplitude of **3 V**. Show component calculations. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Q4(a)

**Step 1 — Circuit:** Op-amp in inverting configuration with capacitor $C$ at input, resistor $R_f$ in feedback.

**Step 2 — Apply virtual ground and KCL:**

$$
C\frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = -\frac{V_{out}}{R_f}
$$

**Step 3 — Output equation:**

$$
V_{out}(t) = -R_f C\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Limitation:** The gain increases with frequency ($|H| = \omega R_f C$), causing high-frequency **noise amplification** and instability. The op-amp's **slew rate limit (0.5 V/µs for 741)** is exceeded, distorting the output. **(7 marks complete)**

### Model Solution — Q4(b)

**Step 1 — For a triangular input of peak $V_p = 4$ V, period $T = 0.5$ ms, the slope during the rising portion:**

$$
\frac{dV_{in}}{dt} = \frac{4V_p}{T/2} = \frac{8}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.6 \times 10^{4}\,\text{V/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiator output amplitude:**

$$
V_{out} = R_f C \cdot \left|\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}\right| = R_f C \times 1.6 \times 10^{4} = 3\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve:**

$$
R_f C = \frac{3}{1.6 \times 10^{4}} = 1.875 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Choose $C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F} = 10^{-8}$ F:**

$$
R_f = \frac{1.875 \times 10^{-4}}{10^{-8}} = 18.75\,\text{k}\Omega \approx 18\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

**Step 5 — Practical differentiator components:** Add series resistor $R_1 \approx 0.1 R_f \approx 1.8\,\text{k}\Omega$ and feedback capacitor $C_f \approx 0.1 C \approx 1\,\text{nF}$ to limit high-frequency gain. **(7 marks complete)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgot the negative sign:** Many students write $V_{out} = +\frac{1}{RC}\int V_{in}\,dt$. The inverting configuration **always** produces a negative output. Loss of **1 mark** if missed.
> 2. **Units mismatch:** Writing $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1\,\mu\text{F}$ but forgetting to convert to SI units ($\Omega$ and Farad) before computing $RC$. Loss of **1–2 marks** in the derivation.
> 3. **Confusing lossy integrator with practical differentiator:** The lossy integrator has $R_f \Vert C$ in feedback. The practical differentiator has $R_1$ in series with $C$ at input AND $C_f$ parallel to $R_f$ in feedback. Don't mix them up.
> 4. **Skipping the circuit diagram:** In KTU 2024 lab exams, the **circuit diagram carries 2–3 marks** out of 14. Always draw with proper labels ($R_1$, $C$, $V_{in}$, $V_{out}$, IC 741 pin numbers).
> 5. **Ignoring initial condition:** $V_{out}(0)$ appears in the integrator equation. For a square wave input, the output is a **triangular wave centered on the average input**, not around 0 V.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Lab Exam**

- ☐ **Integrator** = Inverting op-amp + **Capacitor in feedback** → Output is $-\frac{1}{R_1 C}\int V_{in}\,dt$.
- ☐ **Differentiator** = Inverting op-amp + **Capacitor at input, Resistor in feedback** → Output is $-R_f C\,\frac{dV_{in}}{dt}$.
- ☐ **Virtual ground** assumption is the foundation of both derivations.
- ☐ The **time constant** of integrator is $T_i = R_1 C$ and of differentiator is $T_d = R_f C$.
- ☐ Choose $T_i \gg T_{input}$ for good integration; choose $T_d \ll T_{input}$ for good differentiation.
- ☐ **Square wave → Integrator → Triangular wave**; **Triangular wave → Differentiator → Square wave** — remember this golden pair.
- ☐ **Lossy Integrator** adds $R_f \Vert C$ in feedback to prevent saturation. Cutoff $f_a = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C}$.
- ☐ **Practical Differentiator** adds series $R_1$ at input and parallel $C_f$ at feedback to limit high-frequency noise. Cutoff $f_b = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C}$.
- ☐ **Slew rate** of 741 (0.5 V/µs) limits the maximum operating frequency; check $S R > 2\pi f V_{out,\text{peak}}$.
- ☐ Always verify the **negative sign** (inverting action) in the final expression.
- ☐ IC 741 pinout: **Pin 2 = inverting input**, **Pin 3 = non-inverting input**, **Pin 6 = output**, **Pins 7 & 4 = ±V supply**.
- ☐ Connect **pin 8 (NC)** floating; tie any unused inputs to ground to avoid stray pickup.
- ☐ Use a **dual power supply ±15 V** for proper operation around 0 V.
- ☐ In the lab record, **always tabulate** theoretical vs. observed amplitude and compute **% error** — this is a mandatory KTU record entry.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Comparator and Schmitt Trigger Circuits Using Op-amp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL307 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Basic Comparator and Schmitt Trigger Circuits Using Op-amp

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Basic Op-Amp Comparator — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Standard):** A **comparator** is an open-loop operational amplifier circuit (no negative feedback) that compares two analog input voltages and produces a binary output indicating which input is greater. The output saturates to either the **positive saturation voltage (+Vsat)** or the **negative saturation voltage (−Vsat)** depending on the polarity of the differential input voltage.

Mathematically, the comparator's output is governed by:

$$V_{out} = \begin{cases} +V_{sat} & \text{when } V_{d} = (V_{+} - V_{-}) > 0 \\ -V_{sat} & \text{when } V_{d} = (V_{+} - V_{-}) < 0 \end{cases}$$

The ideal op-amp comparator has:
- **Infinite open-loop gain** (Aol ≈ 10⁵ to 10⁶ for µA741)
- **Infinite input impedance**
- **Zero output impedance**
- **Zero threshold voltage** (switching happens at the exact crossing point)
- **Infinite slew rate** (in ideal model)

### 1.2 Schmitt Trigger — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A **Schmitt Trigger** is a comparator circuit that uses **positive (regenerative) feedback** to introduce **hysteresis** in its transfer characteristic. The hysteresis creates two distinct threshold voltages — the **Upper Threshold Voltage (VUT)** and the **Lower Threshold Voltage (VLT)** — thereby providing noise immunity and preventing rapid chattering at the output.

The width of the hysteresis loop is:

$$V_{H} = V_{UT} - V_{LT}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Vsat Reference Values for µA741 (KTU Lab Standard):** The typical saturation voltages for a dual-supply op-amp with **±Vcc = ±15 V** are **+Vsat ≈ +13.5 V** and **−Vsat ≈ −13.5 V** (not exactly ±15 V due to internal transistor saturation drops of ~1.5 V).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

| Circuit | Real-World Analogy | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|
| **Basic Comparator** | A **balance scale judge** standing at a weigh-off. The moment the needle tilts even slightly to the right, he shouts "RIGHT WINS!" If it trembles, he shouts repeatedly → **chattering**. | Works perfectly for clean DC signals, but fails for noisy signals. |
| **Schmitt Trigger** | A **home thermostat** for your room. It does NOT switch on the AC at 26°C and off at 26°C. It switches ON at 28°C (VUT) and OFF at 24°C (VLT). The **gap of 4°C is hysteresis**. | Even if temperature jitters around 26°C, the AC does not oscillate. |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** *Comparator = single threshold. Schmitt Trigger = two thresholds with a dead-band (hysteresis).* Think of Schmitt Trigger as a comparator with **muscle memory** — it remembers where it came from.

### 1.4 Why This Topic Is a KTU Lab Favorite

This experiment appears in **PCEOL307 (Analog and Digital Electronics Lab)** as a hands-on bridge between:
1. The theoretical op-amp concepts taught in **PCET203 / Linear Integrated Circuits**.
2. The waveform-generation and ADC interfacing topics of higher semesters.

The lab examiner expects you to **wire the circuit on a breadboard**, observe the Lissajous-like **transfer characteristic on a CRO**, and **measure the hysteresis loop** directly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Basic Comparator — Operational Logic

There are **two canonical configurations** that KTU expects you to know.

#### 2.1.1 Inverting Comparator
- The **reference voltage (Vref)** is applied to the **non-inverting (+) terminal**.
- The **input signal (Vin)** is applied to the **inverting (−) terminal**.

| Condition | Output |
|---|---|
| Vin > Vref | Vout = −Vsat (LOW) |
| Vin < Vref | Vout = +Vsat (HIGH) |

The output **inverts** with respect to the input — hence the name.

#### 2.1.2 Non-Inverting Comparator
- The **reference voltage (Vref)** is applied to the **inverting (−) terminal**.
- The **input signal (Vin)** is applied to the **non-inverting (+) terminal**.

| Condition | Output |
|---|---|
| Vin > Vref | Vout = +Vsat (HIGH) |
| Vin < Vref | Vout = −Vsat (LOW) |

### 2.2 Schmitt Trigger — Operational Logic

The **Schmitt Trigger** adds a **positive feedback network** (resistor R1 from output to non-inverting input, and resistor R2 from non-inverting input to ground). The feedback fraction β is:

$$\beta = \frac{R_{2}}{R_{1} + R_{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule:** In any Schmitt Trigger, the **signal input** always enters the **inverting terminal** of the **inverting Schmitt Trigger** (and the non-inverting terminal of the non-inverting one). The **feedback network** always terminates at the **non-inverting input** — this is what creates the regenerative positive feedback.

#### 2.2.1 Inverting Schmitt Trigger (Most Tested Variant)

| Input Action | Threshold Crossed | Output Transition |
|---|---|---|
| Vin increasing from low value | Crosses **VUT** (positive threshold) | Vout flips from **+Vsat to −Vsat** |
| Vin decreasing from high value | Crosses **VLT** (negative threshold) | Vout flips from **−Vsat to +Vsat** |

The two threshold voltages are derived from the **voltage divider at the non-inverting node**:

$$V_{UT} = \beta \cdot (+V_{sat}) = \left(\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1} + R_{2}}\right) \cdot (+V_{sat})$$

$$V_{LT} = \beta \cdot (-V_{sat}) = \left(\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1} + R_{2}}\right) \cdot (-V_{sat})$$

The **hysteresis width** is therefore symmetric about zero:

$$V_{H} = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 2 \beta \cdot V_{sat} = \left(\frac{2 R_{2}}{R_{1} + R_{2}}\right) \cdot V_{sat}$$

#### 2.2.2 Non-Inverting Schmitt Trigger

In this configuration, the input enters the **non-inverting terminal**, and a portion of the input is also fed into the inverting terminal through the feedback network. The threshold voltages become **asymmetric** and depend on Vin.

For a non-inverting Schmitt trigger with input Vin and reference ground (0 V) at the divider:

$$V_{UT} = -\left(\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1}}\right) \cdot V_{sat} \quad \text{(when Vout = −Vsat)}$$

$$V_{LT} = +\left(\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1}}\right) \cdot V_{sat} \quad \text{(when Vout = +Vsat)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Exam Tip:** The non-inverting Schmitt trigger is **rarely** asked in the lab exam, but the **inverting Schmitt trigger is the workhorse** of KTU questions.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **definitive KTU formula sheet** for this experiment. Memorize it before entering the lab.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes / KTU Exam Hint |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Open-loop gain of op-amp | $A_{OL} = 10^{5} \text{ to } 10^{6}$ | V/V | For µA741; used to justify Vout = Vsat when differential input is non-zero |
| 2 | Inverting comparator output | $V_{out} = +V_{sat} \text{ if } V_{in} < V_{ref}$ | V | "Opposite polarity" output |
| 3 | Non-inverting comparator output | $V_{out} = +V_{sat} \text{ if } V_{in} > V_{ref}$ | V | "Same polarity" output |
| 4 | Feedback fraction (Schmitt) | $\beta = R_{2} / (R_{1} + R_{2})$ | dimensionless | Always $\beta < 1$ for stability |
| 5 | Upper Threshold Voltage | $V_{UT} = \beta \cdot (+V_{sat})$ | V | Crossing point on rising Vin |
| 6 | Lower Threshold Voltage | $V_{LT} = \beta \cdot (-V_{sat})$ | V | Crossing point on falling Vin |
| 7 | Hysteresis Width | $V_{H} = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 2 \beta \cdot V_{sat}$ | V | Measure directly on CRO |
| 8 | Schmitt Trigger Frequency (with RC) | $f = \frac{1}{2RC \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}$ | Hz | Used when RC timing network is added |
| 9 | Trip point (general) | $V_{+} = V_{-}$ | V | The fundamental switching condition |
| 10 | Hysteresis as % of Vsat | $\%V_{H} = \beta \cdot 100$ | % | For symmetry, half of it lies on each side of 0 V |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why KTU tests this experiment:** Schmitt Triggers and Comparators are the **building blocks of every digital interface circuit** an engineer will ever design.

| Application Area | Use of Comparator / Schmitt Trigger |
|---|---|
| **Zero-Crossing Detectors (ZCD)** | Square wave generation from sine mains in triac firing circuits |
| **ADC Front-Ends** | Flash ADC uses $2^{N}-1$ comparators in parallel |
| **Oscilloscope Triggering** | CRO trigger circuits use Schmitt triggers to avoid multiple triggers on noise |
| **Automotive** | Window comparators detect over-voltage / under-voltage in battery management |
| **Signal Conditioning** | Converting noisy analog sensor signals to clean logic levels for microcontrollers |
| **Relaxation Oscillators** | Schmitt + RC timing network → astable multivibrator (function generator block) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Directive:** Derivations in this module must be **fully expanded** — every intermediate algebraic step shown. Skipping steps is a guaranteed 2–3 mark loss in ESE valuation.

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Threshold Voltages of the Inverting Schmitt Trigger

**Given:** Op-amp µA741 powered by ±Vcc = ±15 V (so ±Vsat ≈ ±13.5 V), feedback resistors R1 (output to +input) and R2 (+input to ground), input signal Vin applied at the inverting terminal.

**Step 1 — Write the nodal equation at the non-inverting node (call it node A).**

Since the op-amp draws no current into its input terminals, the currents flowing through R1 and R2 must be equal (KCL at the +input node):

$$I_{R_{1}} = I_{R_{2}}$$

This gives the voltage at the +input as a **weighted sum of Vout and ground**:

$$V_{+} = V_{out} \cdot \left(\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1} + R_{2}}\right) + 0 \cdot \left(\frac{R_{1}}{R_{1} + R_{2}}\right)$$

$$\boxed{V_{+} = \beta \cdot V_{out}} \quad \text{where} \quad \beta = \frac{R_{2}}{R_{1} + R_{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the switching condition (the heart of the comparator).**

The op-amp output flips state when the differential input crosses zero:

$$V_{+} = V_{-}$$

At the switching instant, the inverting input equals the input signal:

$$V_{-} = V_{in}$$

So at switching:

$$V_{in} = V_{+} = \beta \cdot V_{out}$$

**Step 3 — Find VUT (the upper threshold, when Vout = +Vsat).**

This is the value of Vin at which the output flips **from +Vsat to −Vsat** as Vin rises. Substitute Vout = +Vsat:

$$V_{UT} = \beta \cdot (+V_{sat}) = \left(\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1} + R_{2}}\right) \cdot (+V_{sat})$$

**Step 4 — Find VLT (the lower threshold, when Vout = −Vsat).**

This is the value of Vin at which the output flips **from −Vsat to +Vsat** as Vin falls. Substitute Vout = −Vsat:

$$V_{LT} = \beta \cdot (-V_{sat}) = \left(\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1} + R_{2}}\right) \cdot (-V_{sat})$$

**Step 5 — Compute the hysteresis width.**

$$V_{H} = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = \beta \cdot V_{sat} - \beta \cdot (-V_{sat}) = 2\beta \cdot V_{sat}$$

$$\boxed{V_{H} = \frac{2 R_{2} \cdot V_{sat}}{R_{1} + R_{2}}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Example (KTU-style Problem).**

Let R1 = 10 kΩ, R2 = 1 kΩ, Vsat = 13.5 V.

$$\beta = \frac{1 \text{ k}\Omega}{10 \text{ k}\Omega + 1 \text{ k}\Omega} = \frac{1}{11} \approx 0.0909$$

$$V_{UT} = 0.0909 \times (+13.5) = +1.227 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{LT} = 0.0909 \times (-13.5) = -1.227 \text{ V}$$

$$V_{H} = 2 \times 0.0909 \times 13.5 = 2.454 \text{ V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in the lab:** You will **measure** these three values on the CRO (using X-Y mode) and they must **match the calculated values within ±10%** to get full marks.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Frequency of a Schmitt-Trigger Relaxation Oscillator

**Given:** Inverting Schmitt Trigger (R1, R2) with a capacitor C connected from the inverting input to ground, and a resistor R from the inverting input to the output.

**Step 1 — Identify the state of the circuit.**

Assume the output is at **+Vsat**. The capacitor C will charge through R towards +Vsat. The voltage at the inverting input (Vc) rises exponentially:

$$V_{c}(t) = V_{final} - (V_{final} - V_{initial}) \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

With Vfinal = +Vsat and the trip point VUT = β·Vsat, the time to trip is found by setting Vc(t) = VUT:

$$V_{UT} = +V_{sat} - (+V_{sat} - (-V_{sat})) \cdot e^{-T_{1}/RC}$$

$$V_{UT} = V_{sat} - 2V_{sat} \cdot e^{-T_{1}/RC}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for T1 (half-period on the charging side).**

$$\beta \cdot V_{sat} = V_{sat} - 2 V_{sat} \cdot e^{-T_{1}/RC}$$

$$\beta = 1 - 2 e^{-T_{1}/RC}$$

$$e^{-T_{1}/RC} = \frac{1 - \beta}{2}$$

$$T_{1} = RC \cdot \ln\left(\frac{2}{1 - \beta}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Solve for T2 (half-period on the discharging side, output = −Vsat).**

By symmetry:

$$T_{2} = RC \cdot \ln\left(\frac{2}{1 - \beta}\right) = T_{1}$$

**Step 4 — Total period and frequency.**

$$T = T_{1} + T_{2} = 2RC \cdot \ln\left(\frac{2}{1 - \beta}\right) = 2RC \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta}\right)$$

(since 1+β = 2 − (1−β), wait — we use the identity 2/(1−β) = (1+β)/(1−β) only if we re-derive; the standard KTU form is):

$$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{2RC \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For the special case where **R1 = R2 (β = 0.5)**, this simplifies to the famous **f = 1/(2RC·ln(3)) ≈ 0.455/RC**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparator vs. Schmitt Trigger Side-by-Side

| Parameter | Basic Comparator | Schmitt Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback type | **None** (open-loop) | **Positive feedback** through R1, R2 |
| Number of threshold voltages | **1** (Vref) | **2** (VUT and VLT) |
| Output for noisy input | **Chattering / multiple transitions** | **Clean single transition** |
| Use as oscillator | No | Yes (with RC network) |
| Speed (switching) | Slower (no regenerative action) | Faster (regenerative snap action) |
| Transfer characteristic | Single straight line in VTC | **Hysteresis loop** in VTC |
| Inverting configuration | Vin at (−), Vref at (+) | Vin at (−), feedback to (+) |
| KTU marks weightage | **3–4 marks** | **10–14 marks** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Basic Op-Amp Comparator

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the signal-flow architecture of a basic non-inverting comparator with reference voltage.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSrc1[Vin Input Signal]:::sigNode --> nodeDiff1[Differential Input Stage]:::procNode
    nodeSrc2[Vref Reference DC Source]:::sigNode --> nodeDiff1
    nodeDiff1 --> nodeGain[Open Loop Gain Stage Aol approx 100000]:::ampNode
    nodeGain --> nodeSat[Saturation Output Stage]:::outNode
    nodeSat --> nodeOut1[Vout equals plus Vsat or minus Vsat]:::outNode
    classDef sigNode fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000
    classDef procNode fill:#9FC5E8,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000
    classDef ampNode fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#385723,color:#000
    classDef outNode fill:#F4B084,stroke:#843C0C,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The op-amp's open-loop gain block is what causes the **tiny** millivolt difference between Vin and Vref to be **amplified by ~100,000×**, driving the output hard into saturation. This is why comparators "snap" to ±Vsat so cleanly.

### 4.2 Schematic Block Diagram — Inverting Schmitt Trigger

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn1[Vin applied at inverting input]:::inNode --> nodeOp[Op-Amp uA741]:::ampNode
    nodeRes2[R2 from plus input to ground]:::fbNode --> nodeOp
    nodeRes1[R1 from output to plus input]:::fbNode --> nodeOp
    nodeOp --> nodeOut2[Vout toggles between plus Vsat and minus Vsat]:::outNode
    classDef inNode fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000
    classDef ampNode fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#385723,color:#000
    classDef fbNode fill:#B4C7E7,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000
    classDef outNode fill:#F4B084,stroke:#843C0C,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on the schematic:** R1 and R2 form a **voltage divider** that samples the output voltage and feeds a fraction β·Vout back to the non-inverting input. The non-inverting input acts as a **"smart reference"** that shifts between VUT and VLT depending on the output state.

### 4.3 Transfer Characteristic Block — Hysteresis Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeHP[Input Vin rising past plus VUT]:::trigNode --> nodeStA[Output snaps from plus Vsat DOWN to minus Vsat]:::outNode
    nodeStA --> nodeHN[Input Vin falling past minus VLT]:::trigNode
    nodeHN --> nodeStB[Output snaps from minus Vsat UP to plus Vsat]:::outNode
    nodeStB --> nodeHP
    classDef trigNode fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#7F6000,color:#000
    classDef outNode fill:#F4B084,stroke:#843C0C,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visual Interpretation:** The two arrow loops form a **clockwise rectangle** in the Vout-vs-Vin plane. The **width of the rectangle = V_H (hysteresis voltage)**. This is exactly the Lissajous figure you will see on the CRO in X-Y mode when Vin is a slow triangular wave and Vout is connected to the Y-axis.

### 4.4 Complete Circuit Block Architecture (Lab Wiring View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSigGen[Function Generator SG1 - Triangular or Sine Wave]:::srcNode --> nodeC1[Coupling Capacitor C1 - DC Block]:::inNode
    nodeC1 --> nodeInvInput[Inverting Input Pin 2]:::ampNode
    nodeNonInv[Non Inverting Input Pin 3 with R1 R2 Network]:::fbNode
    nodePSU[DC Power Supply plus 15V minus 15V]:::srcNode --> nodePins[Power Pins 7 and 4]:::ampNode
    nodeOp1[Op-Amp uA741 IC]:::ampNode --> nodeOut3[Output Pin 6]:::outNode
    nodeOut3 --> nodeR1fb[Feedback R1 to Pin 3]:::fbNode
    nodeOut3 --> nodeCRO1[CRO Channel 1 Y axis]:::monNode
    nodeSigGen --> nodeCRO2[CRO Channel 2 X axis]:::monNode
    classDef srcNode fill:#FFD580,stroke:#8B5A00,color:#000
    classDef inNode fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#7F6000,color:#000
    classDef ampNode fill:#C5E0B4,stroke:#385723,color:#000
    classDef fbNode fill:#B4C7E7,stroke:#1F4E79,color:#000
    classDef outNode fill:#F4B084,stroke:#843C0C,color:#000
    classDef monNode fill:#D9D2E9,stroke:#5B2C6F,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Lab Wiring Order (memorize this):**
> 1. **Power ON first** — connect +15 V to Pin 7 and −15 V to Pin 4 of µA741.
> 2. **Connect feedback network** R1 and R2 before applying input.
> 3. **Apply Vin** from the function generator (sine 1 kHz, 5 Vpp).
> 4. **Connect CRO** in X-Y mode only after the circuit is fully wired.
> 5. **Never leave the input floating** — it picks up 50 Hz hum and damages the demonstration.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Define a comparator using an op-amp. What is the role of saturation voltage in determining the output levels?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A comparator is an open-loop op-amp circuit that compares two input voltages, V1 and V2, applied to its inverting and non-inverting terminals. When V+ > V−, the differential input is positive and is amplified by the very high open-loop gain Aol (≈ 2×10⁵ for µA741), driving the output to the **positive saturation voltage (+Vsat)**. When V+ < V−, the output saturates to the **negative saturation voltage (−Vsat)**. For a ±15 V supply, the typical ±Vsat values are ±13.5 V. The saturation voltages define the HIGH and LOW logic levels of the digital output.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is hysteresis in a Schmitt trigger? Why is it essential for noisy input signals?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Hysteresis is the property of a comparator circuit having **two different switching thresholds** — an upper threshold VUT and a lower threshold VLT — depending on the direction of the input signal. The width of this dead-band is **VH = VUT − VLT**. Hysteresis is essential for noisy input signals because, in a basic comparator, noise around the threshold would cause the output to **chatter** (multiple false transitions). In a Schmitt trigger, noise of magnitude less than VH cannot cause a false transition, because the input must first cross the entire hysteresis band to trigger a state change. This makes the Schmitt trigger **immune to small noise spikes** and is used in zero-crossing detectors, relaxation oscillators, and signal conditioning.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Module Internal Choice – Set A]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an **inverting Schmitt trigger** using an op-amp. Derive the expressions for the upper threshold voltage (VUT) and the lower threshold voltage (VLT).
>
> **(b) [7 marks)** Design an inverting Schmitt trigger using a µA741 op-amp powered by ±15 V (assume ±Vsat = ±13.5 V) to obtain VUT = +4.05 V and VLT = −4.05 V. Calculate the hysteresis width. If the input is a 1 kHz sine wave of 8 Vpp, sketch the expected output waveform with proper voltage and time scales.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Theory [7 marks]:**

The inverting Schmitt trigger uses **positive feedback** through resistors R1 and R2 from the output to the non-inverting input. The input signal Vin is applied to the **inverting terminal**.

**Circuit Operation:**
- When Vout = +Vsat, the voltage at the non-inverting terminal is V+ = β·(+Vsat) = +VUT.
- The output will switch to −Vsat only when Vin (at the inverting terminal) rises just above V+ = VUT.
- After switching, Vout = −Vsat, so V+ drops to β·(−Vsat) = VLT.
- Now the output will switch back to +Vsat only when Vin falls below V+ = VLT.

**Derivation [from Section 3.1, abbreviated but complete]:**

Using KCL at the non-inverting node (no current into op-amp input):

$$V_{+} = \frac{R_{2}}{R_{1}+R_{2}} \cdot V_{out} = \beta \cdot V_{out}$$

The switching condition is V+ = V− = Vin. Substituting Vout = +Vsat gives the upper threshold:

$$V_{UT} = \beta \cdot (+V_{sat}) = \frac{R_{2}}{R_{1}+R_{2}} \cdot V_{sat}$$

Substituting Vout = −Vsat gives the lower threshold:

$$V_{LT} = \beta \cdot (-V_{sat}) = -\frac{R_{2}}{R_{1}+R_{2}} \cdot V_{sat}$$

---

**Part (b) — Design Calculation [7 marks]:**

**Step 1:** Find β from the desired VUT.

$$\beta = \frac{V_{UT}}{V_{sat}} = \frac{4.05}{13.5} = 0.3$$

**Step 2:** Choose a convenient R2 and find R1.

Let R2 = 10 kΩ (standard value). Then:

$$0.3 = \frac{10 \text{ k}\Omega}{R_{1} + 10 \text{ k}\Omega}$$

$$R_{1} + 10 \text{ k}\Omega = \frac{10 \text{ k}\Omega}{0.3} = 33.33 \text{ k}\Omega$$

$$R_{1} = 23.33 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 22 \text{ k}\Omega + 1.33 \text{ k}\Omega \text{ (use 22 k}\Omega \text{ standard)}$$

(For exact design, use **R1 = 22 kΩ in series with 1.2 kΩ** to get 23.2 kΩ, or use 22 kΩ as the nearest standard value.)

**Step 3:** Compute hysteresis width.

$$V_{H} = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 4.05 - (-4.05) = 8.10 \text{ V}$$

Or using the formula:

$$V_{H} = 2\beta \cdot V_{sat} = 2 \times 0.3 \times 13.5 = 8.1 \text{ V} \checkmark$$

**Step 4:** Sketching the output waveform for 8 Vpp sine input (1 kHz).

The input sine wave has peak = +4 V and trough = −4 V.
- Since |Vpeak| = 4 V < VUT = 4.05 V, the input **never quite reaches the upper threshold** under ideal conditions.
- This means the output **stays at −Vsat most of the time** and only briefly flips to +Vsat near the peaks.
- The output is a **narrow positive pulse train** synchronized with the sine peaks.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing R1 and R2:** R1 is the **feedback resistor (output to +input)**, R2 is the **ground resistor (+input to GND)**. Swapping them gives a β of 11 instead of 0.0909 — a factor-of-100 error that costs full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign on VLT:** Students often write VLT = +β·Vsat, which is **wrong** by sign. VLT is **always negative** for a symmetric inverting Schmitt trigger.
> 3. **Using ±15 V instead of ±Vsat in calculations:** The op-amp output saturates at ±13.5 V, not ±15 V. Using 15 V inflates VUT and VLT by ~11%.
> 4. **Forgetting the switching condition:** The trip point is **V+ = V−**, not V− = 0. Several students lose 2 marks by skipping this fundamental step.

---

> **Q3B. [KTU University Exam – Module Internal Choice – Set B]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Compare and contrast a basic op-amp comparator and a Schmitt trigger. Explain with transfer characteristics how hysteresis eliminates multiple transitions in a noisy input.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** In a Schmitt trigger circuit, R1 = 100 kΩ, R2 = 10 kΩ, and the op-amp is powered by ±15 V with ±Vsat = ±13.5 V. Calculate (i) feedback fraction β, (ii) VUT, (iii) VLT, and (iv) hysteresis voltage VH. If this circuit is converted into a relaxation oscillator by adding R = 10 kΩ and C = 0.1 µF, find the frequency of oscillation.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Comparison [7 marks]:**

| Feature | Basic Comparator | Schmitt Trigger |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback | Open loop (no feedback) | Positive feedback through R1, R2 |
| Threshold | Single threshold (Vref) | Two thresholds (VUT, VLT) |
| Transfer characteristic | A single switching line at Vref | A rectangular hysteresis loop |
| Noise immunity | Poor (output chatters near Vref) | Excellent (immune to noise < VH/2) |
| Output transitions | Slow, dependent on op-amp slew rate | Fast, regenerative snap-action |
| Use cases | ADC, zero-crossing (clean input) | Oscillators, signal conditioning, line receivers |

**Explanation of hysteresis effect on noisy input:**
When a basic comparator receives a noisy input around Vref, the noise causes the input to cross Vref multiple times, producing a **chattering output**. In a Schmitt trigger with hysteresis band VH, the input must first cross VUT (when rising) to flip the output, and then must fall all the way below VLT to flip it back. Any noise of magnitude **< VH/2** is **filtered out** because it cannot drive the input across the required threshold. Hence the output transitions are **clean, single, and debounced**.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical [7 marks]:**

**Given:** R1 = 100 kΩ, R2 = 10 kΩ, ±Vsat = ±13.5 V, R = 10 kΩ, C = 0.1 µF.

**Step 1: Feedback fraction β.**

$$\beta = \frac{R_{2}}{R_{1} + R_{2}} = \frac{10 \text{ k}\Omega}{100 \text{ k}\Omega + 10 \text{ k}\Omega} = \frac{10}{110} = \frac{1}{11} \approx 0.0909$$

**Step 2: VUT.**

$$V_{UT} = \beta \cdot (+V_{sat}) = \frac{1}{11} \times 13.5 = +1.227 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3: VLT.**

$$V_{LT} = \beta \cdot (-V_{sat}) = \frac{1}{11} \times (-13.5) = -1.227 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4: Hysteresis voltage VH.**

$$V_{H} = V_{UT} - V_{LT} = 1.227 - (-1.227) = 2.454 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5: Relaxation oscillator frequency.**

Using the KTU formula:

$$f = \frac{1}{2RC \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}$$

Compute the argument of the logarithm:

$$\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta} = \frac{1 + 0.0909}{1 - 0.0909} = \frac{1.0909}{0.9091} = 1.2$$

Compute ln(1.2):

$$\ln(1.2) \approx 0.1823$$

Compute 2RC:

$$2RC = 2 \times 10 \text{ k}\Omega \times 0.1 \text{ }\mu\text{F} = 2 \times 10^{4} \times 10^{-7} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2 \text{ ms}$$

Therefore:

$$f = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1823} = \frac{1}{3.646 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 2742.6 \text{ Hz} \approx 2.74 \text{ kHz}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes on This Question:**
> 1. **Forgetting to compute the logarithm:** Many students stop at $f = 1/(2RC)$, getting $f = 500$ Hz. The correct answer is ~2.74 kHz, more than 5× higher. The hysteresis band makes the capacitor charge faster than in a simple RC.
> 2. **Using natural log vs. log base 10:** Always use **ln** in this formula. Using log₁₀ introduces a factor of 2.303 error.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in RC:** Ensure R is in **ohms** and C is in **farads**, not kΩ and µF. Or carefully convert: 10 kΩ = 10⁴ Ω, 0.1 µF = 10⁻⁷ F.
> 4. **Treating β as a ratio of R1 to R2:** This is the **opposite** of correct. The β formula is R2/(R1+R2), not R1/R2.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Read this aloud twice before entering the exam hall.**

**✅ Core Concepts:**
- [ ] A **basic comparator** is an open-loop op-amp with a **single threshold**.
- [ ] A **Schmitt trigger** is a comparator with **positive feedback** and **two thresholds (VUT, VLT)**.
- [ ] The switching condition is always **V+ = V−** (zero differential input).
- [ ] The **hysteresis width VH = VUT − VLT** defines the noise-immunity band.
- [ ] For µA741 on ±15 V, the **saturation voltage is ±13.5 V** (not ±15 V).
- [ ] In an **inverting Schmitt trigger**, the input is at the **inverting terminal**; feedback goes to the **non-inverting terminal**.

**✅ Key Formulas (Memorize These):**
- [ ] $\beta = R_{2}/(R_{1} + R_{2})$ — feedback fraction
- [ ] $V_{UT} = +\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ — upper threshold
- [ ] $V_{LT} = -\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ — lower threshold
- [ ] $V_{H} = 2\beta \cdot V_{sat}$ — hysteresis width
- [ ] $f = 1/[2RC \cdot \ln((1+\beta)/(1-\beta))]$ — relaxation oscillator frequency

**✅ Lab Procedure Checklist:**
- [ ] Power ON the op-amp **before** connecting the input signal.
- [ ] Use a **1 kHz sine or triangular wave** (5–10 Vpp) from the function generator.
- [ ] Connect the CRO in **X-Y mode** to see the hysteresis loop directly.
- [ ] For a **time-domain view**, use **dual-trace mode** (Ch1: input, Ch2: output).
- [ ] Measure VUT, VLT, VH using the **CRO's cursor function** or graticule.

**✅ Common KTU Mistakes to Avoid:**
- [ ] Using ±15 V instead of ±Vsat = ±13.5 V in formulas.
- [ ] Swapping R1 and R2 in the feedback network.
- [ ] Forgetting the **negative sign** in VLT.
- [ ] Treating the Schmitt trigger as an oscillator without an RC network.
- [ ] Confusing the **transfer characteristic** (Vout vs Vin) with the **time-domain waveform**.

**✅ Engineering Intuition:**
- [ ] Comparator = **single-threshold** device. Schmitt = **dual-threshold** device.
- [ ] Hysteresis = **dead-band** that swallows noise spikes.
- [ ] Regenerative feedback = **snap action** that makes switching faster.
- [ ] Schmitt + RC = **relaxation oscillator** (the heart of every function generator).
- [ ] The µA741 is **not a rail-to-rail** op-amp — it cannot swing to ±15 V; only to ±Vsat.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waveform Generation - Square, Triangular and Saw Tooth Waveform Generation Using OPAMPs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL307 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Waveform Generation - Square, Triangular and Saw Tooth Waveform Generation Using OPAMPs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **function generator** is an electronic circuit that produces repetitive, non-sinusoidal waveforms such as **square, triangular, and sawtooth waves** using operational amplifiers (op-amps) as the active building blocks. The square wave is generated by an **astable multivibrator** (a regenerative comparator with RC timing), the **triangular wave** is derived by integrating the square wave through an **active integrator** (Miller integrator), and the **sawtooth wave** is produced by modifying the integrator to allow **asymmetric charge-discharge cycles** (unidirectional charging with rapid reset).

The frequency of operation is governed by the **RC time constant** and the **feedback fraction (β)** of the resistive divider, while the amplitude is bounded by the op-amp **saturation voltage (V_sat)**, nominally **±13.5 V** for a standard 741 op-amp powered by **±15 V** rails.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment belongs to the core analog portion of the lab and tests Course Outcome **CO2**: *Design and verify analog electronic circuits using op-amps*. Students must be able to plot the waveforms on a CRO/DSO, measure **amplitude, time period, and frequency**, and compare theoretical versus practical values.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **see-saw (teeter-totter)** in a playground:
- The **square wave generator** is like the see-saw itself — it suddenly flips from one extreme position to the other and stays there briefly, like a child kicking their legs alternately.
- The **triangular wave** is like watching the **height of the centre of the see-saw board** as it smoothly moves from one tilted extreme to the other — a slow, linear rise, then a slow, linear fall.
- The **sawtooth wave** is like a **water tank that fills slowly but empties instantly** through a wide pipe — a long, slow rise followed by a sudden drop. This sudden drop is called the **flyback** or **retrace**.

The op-amp's role is that of a **decision-maker (comparator)** that constantly watches a slowly rising/falling voltage and decides when to flip the output — much like a referee who blows the whistle when a runner reaches a marked line.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Golden Rules of an Ideal Op-Amp used in waveform generation:**
> 1. **Open-loop gain A → ∞** (comparator switches instantly).
> 2. **Input impedance Z_in → ∞** (no current into ± inputs).
> 3. **Output saturates at ±V_sat** when overdriven (used as a voltage limiter).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Op-amp supply rails (V_CC, -V_EE) | **+15, -15** | V |
| Output saturation voltage (±V_sat) | **±13.5** | V |
| Resistor range (R) | **1 kΩ to 1 MΩ** | Ω |
| Capacitor range (C) | **0.01 μF to 10 μF** | F |
| Frequency range of operation | **10 Hz to 100 kHz** | Hz |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Square Wave Generator (Astable Multivibrator using Op-Amp)

The square wave generator uses a **single op-amp configured as a comparator with positive feedback** through a resistive divider (R_1, R_2) and an RC timing network (R, C) connected to the inverting input.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

- **Step 1 — Initial State:** Assume the output saturates at +V_sat. The non-inverting input (V_+) is held at a fraction of V_sat:
$$V_+ = +\beta V_{sat} = +\frac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}V_{sat}$$

- **Step 2 — Capacitor Charging:** The capacitor C charges towards +V_sat through resistor R. As long as the inverting input voltage V_- < V_+, the output remains at +V_sat.

- **Step 3 — Threshold Crossing:** The instant V_- crosses V_+, the comparator flips. The output jumps from +V_sat to -V_sat.

- **Step 4 — Reverse Charging:** Now V_+ becomes -βV_sat, and the capacitor starts discharging from +βV_sat towards -V_sat.

- **Step 5 — Cycle Repeats:** When V_- reaches -βV_sat, the output flips back to +V_sat, completing one full cycle.

### 2.2 Triangular Wave Generator (Square Wave + Integrator)

A triangular wave is obtained by feeding the **square wave** output of an astable multivibrator into the input of a **Miller integrator** (inverting integrator with feedback capacitor C).

**Why an Integrator?**
The output of an integrator is the **negative integral of its input**. A constant input (either +V_sat or -V_sat) produces a **linear ramp**. Hence, a square wave input (constant +V_sat for half period, -V_sat for the other half) produces a **triangular wave** at the integrator output.

The amplitude of the triangular wave is:
$$V_{tri,pk} = \frac{V_{sat}}{4 f R C}$$

where f is the frequency of the input square wave.

### 2.3 Sawtooth Wave Generator (Modified Integrator)

The sawtooth wave is produced by making the **charging and discharging rates unequal**:
- A **slow charging path** through a large resistor R.
- A **fast discharge path** through a transistor switch (or a diode) that activates when the integrator output reaches a threshold.

This gives a **gradual rise** followed by an **abrupt fall**, producing the characteristic sawtooth shape. The **flyback time** is much shorter than the **sweep time**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Waveform | Key Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Square | $\beta = \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$ | Feedback fraction (threshold ratio) |
| 2 | Square | $T = 2RC \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)$ | Total time period of oscillation |
| 3 | Square | $f = \dfrac{1}{2RC \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}$ | Frequency of oscillation |
| 4 | Square | $T = 2.2\,RC$ | Special case when $R_1 = R_2$ (i.e. β = 0.5) |
| 5 | Triangular | $V_{o,pk} = \dfrac{V_{sat}}{4 f R_3 C}$ | Peak amplitude of triangular wave |
| 6 | Triangular | $f = \dfrac{R_1}{4 R_2 R_3 C}$ | Frequency (two-op-amp topology) |
| 7 | Triangular | $T = \dfrac{4 R_2 R_3 C}{R_1}$ | Time period (two-op-amp topology) |
| 8 | Sawtooth | $T_{sweep} = \dfrac{V_{pk} \, R_{charge} \, C}{V_{ref}}$ | Slow rising (sweep) period |
| 9 | Sawtooth | $T_{flyback} = \dfrac{V_{pk} \, R_{discharge} \, C}{V_{ref}}$ | Rapid falling (flyback) period |
| 10 | Sawtooth | $f = \dfrac{1}{T_{sweep}+T_{flyback}}$ | Net sawtooth frequency |
| 11 | Op-amp | $V_{sat} \approx V_{CC} - 1.5$ | Practical saturation voltage |
| 12 | Integrator | $V_o(t) = -\dfrac{1}{R C}\!\int V_{in}\,dt$ | Ideal integrator output |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL EXAM TIP:** For KTU viva, always state that the op-amp works in the **non-linear (saturation) region** for square wave generation but in the **linear region** for the integrator stage. Many students lose marks by claiming the op-amp is "linear" throughout the entire function generator.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Waveform Used | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **CRO / DSO Time-base** | Sawtooth | Linear horizontal sweep of the electron beam |
| **PWM Motor Control** | Square + Sawtooth | Compare to generate variable duty-cycle pulses |
| **Audio Synthesis** | All three | Build musical tones and electronic instruments |
| **ADC (Dual-Slope)** | Sawtooth + Square | Used in digital voltmeters and multimeters |
| **Function Generators (lab equipment)** | All three | HP/Keysight 33xxx series, Tektronix AFG series |
| **PLL / VCO Circuits** | Triangular | Linear voltage-to-frequency conversion |
| **TV / Oscilloscope Deflection** | Sawtooth | Horizontal and vertical deflection |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Square Wave Time Period

**Given:** Op-amp astable multivibrator with output saturating at ±V_sat, feedback resistors R_1 (from V_o to V_+) and R_2 (from V_+ to ground), and timing network R, C (V_- node).

**Goal:** Derive the expression for the time period T.

**Step 1 — Expression for the non-inverting input voltage V_+:**

Using the superposition principle (no current flows into the + input), the voltage at V_+ is set by the resistive divider formed by R_1 and R_2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_+ &= V_o \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the feedback fraction β:**

Let us define the **feedback fraction β** as the ratio of the voltage fed back to V_+:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence V_+ = β · V_o. When the output is at +V_sat, V_+ = +βV_sat. When output is at -V_sat, V_+ = -βV_sat.

**Step 3 — Write the capacitor charging equation:**

Assume the output just flipped to +V_sat at t = 0. The capacitor C charges from its previous value of -βV_sat towards +V_sat through resistor R. The voltage across the capacitor is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_C(t) &= V_{final} + (V_{initial} - V_{final})\,e^{-t/RC} \\
V_C(t) &= V_{sat} + (- \beta V_{sat} - V_{sat})\,e^{-t/RC} \\
V_C(t) &= V_{sat} - V_{sat}(1+\beta)\,e^{-t/RC}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Find the time T_1 for the first half-cycle (capacitor to reach +βV_sat):**

The comparator switches when V_C(t) = +βV_sat. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta V_{sat} &= V_{sat} - V_{sat}(1+\beta)\,e^{-T_1/RC} \\
\beta V_{sat} - V_{sat} &= -V_{sat}(1+\beta)\,e^{-T_1/RC} \\
- V_{sat}(1-\beta) &= -V_{sat}(1+\beta)\,e^{-T_1/RC} \\
(1-\beta) &= (1+\beta)\,e^{-T_1/RC} \\
e^{-T_1/RC} &= \frac{1-\beta}{1+\beta}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Take natural logarithm to solve for T_1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{T_1}{RC} &= \ln\!\left(\frac{1-\beta}{1+\beta}\right) \\
T_1 &= RC \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Use symmetry for the second half-cycle T_2:**

By the symmetry of the circuit, the time taken for V_C to swing from +βV_sat down to -βV_sat (when output is at -V_sat) is **exactly the same as T_1**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= RC \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Total time period of square wave:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= T_1 + T_2 = 2 RC \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Frequency of oscillation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{2 RC \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 9 — Special case for symmetric square wave (50% duty cycle):**

Choose R_1 = R_2, so β = 0.5. Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 2 RC \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{1+0.5}{1-0.5}\right) = 2 RC \cdot \ln(3) \approx 2 RC \cdot 1.0986 \\
T &\approx 2.197 RC \approx 2.2 RC
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the famous **T = 2.2 RC** result cited in most KTU textbooks.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Triangular Wave Amplitude

**Given:** A two-op-amp triangular wave generator. Op-amp A1 is a comparator (Schmitt trigger) producing a square wave V_sq, and Op-amp A2 is an inverting integrator producing the triangular wave V_tri. Resistor R_3 and capacitor C form the integrator. R_1 and R_2 set the comparator threshold.

**Goal:** Derive the peak amplitude of the triangular wave.

**Step 1 — Output of the comparator:**

The comparator output is V_sq = ±V_sat (square wave at the same frequency as the astable action).

**Step 2 — Output of the integrator:**

For an ideal inverting integrator with input V_sq:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tri}(t) &= -\frac{1}{R_3 C}\int V_{sq}(t)\,dt
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — During the first half-period (V_sq = +V_sat):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tri}(t) &= -\frac{V_{sat}}{R_3 C}\cdot t + V_{tri}(0)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **linearly decreasing ramp** with slope = -V_sat/(R_3 C).

**Step 4 — The ramp continues until V_tri reaches the negative threshold of the comparator:**

The threshold of the comparator at the V_+ input, when V_sq is at +V_sat, is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tri,pk} &= \beta \cdot V_{sat} = \frac{R_2}{R_1} \cdot V_{sat}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Time to reach the threshold (half-period):**

Setting V_tri(t) = -V_tri,pk and solving for t = T/2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\beta V_{sat} &= -\frac{V_{sat}}{R_3 C}\cdot\frac{T}{2} + \beta V_{sat} \\
\frac{T}{2} &= \frac{2 \beta R_3 C V_{sat}}{V_{sat}} = 2 \beta R_3 C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Express the time period in terms of the components:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 4 \beta R_3 C = \frac{4 R_2 R_3 C}{R_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Frequency of triangular wave:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{R_1}{4 R_2 R_3 C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Peak amplitude (final form):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tri,pk} = \beta V_{sat} = \frac{R_2}{R_1} V_{sat}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows that the **amplitude of the triangular wave is independent of the integrating capacitor C and is set only by the resistor ratio R_2/R_1 and the saturation voltage V_sat**.

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation of Sawtooth Wave Time Period

**Given:** Modified integrator where R_charge is the slow charging resistance, R_discharge << R_charge is the rapid discharge path via a transistor switch.

**Step 1 — Charging phase (slow rise):**

The capacitor charges from 0 V towards the reference voltage V_ref with a time constant R_charge · C. The voltage at time t is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_C(t) &= V_{ref}\!\left(1 - e^{-t/(R_{charge}\,C)}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Time to reach the trigger voltage V_pk:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{pk} &= V_{ref}\!\left(1 - e^{-T_{sweep}/(R_{charge}\,C)}\right) \\
e^{-T_{sweep}/(R_{charge}\,C)} &= 1 - \frac{V_{pk}}{V_{ref}} \\
T_{sweep} &= R_{charge}\,C \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{V_{ref}}{V_{ref}-V_{pk}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Discharge phase (rapid fall):**

After triggering, the transistor switch connects R_discharge across the capacitor, discharging it rapidly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{flyback} &\approx 3 R_{discharge} C \quad \text{(for 95\% discharge)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Net time period:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T = T_{sweep} + T_{flyback}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since R_discharge << R_charge, we have T_flyback << T_sweep, producing the characteristic **sawtooth shape** (slow rise, sharp fall).

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Square Wave | Triangular Wave | Sawtooth Wave |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Active building block** | 1 op-amp (comparator) | 2 op-amps (comparator + integrator) | 1 op-amp + transistor switch |
| **Op-amp region of operation** | Non-linear (saturated) | Comparator non-linear, integrator linear | Non-linear (saturated) |
| **Shape characteristics** | Equal positive/negative halves | Symmetric linear rise/fall | Long slow rise, short fast fall |
| **Duty cycle (ideal)** | 50% | 50% (symmetric) | < 10% (long sweep) |
| **Slope of rising edge** | Infinite (ideal) | Constant = -V_sat/(RC) | Constant = V_ref/(R_charge C) |
| **Slope of falling edge** | Infinite (ideal) | Constant = +V_sat/(RC) | Very steep (R_discharge small) |
| **Typical frequency** | 1 Hz to 100 kHz | 100 Hz to 10 kHz | 50 Hz to 1 kHz |
| **THD (harmonic content)** | High (odd harmonics ~1/n) | Moderate (odd harmonics ~1/n²) | Very high (all harmonics ~1/n) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Square Wave Generator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["DC Supply ±15V"] --> B1["Op-Amp 741 Comparator"]
    B1 --> C1["Output Square Wave ±Vsat"]
    C1 --> D1["R1 Resistor"]
    C1 --> E1["R2 Resistor"]
    D1 --> F1["V+ Node Positive Feedback"]
    E1 --> F1
    F1 --> B1
    C1 --> G1["R Timing Resistor"]
    G1 --> H1["C Capacitor to V-"]
    H1 --> I1["V- Node Negative Input"]
    I1 --> B1
    C1 --> J1["CRO or DSO Display"]
    J1 --> K1["Square Wave Trace"]
    C1 --> L1["Frequency Measurement f = 1/T"]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Triangular Wave Generator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2["DC Supply ±15V"] --> B2["Op-Amp 1 Comparator"]
    B2 --> C2["Square Wave Output ±Vsat"]
    C2 --> D2["R3 Input Resistor"]
    D2 --> E2["Op-Amp 2 Inverting Integrator"]
    F2["C Feedback Capacitor"] --> E2
    E2 --> G2["Triangular Wave Output"]
    C2 --> H2["R1 Resistor"]
    C2 --> I2["R2 Resistor"]
    H2 --> J2["V+ Node of Comparator"]
    I2 --> J2
    J2 --> B2
    G2 --> K2["CRO or DSO Display"]
    K2 --> L2["Triangular Wave Trace"]
    G2 --> M2["Amplitude = R2/R1 times Vsat"]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Sawtooth Wave Generator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A3["DC Supply ±15V"] --> B3["Op-Amp Comparator"]
    B3 --> C3["Output Drives Q1 Transistor Switch"]
    C3 --> D3["R_charge Large Resistance Slow Path"]
    D3 --> E3["C Capacitor Voltage Rises Linearly"]
    E3 --> F3["V_in- Node to Comparator"]
    F3 --> B3
    C3 --> G3["R_discharge Small Resistance Fast Path"]
    G3 --> E3
    E3 --> H3["Sawtooth Waveform Output"]
    H3 --> I3["CRO Sweep Display"]
    I3 --> J3["Slow Rise Then Sharp Fall"]
    H3 --> K3["Time Period T = Tsweep plus Tflyback"]
```
