## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define an astable multivibrator. Why is it called "astable"?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An astable multivibrator is a regenerative electronic circuit that has **no stable state** and continuously switches between two quasi-stable states, producing a square wave at the output. It is called "astable" (a- = "no", stable = "stable state") because **neither output state is permanent**; the circuit keeps oscillating on its own without any external trigger once power is applied. The op-amp version uses positive feedback through a resistive divider (R_1, R_2) and an RC timing network, and the time period is given by:
$$T = 2RC\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)$$

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Why is a triangular wave obtained by integrating a square wave?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The output of an ideal inverting integrator is the **negative time-integral of its input voltage**, scaled by 1/(RC). A square wave has two constant voltage levels (+V_sat and -V_sat). Integrating a **constant positive voltage** over half a period produces a **linearly decreasing ramp**, and integrating a **constant negative voltage** over the next half period produces a **linearly increasing ramp**. The two ramps together form a **triangular wave** whose peak amplitude is:
$$V_{tri,pk} = \beta V_{sat} = \frac{R_2}{R_1}V_{sat}$$

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

**Q3. (a)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(7 Marks)**
**With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an op-amp based square wave generator. Derive the expression for its time period and frequency.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Circuit Description (2 Marks):**
The square wave generator uses a single 741 op-amp with the non-inverting input (+) connected to a voltage divider formed by R_1 (between V_o and V_+) and R_2 (between V_+ and ground). The inverting input (-) is connected to the junction of R and C, where R is connected to V_o and C is connected to ground.

**Working (3 Marks):**
- The op-amp acts as a comparator with positive feedback. Its output saturates at +V_sat or -V_sat.
- The voltage at V_+ is βV_o = ±βV_sat, where β = R_2/(R_1 + R_2).
- The capacitor C charges towards V_o through R. When V_C crosses V_+, the output flips, and C starts charging in the opposite direction. This produces a **continuous square wave** at the output.

**Derivation (2 Marks):**
$$T = 2RC\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right), \quad f = \frac{1}{2RC\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)}$$

For the special case R_1 = R_2 (β = 0.5), T ≈ 2.2 RC.

**Q3. (b)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(7 Marks)**
**For the circuit in part (a), R_1 = R_2 = 10 kΩ, R = 10 kΩ, C = 0.1 μF, and V_sat = ±13.5 V. Calculate the time period, frequency, and peak-to-peak output voltage.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- R_1 = R_2 = 10 kΩ, R = 10 kΩ, C = 0.1 μF, V_sat = ±13.5 V

**Step 1 — Calculate β (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\beta &= \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{10\,k\Omega}{10\,k\Omega + 10\,k\Omega} = 0.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Time period (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 2RC\,\ln\!\left(\frac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right) = 2 \times 10 \times 10^{3} \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} \times \ln\!\left(\frac{1.5}{0.5}\right) \\
T &= 2 \times 10^{-3} \times \ln(3) = 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.0986 = 2.197\,ms
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Frequency (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{2.197 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 455\,Hz
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Peak-to-peak output (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{o,pk\text{-}pk} &= 2 V_{sat} = 2 \times 13.5 = 27\,V
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Q4. (a)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(7 Marks)**
**Explain with a circuit diagram, the generation of a triangular wave using two op-amps. Derive the expression for its frequency and amplitude.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Circuit Description (2 Marks):**
The circuit uses op-amp A1 as a **Schmitt trigger (regenerative comparator)** with positive feedback through R_1 and R_2. Op-amp A2 is configured as a **Miller integrator** with input resistor R_3 and feedback capacitor C. The output of the integrator is fed back to the inverting input of A1, while the output of A1 drives the integrator input.

**Working (3 Marks):**
- A1 produces a square wave V_sq = ±V_sat. This square wave is applied to the integrator A2.
- A2 integrates V_sq to produce a triangular wave V_tri. When V_sq = +V_sat, V_tri ramps down linearly; when V_sq = -V_sat, V_tri ramps up linearly.
- V_tri is fed back to A1, and whenever V_tri reaches the threshold ±βV_sat, A1 flips, producing continuous oscillation.

**Derivation (2 Marks):**
$$V_{tri,pk} = \frac{R_2}{R_1}V_{sat}, \quad f = \frac{R_1}{4 R_2 R_3 C}$$

**Q4. (b)** [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(7 Marks)**
**For the triangular wave generator of part (a), R_1 = 20 kΩ, R_2 = 10 kΩ, R_3 = 10 kΩ, C = 0.05 μF, V_sat = ±13.5 V. Calculate the frequency, peak amplitude, and time period of the triangular wave.**

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- R_1 = 20 kΩ, R_2 = 10 kΩ, R_3 = 10 kΩ, C = 0.05 μF, V_sat = ±13.5 V

**Step 1 — Calculate frequency (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{R_1}{4 R_2 R_3 C} = \frac{20 \times 10^{3}}{4 \times 10 \times 10^{3} \times 10 \times 10^{3} \times 0.05 \times 10^{-6}} \\
f &= \frac{20\,000}{4 \times 10^{4} \times 10^{4} \times 0.05 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{20\,000}{20} = 1000\,Hz = 1\,kHz
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Time period (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1\,ms
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Peak amplitude (3 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{tri,pk} &= \frac{R_2}{R_1} \times V_{sat} = \frac{10\,k\Omega}{20\,k\Omega} \times 13.5\,V \\
V_{tri,pk} &= 0.5 \times 13.5 = 6.75\,V
\end{aligned}
$$

Peak-to-peak amplitude: V_tri,pk-pk = 2 × 6.75 = **13.5 V**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the logarithm:** Many students write T = 2RC for a square wave. This is **only true** when R_1 = R_2 (β = 0.5). For arbitrary resistors, the full logarithm expression must be used. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing integration output polarity:** A Miller integrator is **inverting**. So +V_sat input gives a **negative-going** ramp. Some students draw the triangular wave in phase with the square wave, which is wrong. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Wrong unit conversions:** C = 0.1 μF = 0.1 × 10⁻⁶ F. Forgetting this gives 1000× error in f. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 4. **Not specifying V_sat:** KTU expects you to use the given V_sat (not V_CC = 15 V) in all calculations. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 5. **Sawtooth = triangular wave:** These are different! Sawtooth has a fast flyback; triangular is symmetric. Examiners specifically test this distinction. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 6. **Forgetting feedback fraction β:** Several students use R_1/R_2 in place of R_2/(R_1 + R_2) for β. **Loss: 1 mark.**

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Square wave generator** uses **one op-amp** as a **regenerative comparator with RC feedback**; output alternates between **±V_sat** = **±13.5 V**.
- [ ] **Feedback fraction** β = R_2/(R_1 + R_2) sets the **threshold levels** at ±βV_sat.
- [ ] **Time period** of square wave: T = 2RC · ln[(1+β)/(1-β)]; **symmetric case** (R_1 = R_2): T ≈ 2.2 RC.
- [ ] **Triangular wave generator** requires **two op-amps** — a **comparator** (Schmitt trigger) feeding an **inverting integrator**.
- [ ] **Triangular frequency** f = R_1/(4 R_2 R_3 C); **amplitude** V_tri,pk = (R_2/R_1) V_sat.
- [ ] **Sawtooth wave** is produced by **asymmetric integrator** — slow charge (R_charge large) + fast discharge (R_discharge small + transistor switch).
- [ ] **Sawtooth has flyback** — short, sharp falling edge; the rest of the period is a slow rising ramp.
- [ ] **Op-amp saturation voltage** V_sat ≈ V_CC − 1.5 V (for 741 powered by ±15 V, V_sat = ±13.5 V).
- [ ] **CRO/DSO setup:** Set vertical scale to 5 V/div, time-base to 0.5 ms/div (typical), trigger on rising edge, AC coupling.
- [ ] **Percentage error** = (|Theoretical − Practical| / Theoretical) × 100%; report this in the lab record.
- [ ] **Component tolerance:** Resistors ±5%, capacitors ±10% — these contribute to deviation from theoretical frequency.
- [ ] **KTU CO mapping:** This experiment maps to **CO2** (Design analog circuits using op-amps) and **CO4** (Use modern tools like CRO/DSO for measurement).
- [ ] **Viva essentials:** Be ready to explain the **role of each resistor and capacitor**, the **difference between the comparator and integrator op-amp stages**, and **why the op-amp is saturated** in the square wave stage.
- [ ] **Common components in lab kit:** 741 op-amp, resistors 1 kΩ to 100 kΩ (¼ W), capacitors 0.01 μF to 1 μF (ceramic/polyester), breadboard, ±15 V dual power supply, CRO/DSO with probes.
- [ ] **Safety reminder:** Always connect the op-amp power supply **before** applying input signals; never exceed ±18 V on the supply pins.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Astable and Monostable Circuit Using 555 Timer IC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEOL307 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Astable and Monostable Circuit Using 555 Timer IC # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **NE555 Timer IC** is a highly stable, monolithic integrated circuit capable of producing accurate time delays or oscillation. It is an 8-pin device that can be configured in three primary modes: **Monostable (one-shot)**, **Astable (free-running oscillator)**, and **Bistable (flip-flop)**. For the KTU 2024 scheme (PCEOL307), the primary focus is the practical implementation and theoretical analysis of the **Astable Multivibrator** and **Monostable Multivibrator** configurations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pin Configuration of 555 Timer (DIP-8 Package):** > - **Pin 1:** Ground (GND) > - **Pin 2:** Trigger (Active LOW input, threshold < Vcc/3) > - **Pin 3:** Output (Drives up to **200 mA** load) > - **Pin 4:** Reset (Active LOW master reset) > - **Pin 5:** Control Voltage (Bypassed to ground via 0.01 µF capacitor) > - **Pin 6:** Threshold (Active HIGH input, threshold > 2Vcc/3) > - **Pin 7:** Discharge (Open-collector transistor to GND) > - **Pin 8:** Supply Voltage (**+5 V to +15 V** standard, absolute max **+18 V**) ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (The "Water Bucket" Analogy) Imagine a water bucket with a small hole at the bottom and a tap above it. The tap fills the bucket (analogous to **charging a capacitor** through a resistor). The bucket has two water-level sensors: a "LOW" sensor (Pin 2 - Trigger, set at **1/3 Vcc**) and a "HIGH" sensor (Pin 6 - Threshold, set at **2/3 Vcc**). - **Astable Mode (Self-Flipping Bucket):** The tap is always ON, but as soon as the water hits the HIGH sensor, a valve opens at the bottom and drains water rapidly. The moment the water drops below the LOW sensor, the valve closes and the tap refills it. This endless cycle produces a **continuous square wave**. - **Monostable Mode (One-Shot Bucket):** The bucket is normally empty (stable LOW state). When you press a button (apply a negative trigger pulse), the tap turns ON for a fixed duration. The water rises until it hits the HIGH sensor, then automatically shuts off. The system then returns to its empty (LOW) stable state, waiting for the next button press. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always specify the operating supply voltage (typically **+5 V** for digital logic compatibility or **+9 V / +12 V** for analog timing applications) in your circuit diagrams for full marks. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Internal Block Diagram Analysis The 555 IC consists of four critical functional blocks: 1. **Voltage Divider Network:** Three internal **5 kΩ** resistors create two precise reference voltages: - **V_ref1 = (1/3) × Vcc** → fed to the inverting input of the Trigger Comparator (Comparator 1). - **V_ref2 = (2/3) × Vcc** → fed to the non-inverting input of the Threshold Comparator (Comparator 2). - *The name "555" derives from these three 5 kΩ resistors in series.* 2. **Two Comparators (Op-amps):** - **Trigger Comparator:** Compares Pin 2 voltage with (1/3)Vcc. - **Threshold Comparator:** Compares Pin 6 voltage with (2/3)Vcc. 3. **SR Flip-Flop (Latch):** Stores the state based on comparator outputs. R is the Threshold comparator output, S is the Trigger comparator output. 4. **Discharge Transistor (NPN, Open Collector at Pin 7):** When the flip-flop is RESET, Pin 7 is connected to ground (transistor ON), allowing external capacitor discharge. ## 2.2 Operating Logic Table | Trigger (Pin 2) | Threshold (Pin 6) | Reset (Pin 4) | Output (Pin 3) | Discharge (Pin 7) | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | < Vcc/3 | Any | HIGH | **HIGH** | OFF (Open) | | > Vcc/3 | > 2Vcc/3 | HIGH | **LOW** | ON (Connected to GND) | | Any | Any | **LOW** | **LOW** | ON (Connected to GND) | ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **All formulas below are board-exam essentials. Memorize units: R in Ω (or kΩ), C in F (or µF/nF) → Time in seconds.** | Parameter | Astable Multivibrator | Monostable Multivibrator | |:---|:---|:---| | **Charging Time (tH)** | $t_H = 0.693 \times (R_1 + R_2) \times C$ | Pulse Width: $T = 1.1 \times R \times C$ | | **Discharging Time (tL)** | $t_L = 0.693 \times R_2 \times C$ | N/A (Output LOW is stable state) | | **Total Time Period (T)** | $T = t_H + t_L = 0.693 \times (R_1 + 2R_2) \times C$ | $T = 1.1 \times R \times C$ | | **Frequency (f)** | $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \times C}$ | N/A (Single pulse) | | **Duty Cycle (D)** | $D = \dfrac{(R_1 + R_2)}{(R_1 + 2R_2)} \times 100\%$ | 100% (within the pulse) | | **Boundary Condition** | $D > 50\%$ always (without diode trick) | $T \geq 10\ \mu s$ (monostable constraint) | > [!NOTE] > **Note on Duty Cycle:** The standard astable circuit cannot produce a duty cycle below 50% because both R1 and R2 are in the charging path, but only R2 is in the discharging path. To achieve **D < 50%**, a bypass diode is added in parallel with R2 to allow charging through R1 only. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Astable 555 Applications:** LED flashers, pulse/square wave generators, clock generators for digital logic, PWM modulators, tone generators in toys/alarms. - **Monostable 555 Applications:** Pulse stretching for narrow pulses, debouncing mechanical switches, missing pulse detectors, time-delay relays, sequential timing circuits. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Astable Multivibrator Frequency (Charging Cycle) When the output is **HIGH**, the discharge transistor (Pin 7) is **OFF** (open circuit). The capacitor $C$ charges through the series combination of $R_1$ and $R_2$ from supply $V_{cc}$. **Step 1:** The voltage across the capacitor follows the standard RC charging equation for a capacitor charging from initial voltage $V_i$ to final voltage $V_{cc}$: $$ V_C(t) = V_{cc} - (V_{cc} - V_i) \cdot e^{-t / \tau} $$ **Step 2:** Identify the time constant $\tau$. Since $R_1$ and $R_2$ are in series during charging: $$ \tau_{charge} = (R_1 + R_2) \cdot C $$ **Step 3:** Apply the boundary conditions. The capacitor starts charging from $V_i = \dfrac{V_{cc}}{3}$ (because it just discharged to that level) and stops at $V_f = \dfrac{2V_{cc}}{3}$ (threshold level): $$ \dfrac{2V_{cc}}{3} = V_{cc} - \left(V_{cc} - \dfrac{V_{cc}}{3}\right) \cdot e^{-t_H / [(R_1 + R_2)C]} $$ **Step 4:** Simplify the term in the parenthesis: $$ V_{cc} - \dfrac{V_{cc}}{3} = \dfrac{2V_{cc}}{3} $$ Substituting back: $$ \dfrac{2V_{cc}}{3} = V_{cc} - \dfrac{2V_{cc}}{3} \cdot e^{-t_H / [(R_1 + R_2)C]} $$ **Step 5:** Rearrange to isolate the exponential term: $$ \dfrac{2V_{cc}}{3} \cdot e^{-t_H / [(R_1 + R_2)C]} = V_{cc} - \dfrac{2V_{cc}}{3} = \dfrac{V_{cc}}{3} $$ **Step 6:** Divide both sides by $\dfrac{2V_{cc}}{3}$: $$ e^{-t_H / [(R_1 + R_2)C]} = \dfrac{V_{cc}/3}{2V_{cc}/3} = \dfrac{1}{2} $$ **Step 7:** Take the natural logarithm ($\ln(1/2) = -\ln(2) \approx -0.693$): $$ -\dfrac{t_H}{(R_1 + R_2)C} = \ln\left(\dfrac{1}{2}\right) = -0.693 $$ **Step 8:** Final charging time expression: $$ \boxed{t_H = 0.693 \times (R_1 + R_2) \times C} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation of Astable Multivibrator Frequency (Discharging Cycle) When the output is **LOW**, the discharge transistor (Pin 7) is **ON** (connected to ground). The capacitor $C$ discharges through $R_2$ only (since Pin 7 short-circuits $R_1$ via ground). **Step 1:** Standard RC discharge equation starting from $V_i = \dfrac{2V_{cc}}{3}$ down to $V_f = \dfrac{V_{cc}}{3}$: $$ V_C(t) = V_i \cdot e^{-t / (R_2 C)} $$ **Step 2:** Apply final boundary condition $V_C(t_L) = \dfrac{V_{cc}}{3}$: $$ \dfrac{V_{cc}}{3} = \dfrac{2V_{cc}}{3} \cdot e^{-t_L / (R_2 C)} $$ **Step 3:** Simplify and take natural log: $$ e^{-t_L / (R_2 C)} = \dfrac{1}{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad -\dfrac{t_L}{R_2 C} = -0.693 $$ **Step 4:** Final discharging time expression: $$ \boxed{t_L = 0.693 \times R_2 \times C} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation of Total Period and Frequency **Step 1:** Total time period is the sum of charging and discharging durations: $$ T = t_H + t_L = 0.693(R_1 + R_2)C + 0.693 R_2 C $$ **Step 2:** Factor out $0.693 \times C$: $$ T = 0.693 \cdot C \cdot \left[ (R_1 + R_2) + R_2 \right] = 0.693 \cdot C \cdot (R_1 + 2R_2) $$ **Step 3:** The frequency $f = 1/T$: $$ f = \dfrac{1}{0.693 \cdot C \cdot (R_1 + 2R_2)} = \dfrac{1.44}{C \cdot (R_1 + 2R_2)} $$ $$ \boxed{f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \cdot C}} $$ ## 3.4 Derivation of Monostable Pulse Width In monostable mode, the capacitor charges from **0 V** (or near 0 V after the trigger) toward $V_{cc}$ through resistor $R$ until it reaches the threshold $\dfrac{2V_{cc}}{3}$. **Step 1:** RC charging equation with $V_i = 0$: $$ V_C(t) = V_{cc} \left( 1 - e^{-t / (RC)} \right) $$ **Step 2:** Set $V_C(T) = \dfrac{2V_{cc}}{3}$: $$ \dfrac{2V_{cc}}{3} = V_{cc} \left(1 - e^{-T / (RC)}\right) $$ **Step 3:** Simplify: $$ \dfrac{2}{3} = 1 - e^{-T / (RC)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad e^{-T / (RC)} = \dfrac{1}{3} $$ **Step 4:** Take natural log ($\ln(1/3) = -1.0986 \approx -1.1$): $$ -\dfrac{T}{RC} = -1.1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = 1.1 \cdot R \cdot C $$ $$ \boxed{T = 1.1 \times R \times C} $$ ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (Astable Design) **Problem:** Design a 555 astable circuit with $f = 1$ kHz and $D = 75\%$. **Step 1:** Express duty cycle formula: $$ D = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} = 0.75 $$ **Step 2:** Solve for $R_1$ in terms of $R_2$: $$ R_1 + R_2 = 0.75 R_1 + 1.5 R_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0.25 R_1 = 0.5 R_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_1 = 2 R_2 $$ **Step 3:** Substitute into frequency formula. Let $C = 0.1\ \mu F$ (chosen arbitrarily): $$ f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) C} = \dfrac{1.44}{(2R_2 + 2R_2) C} = \dfrac{1.44}{4 R_2 C} $$ **Step 4:** Solve for $R_2$: $$ 1000 = \dfrac{1.44}{4 \cdot R_2 \cdot 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_2 = \dfrac{1.44}{4 \cdot 1000 \cdot 10^{-7}} = 3600\ \Omega = 3.6\ k\Omega $$ **Step 5:** Therefore, $R_1 = 2 R_2 = 7.2\ k\Omega$. **Step 6:** Verify charging and discharging times: $$ t_H = 0.693 \times (7.2k + 3.6k) \times 0.1\mu = 0.693 \times 10.8k \times 0.1\mu = 0.748\ ms $$ $$ t_L = 0.693 \times 3.6k \times 0.1\mu = 0.249\ ms $$ $$ T = 0.748 + 0.249 = 0.997\ ms \approx 1\ ms \quad \Rightarrow \quad f \approx 1\ kHz \quad \checkmark $$ # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Astable Multivibrator - Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR VCC[("VCC Supply
+5V to +15V")] R1[("Resistor R1")] R2[("Resistor R2")] C[("Capacitor C")] TRIG[("Pin 2
Trigger")] THR[("Pin 6
Threshold")] DISC[("Pin 7
Discharge")] GND[("Ground")] OUT[("Pin 3
Output")] COMP1[/"Trigger
Comparator"/] COMP2[/"Threshold
Comparator"/] FF[("SR Flip-Flop")] TRX[("NPN Discharge
Transistor")] REF1(("VCC/3")) REF2(("2VCC/3")) VCC --- R1 R1 --- R2 R2 --- THR THR --- C C --- GND R1 --- DISC DISC --- TRX TRX --- GND THR --- COMP2 COMP2 --- FF TRIG --- COMP1 COMP1 --- FF FF --- TRX FF --- OUT REF1 --- COMP1 REF2 --- COMP2 THR -.Triggers when 2Vcc/3.-> COMP2 TRIG -.Triggers when Vcc/3.-> COMP1 ``` ## 4.2 Monostable Multivibrator - Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB VCC2[("VCC")] R_PULL[("Pull-up Resistor R")] C_EXT[("External Capacitor C")] THR2[("Pin 6
Threshold")] TRIG2[("Pin 2
Trigger Input")] DISC2[("Pin 7
Discharge")] OUT2[("Pin 3
Output Pulse")] GND2[("Ground")] STABLE{"Stable State
Output LOW?"} VCC2 --- R_PULL R_PULL --- THR2 THR2 --- DISC2 DISC2 --- C_EXT C_EXT --- GND2 TRIG2 -.Negative Pulse.-> STABLE STABLE ---|Trigger Event| OUT2 OUT2 ---|Goes HIGH for T=1.1RC| THR2 ``` ## 4.3 Astable Output Waveform - Timing Relationship ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph charging["CHARGING CYCLE tH"] CH1["Vcap rises from Vcc/3 to 2Vcc/3"] CH2["Path: Vcc through R1 then R2 into C"] CH3["Pin 7 is OFF open circuit"] end subgraph discharging["DISCHARGING CYCLE tL"] DC1["Vcap falls from 2Vcc/3 to Vcc/3"] DC2["Path: C through R2 into Pin 7 to GND"] DC3["Pin 7 is ON grounded"] end subgraph output["OUTPUT AT PIN 3"] OP1["HIGH during charging"] OP2["LOW during discharging"] end charging ==> output discharging ==> output ``` ## 4.4 Pin Description Matrix (Quick Reference for KTU Board Drawings) | Pin Number | Pin Name | Function in Astable | Function in Monostable | |:---:|:---:|:---|:---| | 1 | GND | Common ground reference | Common ground reference | | 2 | TRIGGER | Tied to Pin 6 (junction of R2 & C) | External negative trigger input | | 3 | OUTPUT | Square wave output (f = 1.44/[(R1+2R2)C]) | Single pulse output (T = 1.1RC) | | 4 | RESET | Tied to Vcc (disable reset) | Tied to Vcc (disable reset) | | 5 | CTRL VOLT | Bypassed to GND via 0.01 µF cap | Bypassed to GND via 0.01 µF cap | | 6 | THRESHOLD | Tied to Pin 2 (capacitor voltage sensing) | Tied to Pin 7 (capacitor voltage sensing) | | 7 | DISCHARGE | Connected between R1 and R2 | Connected between R and C | | 8 | V+ | Supply voltage +Vcc | Supply voltage +Vcc | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define the term "Duty Cycle" with reference to a 555 astable multivibrator. What is the minimum possible duty cycle in the standard configuration, and why? **Model Answer:** The duty cycle (D) of an astable multivibrator is defined as the ratio of the time the output remains HIGH ($t_H$) to the total time period (T), expressed as a percentage. $$ D = \dfrac{t_H}{T} \times 100\% = \dfrac{(R_1 + R_2)}{(R_1 + 2R_2)} \times 100\% $$ The minimum possible duty cycle in the standard configuration is **50%**. This is because in the standard 555 astable circuit, the charging path includes both $R_1$ and $R_2$ in series, while the discharging path uses only $R_2$. Since $R_1 \geq 0$, the charging time is always greater than or equal to the discharging time, making $t_H \geq t_L$, and therefore $D \geq 50\%$. To achieve D < 50%, a bypass diode must be added across R2. --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`** List any three applications of the 555 timer IC in monostable mode. **Model Answer:** Three applications of the 555 timer in monostable mode are: 1. **Pulse Stretching** - Used to widen narrow input pulses to make them measurable. 2. **Missing Pulse Detector** - Used in industrial monitoring systems to detect when a periodic pulse train is interrupted. 3. **Switch Debouncing** - Used to eliminate the contact bounce of mechanical switches by producing a single clean output pulse per switch activation. --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks - Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an astable multivibrator using 555 timer IC. Derive the expression for frequency of oscillation and duty cycle. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Circuit Description:** In the astable configuration, the 555 timer is connected such that: - Pin 1 is connected to **ground**. - Pins 2 and 6 are shorted together and connected to the junction of resistor $R_2$ and capacitor $C$. - Pin 7 (discharge) is connected between $R_1$ and $R_2$. - Pin 4 (reset) is tied to $V_{cc}$ to disable reset. - Pin 5 is bypassed to ground via a **0.01 µF** capacitor. - Pin 8 is connected to $V_{cc}$ (+5V to +15V). - Pin 3 provides the square wave output. **Working Principle:** **Phase 1 - Charging (Output HIGH):** Initially, suppose the flip-flop is in the RESET state, so the discharge transistor (Pin 7) is OFF. The capacitor charges through the series combination of $R_1$ and $R_2$ from $V_{cc}$. The voltage across the capacitor rises exponentially from $\frac{V_{cc}}{3}$ toward $V_{cc}$. During this time, the output at Pin 3 is **HIGH**. When $V_C$ reaches $\frac{2V_{cc}}{3}$, the threshold comparator triggers and SETs the flip-flop, ending the charging phase. **Phase 2 - Discharging (Output LOW):** With the flip-flop SET, the discharge transistor turns ON, grounding Pin 7. The capacitor now discharges through $R_2$ only into Pin 7. The voltage across $C$ falls exponentially from $\frac{2V_{cc}}{3}$ toward 0. During this time, the output at Pin 3 is **LOW**. When $V_C$ falls to $\frac{V_{cc}}{3}$, the trigger comparator activates and RESETs the flip-flop, ending the discharge phase and restarting the cycle. **Derivation (covered in Section 3):** The charging time, discharging time, frequency, and duty cycle formulas are: $$ t_H = 0.693(R_1 + R_2)C, \quad t_L = 0.693 R_2 C $$ $$ f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2)C}, \quad D = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\% $$ --- **(b)** Design a 555 monostable multivibrator to generate an output pulse of width **5 milliseconds**. Assume $C = 0.1\ \mu F$. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** Pulse width $T = 5\ ms = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ s$, $C = 0.1\ \mu F = 0.1 \times 10^{-6}\ F = 10^{-7}\ F$ **Step 1:** State the monostable pulse width formula: $$ T = 1.1 \times R \times C $$ **Step 2:** Substitute the known values and solve for $R$: $$ 5 \times 10^{-3} = 1.1 \times R \times 10^{-7} $$ $$ R = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 10^{-7}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{4}}{1.1} = 45,454.5\ \Omega \approx 45.45\ k\Omega $$ **Step 3:** Practical component selection: Use standard value $R = 47\ k\Omega$ (closest E12 series value). **Step 4:** Circuit connections: - Pin 1 → Ground - Pin 2 → External negative trigger pulse (active LOW, must drop below $V_{cc}/3$) - Pin 3 → Output (provides single pulse of 5 ms duration on trigger) - Pin 4 → $V_{cc}$ (disable reset) - Pin 5 → 0.01 µF capacitor to ground (noise filtering) - Pin 6 → Connected to Pin 7 (threshold sensing of capacitor voltage) - Pin 7 → Connected to junction of R and C (discharge path) - Pin 8 → $V_{cc}$ = +5V (supply) **Step 5:** Operating sequence: 1. In the **stable state**, the output is LOW. The internal flip-flop holds the discharge transistor ON, keeping the capacitor shorted to ground. 2. When a **negative trigger pulse** (< $V_{cc}/3$) is applied to Pin 2, the trigger comparator SETs the flip-flop. 3. The discharge transistor turns OFF, allowing C to charge through R toward $V_{cc}$. 4. The output goes **HIGH** simultaneously. 5. When $V_C$ reaches $\frac{2V_{cc}}{3}$, the threshold comparator RESETs the flip-flop, turning the discharge transistor back ON, dropping the output to LOW, and rapidly discharging the capacitor. 6. The circuit returns to its stable state, ready for the next trigger. **Final Answer:** $R \approx 45.45\ k\Omega$ (use standard $47\ k\Omega$), $C = 0.1\ \mu F$. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` **(a)** Draw the internal block diagram of 555 timer IC and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** The internal block diagram of the 555 timer consists of **four main functional blocks:** **Block 1: Voltage Divider (Three 5 kΩ Resistors)** Three identical 5 kΩ resistors are connected in series between $V_{cc}$ (Pin 8) and Ground (Pin 1). This divider generates two reference voltages: - $V_{ref1} = \dfrac{V_{cc}}{3}$ at the non-inverting input of the Trigger Comparator. - $V_{ref2} = \dfrac{2V_{cc}}{3}$ at the inverting input of the Threshold Comparator. The name "555" derives from these three internal 5 kΩ resistors. **Block 2: Two Comparators** - **Trigger Comparator (Comparator 1):** Inverting input is connected to Pin 2 (Trigger), non-inverting input to $V_{cc}/3$. Output is HIGH when Pin 2 voltage < $V_{cc}/3$. - **Threshold Comparator (Comparator 2):** Non-inverting input is connected to Pin 6 (Threshold), inverting input to $2V_{cc}/3$. Output is HIGH when Pin 6 voltage > $2V_{cc}/3$. **Block 3: SR Flip-Flop (Latch)** The output of the Trigger Comparator connects to the SET (S) input, while the output of the Threshold Comparator connects to the RESET (R) input of the SR flip-flop. The Q output drives the output buffer stage. The complementary $\bar{Q}$ output drives the discharge transistor. The flip-flop also has a direct RESET pin (Pin 4) which is active LOW. **Block 4: Output Buffer and Discharge Transistor** - A **push-pull output stage** (consisting of a Darlington pair for sourcing current and an NPN transistor for sinking current) drives Pin 3, capable of sourcing or sinking up to 200 mA. - An **NPN open-collector transistor** with its collector at Pin 7 provides the discharge path. When the flip-flop is RESET, the transistor is ON, connecting Pin 7 to ground. When SET, the transistor is OFF (open circuit). --- **(b)** A 555 timer is connected in astable mode with $R_1 = 4.7\ k\Omega$, $R_2 = 1\ k\Omega$, and $C = 0.01\ \mu F$. Calculate the frequency of oscillation, duty cycle, charging time, and discharging time. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $R_1 = 4.7\ k\Omega = 4.7 \times 10^3\ \Omega$, $R_2 = 1\ k\Omega = 1 \times 10^3\ \Omega$, $C = 0.01\ \mu F = 0.01 \times 10^{-6}\ F = 10^{-8}\ F$ **Step 1: Calculate Charging Time (tH)** $$ t_H = 0.693 \times (R_1 + R_2) \times C $$ $$ t_H = 0.693 \times (4.7 \times 10^3 + 1 \times 10^3) \times 10^{-8} $$ $$ t_H = 0.693 \times 5.7 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8} = 0.693 \times 5.7 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ \boxed{t_H = 3.95 \times 10^{-5}\ s = 39.5\ \mu s} $$ **Step 2: Calculate Discharging Time (tL)** $$ t_L = 0.693 \times R_2 \times C = 0.693 \times 1 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8} $$ $$ \boxed{t_L = 6.93 \times 10^{-6}\ s = 6.93\ \mu s} $$ **Step 3: Calculate Total Time Period (T)** $$ T = t_H + t_L = 39.5\ \mu s + 6.93\ \mu s = 46.43\ \mu s $$ **Step 4: Calculate Frequency of Oscillation (f)** $$ f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{46.43 \times 10^{-6}} $$ $$ \boxed{f \approx 21,536\ Hz \approx 21.54\ kHz} $$ **Step 5: Calculate Duty Cycle (D)** $$ D = \dfrac{t_H}{T} \times 100\% = \dfrac{39.5}{46.43} \times 100\% $$ $$ \boxed{D \approx 85.07\%} $$ **Verification using alternate formula:** $$ D = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\% = \dfrac{4.7k + 1k}{4.7k + 2k} \times 100\% = \dfrac{5.7}{6.7} \times 100\% \approx 85.07\% \quad \checkmark $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the constant 0.693 vs 0.7:** The factor 0.693 comes from $-\ln(0.5) = \ln(2)$. Using 0.7 instead will cost **0.5 to 1 mark** per wrong answer in derivations. > 2. **Unit Mismatch:** Always convert R to Ω and C to F before multiplying. A common error is using kΩ and µF directly, which gives wrong time scale. > 3. **Confusing 1.1 vs 0.693:** Monostable uses **$T = 1.1 RC$** (from $\ln(3) = 1.0986$); Astable uses **$0.693 RC$** (from $\ln(2) = 0.693$). Mixing these constants is a frequent error. > 4. **Duty Cycle Confusion:** The standard astable CANNOT give D < 50%. If the problem asks for D = 25%, students must add a diode across R2 and recalculate. > 5. **Pin 5 Floating:** Forgetting to bypass Pin 5 with a 0.01 µF capacitor causes noise-induced false triggering. Examiners deduct marks if the control voltage pin is left unconnected in the circuit diagram. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:** - **555 IC Origin Name:** Three internal **5 kΩ** resistors → "555". - **Two Reference Voltages:** $\frac{V_{cc}}{3}$ (Trigger threshold) and $\frac{2V_{cc}}{3}$ (Threshold threshold). - **Output Current Capability:** Maximum **±200 mA** (can directly drive small relays, LEDs, speakers). - **Supply Range:** +4.5 V to +16 V (typical **+5 V** for digital, **+9 V to +15 V** for analog/timing). - **Astable Formula Trio:** - $t_H = 0.693(R_1 + R_2)C$ - $t_L = 0.693 R_2 C$ - $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2)C}$ - **Monostable Formula:** $T = 1.1 RC$ (the **1.1** comes from $\ln(3)$, not $\ln(2)$). - **Duty Cycle Rule:** Standard astable gives $D > 50\%$ always. Add **diode across R2** (cathode toward Pin 7) to get D < 50%. - **Trigger Condition:** Monostable trigger is **active LOW**; pulse must go below $\frac{V_{cc}}{3}$. - **Reset Pin 4:** Must be tied HIGH to $V_{cc}$; pulling LOW forces output LOW (used for emergency shutdown). - **Pin 5 Decoupling:** Always connect a **0.01 µF** capacitor from Pin 5 to ground to prevent false triggering from supply noise. - **Practical Component Range:** R from **1 kΩ to 1 MΩ**; C from **100 pF to 1000 µF** for stable operation. - **Astable Output Frequency Range:** Practically **0.001 Hz to 500 kHz** (above 500 kHz, timing becomes inaccurate). - **Monostable Pulse Width Range:** Practically **10 µs to several minutes** (theoretically unlimited). - **KTU Viva Tip:** Be prepared to explain why $R_1$ cannot be zero in astable mode (it would short the supply through the discharge transistor when ON, drawing destructive current).

topics.title: Pre-lab Assignment: Familiarisation of Logic Gates, Identification of Typical Logic ICs, Interpreting IC Datasheets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEOL307 - ANALOG AND DIGITAL ELECTRONICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Pre-lab Assignment: Familiarisation of Logic Gates, Identification of Typical Logic ICs, Interpreting IC Datasheets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Pre-Lab Assignment: Scope & Purpose

The Pre-Lab Assignment for Experiment 2 forms the foundational readiness test before the student is permitted to handle the digital trainer kit, ICs, and oscilloscope. It is a *paper-and-pen* plus *datasheet-reading* exercise that validates three core competencies required by the KTU 2024 Scheme lab manual:

1. **Logical Competence** — Recognising and stating the truth table, Boolean expression, and logic symbol of the seven elementary gates (AND, OR, NOT, NAND, NOR, XOR, XNOR).
2. **Hardware Competence** — Visually identifying a TTL 74xx or CMOS 40xx series IC from its package marking, knowing how to orient pin 1, and locating Vcc/Vdd and GND pins.
3. **Specification Competence** — Reading manufacturer datasheets to extract *threshold voltages, propagation delays, fan-out, and power dissipation* before powering the IC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Syllabus Directive (PCEOL307):** The pre-lab questions in the lab record carry **10 marks** (typically split as 4 + 3 + 3 across the three competencies above). These marks are awarded *only* if the student writes the pre-lab answers *before* the day of the experiment and gets them countersigned.

## 1.2 Formal Definition of a Logic Gate

A **logic gate** is a single-input or multi-input electronic building block that implements a deterministic Boolean function on binary inputs (logic **0 = LOW ≈ 0 V**, logic **1 = HIGH ≈ +5 V** for TTL) to produce a single binary output. Gates are the atomic units of all combinational and sequential digital circuits.

Mathematically, for $n$ binary inputs $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \in \{0,1\}$, a gate computes:

$$
F: \{0,1\}^n \rightarrow \{0,1\}
$$

The seven elementary gates and their Boolean expressions are:

| Gate | Boolean Expression | Mnemonic |
|------|--------------------|----------|
| AND  | $F = A \cdot B$ | All HIGH → HIGH |
| OR   | $F = A + B$ | Any HIGH → HIGH |
| NOT  | $F = \overline{A}$ | Inverter |
| NAND | $F = \overline{A \cdot B}$ | Universal gate |
| NOR  | $F = \overline{A + B}$ | Universal gate |
| XOR  | $F = A \oplus B$ | Difference detector |
| XNOR | $F = \overline{A \oplus B}$ | Equality detector |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Voting Booth" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine each logic gate as a tiny **voting booth**.
> - An **AND gate** is a *strict committee*: the output light turns ON **only if every member votes YES**. One NO vote from any member keeps the light OFF.
> - An **OR gate** is a *lenient committee*: the output light turns ON if **at least one member votes YES**.
> - A **NOT gate** is a *contrarian*: whatever the input decides, the output does the **exact opposite**.
> - A **NAND gate** is a *strict committee with one rebel*: everyone says YES, but a built-in rebel flips the final sign to NO.
> - An **XOR gate** is a *disagreement detector*: the output is YES **only when the two inputs disagree** (one YES, one NO).

This "voting booth" mental model helps you remember truth tables instantly during the viva voce.

## 1.4 Formal Definition of an IC Datasheet

An **IC datasheet** is the manufacturer's authoritative specification document that defines the **electrical, mechanical, thermal, and timing** boundaries within which an integrated circuit is guaranteed to operate correctly. It is the *contract* between the manufacturer and the circuit designer.

For a 74LS08 (Quad 2-input AND), a datasheet provides, *inter alia*:
- Absolute maximum ratings (e.g., $V_{CC} \le \mathbf{7\,V}$)
- Recommended operating conditions (e.g., $V_{CC} = \mathbf{5\,V \pm 5\%}$)
- DC electrical characteristics ($V_{IH}, V_{IL}, V_{OH}, V_{OL}, I_{IH}, I_{IL}$)
- AC switching characteristics ($t_{PLH}, t_{PHL}$, propagation delay)
- Package pinout (top view with notch/dot indicating pin 1)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU Tests Datasheet Reading:** A student who *cannot read a datasheet* cannot troubleshoot. If an LED refuses to light up at the output of a 7432, the answer is *not* "blame the wire" — it is "check whether the input HIGH voltage meets the $V_{IH(min)}$ specification of **2.0 V** for standard TTL."

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Dominant Logic Families: TTL vs CMOS

Every logic IC the student handles in PCEOL307 belongs to one of two transistor-level technologies. The pre-lab *must* differentiate them.

### 2.1.1 Transistor-Transistor Logic (TTL) — 74xx Series

TTL uses **bipolar junction transistors (BJTs)** as the active switching elements. The "74" prefix denotes the commercial temperature range (0 °C to 70 °C); the "54" prefix denotes the military range (–55 °C to +125 °C).

Sub-families (KTU-relevant):

| Sub-family | Example IC | Full Part Number | Key Property |
|------------|------------|------------------|--------------|
| Standard TTL | 7400 | SN7400 | Baseline, high noise margin |
| Low-power Schottky | 74LS00 | SN74LS00 | Lower power, Schottky clamping |
| Advanced Schottky | 74AS00 | SN74AS00 | Higher speed |
| Advanced Low-power Schottky | 74ALS00 | SN74ALS00 | Best speed-power trade-off |

### 2.1.2 Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor (CMOS) — 40xx / 74HCxx Series

CMOS uses complementary **p-channel and n-channel MOSFETs**. The "40xx" series (e.g., 4011, 4081) is the original CMOS line operating from **3 V to 18 V**. The "74HCxx" series (e.g., 74HC00) is the CMOS pin-compatible replacement for TTL with 2 V to 6 V supply.

### 2.1.3 Structural Comparison

| Parameter | TTL (74LS00) | CMOS (4011) |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| Active device | BJT | MOSFET |
| Supply $V_{CC}/V_{DD}$ | $\mathbf{5\,V \pm 5\%}$ | $\mathbf{3\,V \text{ to } 18\,V}$ |
| Input impedance | Low (~4 k$\Omega$) | Extremely high (~10$^{12}$ $\Omega$) |
| Static power dissipation | Moderate (~2 mW/gate) | Negligible (~10 nW/gate) |
| Speed (propagation delay) | ~9.5 ns | ~50 ns (at 5 V, 50 pF) |
| Noise margin | ~0.4 V (typical) | ~1.5 V (at 5 V supply) |
| Output drive current | High (8 mA typ) | Limited (0.5 mA typ at 5 V) |
| Susceptibility to ESD | Low | **High** (handle with care!) |

## 2.2 Identification of Typical Logic ICs — KTU High-Yield List

The following ICs are the **workhorses** of every KTU digital electronics lab. The pre-lab examiner expects pin-by-pin knowledge of at least the first five.

| IC Number | Family | Function | Pinout (14-pin DIP) |
|-----------|--------|----------|---------------------|
| **7400** | TTL | Quad 2-input NAND | Pin 14 = Vcc, Pin 7 = GND |
| **7402** | TTL | Quad 2-input NOR | Pin 14 = Vcc, Pin 7 = GND |
| **7404** | TTL | Hex Inverter (NOT ×6) | Pin 14 = Vcc, Pin 7 = GND |
| **7408** | TTL | Quad 2-input AND | Pin 14 = Vcc, Pin 7 = GND |
| **7432** | TTL | Quad 2-input OR | Pin 14 = Vcc, Pin 7 = GND |
| **7486** | TTL | Quad 2-input XOR | Pin 14 = Vcc, Pin 7 = GND |
| **4011** | CMOS | Quad 2-input NAND | Pin 14 = Vdd, Pin 7 = Vss |
| **4081** | CMOS | Quad 2-input AND | Pin 14 = Vdd, Pin 7 = Vss |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Pinout Rule for 14-pin DIPs:** For *all* 74xx TTL and *most* 40xx CMOS gates in a 14-pin package, **Pin 14 = Supply (Vcc/Vdd)** and **Pin 7 = Ground (GND/Vss)**. This is the single most important "cheat code" for IC identification on the bench.

## 2.3 Datasheet Parameters — The KTU Formula Sheet

The following table is the **complete cheat sheet** of every quantity a KTU examiner can ask from a datasheet. Memorise the symbols and the standard TTL (74LS) numerical values at $V_{CC} = \mathbf{5\,V}$ and $T_A = 25\,\text{°C}$.

| Symbol | Parameter | Definition | TTL (74LS) Typical Value | Unit |
|--------|-----------|------------|---------------------------|------|
| $V_{CC}$ / $V_{DD}$ | Supply voltage | Power applied to the IC | $\mathbf{5}$ (TTL), $\mathbf{3\text{–}18}$ (CMOS) | V |
| $V_{IH(min)}$ | Minimum HIGH input voltage | Smallest input recognised as logic 1 | $\mathbf{2.0}$ | V |
| $V_{IL(max)}$ | Maximum LOW input voltage | Largest input still recognised as logic 0 | $\mathbf{0.8}$ | V |
| $V_{OH(min)}$ | Minimum HIGH output voltage | Guaranteed output HIGH level | $\mathbf{2.7}$ | V |
| $V_{OL(max)}$ | Maximum LOW output voltage | Guaranteed output LOW level | $\mathbf{0.5}$ | V |
| $I_{IH}$ | HIGH-level input current | Current into input when HIGH | $\mathbf{20}$ | $\mu$A |
| $I_{IL}$ | LOW-level input current | Current out of input when LOW | $\mathbf{-0.4}$ | mA |
| $I_{OH}$ | HIGH-level output current | Output source current | $\mathbf{-0.4}$ | mA |
| $I_{OL}$ | LOW-level output current | Output sink current | $\mathbf{8}$ | mA |
| $t_{PLH}$ | Propagation delay, LOW-to-HIGH | Time from input change to output rising | $\mathbf{9}$ | ns |
| $t_{PHL}$ | Propagation delay, HIGH-to-LOW | Time from input change to output falling | $\mathbf{10}$ | ns |
| $t_{pd}$ | Average propagation delay | $(t_{PLH}+t_{PHL})/2$ | $\mathbf{9.5}$ | ns |
| Fan-out | Maximum load drives | Number of standard loads the output can drive | $\mathbf{20}$ (LS-TTL) | unitless |
| $P_D$ | Static power dissipation | $V_{CC} \times I_{CC}$ per gate | $\mathbf{2}$ | mW |

## 2.4 Derivation of Noise Margin

The **DC noise margin** quantifies how much unwanted noise voltage the gate can tolerate on its inputs without flipping the logic level.

The HIGH-side noise margin is:

$$
NM_H = V_{OH(min)} - V_{IH(min)}
$$

The LOW-side noise margin is:

$$
NM_L = V_{IL(max)} - V_{OL(max)}
$$

For 74LS TTL, substituting the values from the table above:

$$
NM_H = 2.7 - 2.0 = \mathbf{0.7\,V}
$$

$$
NM_L = 0.8 - 0.5 = \mathbf{0.3\,V}
$$

## 2.5 Propagation Delay — The Speed Bottleneck

Propagation delay is the time interval between the **50% transition point of the input** and the **corresponding 50% transition point of the output**. Two delays exist because the gate is not perfectly symmetric:

- $t_{PLH}$ — input to output transition from LOW to HIGH
- $t_{PHL}$ — input to output transition from HIGH to LOW

The **average propagation delay** used in datasheets and speed-limit calculations is:

$$
t_{pd} = \frac{t_{PLH} + t_{PHL}}{2}
$$

## 2.6 Fan-Out Computation

Fan-out is the maximum number of *standard* gate inputs that one gate output can reliably drive. It is computed as the smaller of the two ratios:

$$
\text{Fan-out} = \min\!\left( \frac{\vert I_{OL} \vert}{\vert I_{IL} \vert},\; \frac{\vert I_{OH} \vert}{\vert I_{IH} \vert} \right)
$$

For 74LS00 driving other 74LS loads:

$$
\text{Fan-out} = \min\!\left( \frac{8\,\text{mA}}{0.4\,\text{mA}},\; \frac{0.4\,\text{mA}}{20\,\mu\text{A}} \right) = \min(20, 20) = \mathbf{20}
$$

## 2.7 Real-World Utility of This Knowledge

| Engineering Domain | Application of Pre-Lab Knowledge |
|--------------------|----------------------------------|
| Microcontroller board design | Choosing 74HC vs 74LS buffers for 5 V tolerance |
| FPGA prototyping | Substituting discrete TTL/CMOS glue logic |
| Industrial PLC design | Selecting logic families for noisy environments (CMOS preferred) |
| Repair & troubleshooting | Reading manufacturer markings on legacy boards |
| Battery-powered IoT | Choosing 4000-series CMOS for near-zero static current |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow When Reading an IC

The following matrix is the step-by-step reasoning chain a student must follow when handed an unmarked IC on the bench. It is exactly what the KTU examiner looks for in the pre-lab record.

**Step 1 — Locate the manufacturer's logo and part number** printed on the top of the IC. Common manufacturers: Texas Instruments (TI), Motorola, Fairchild, Signetics, Philips.

**Step 2 — Identify the family prefix** ("74" = TTL, "54" = TTL-mil, "40" = CMOS, "74HC/HCT" = high-speed CMOS).

**Step 3 — Identify the function number** from the three or four digits that follow (00, 02, 04, 08, 32, 86, 125, 138, 151, etc.).

**Step 4 — Identify the package** by the second suffix letter (N = plastic DIP, J = ceramic DIP, D = SOIC, PW = TSSOP).

**Step 5 — Locate pin 1** using either the **notch** on one end of the DIP or the **small dot** next to pin 1. With pin 1 identified and the IC oriented "notch to the left", pin numbering proceeds counter-clockwise down the left side, then up the right side.

**Step 6 — Identify Vcc/Vdd and GND** using the standard pinout rule for that IC.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Datasheet Noise Margin Calculation

**Problem:** From the datasheet of a 74LS08 (Quad 2-input AND gate), the following DC parameters are tabulated at $V_{CC} = 5\,\text{V}$:

$$
V_{OH(min)} = 2.7\,\text{V},\quad V_{IH(min)} = 2.0\,\text{V}
$$

$$
V_{OL(max)} = 0.5\,\text{V},\quad V_{IL(max)} = 0.8\,\text{V}
$$

Compute the HIGH-side and LOW-side DC noise margins.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — State the governing equations for noise margin.

$$
NM_H = V_{OH(min)} - V_{IH(min)}
$$

$$
NM_L = V_{IL(max)} - V_{OL(max)}
$$

Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values for the HIGH-side margin.

$$
NM_H = 2.7\,\text{V} - 2.0\,\text{V} = \mathbf{0.7\,V}
$$

Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values for the LOW-side margin.

$$
NM_L = 0.8\,\text{V} - 0.5\,\text{V} = \mathbf{0.3\,V}
$$

Step 4 — Interpretation. The HIGH-side margin of 0.7 V is the amount of *positive-going* noise the input can absorb while still being read as a logic 1. The LOW-side margin of 0.3 V is the *negative-going* noise tolerance on a logic 0 input.

> [!NOTE]
> **Asymmetry Insight:** TTL has *asymmetric* noise margins ($NM_H > NM_L$), whereas CMOS has *near-symmetric* noise margins (typically $NM_H \approx NM_L \approx 1.5\,\text{V}$ at $V_{DD} = 5\,\text{V}$). This is one of the reasons CMOS is preferred in electrically noisy industrial environments.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Average Propagation Delay

**Problem:** The datasheet of a 74LS32 (Quad 2-input OR gate) specifies:

$$
t_{PLH} = 14\,\text{ns},\quad t_{PHL} = 14\,\text{ns}
$$

Compute the average propagation delay $t_{pd}$ and the maximum operating frequency of this gate in a ring-oscillator configuration (odd number of inversions giving one cycle = 2 gate delays).

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Apply the average definition.

$$
t_{pd} = \frac{t_{PLH} + t_{PHL}}{2}
$$

Step 2 — Substitute the datasheet values.

$$
t_{pd} = \frac{14 + 14}{2} = \mathbf{14\,ns}
$$

Step 3 — For a ring oscillator with $N$ inverters, the oscillation period is the time for one round-trip:

$$
T = 2 \cdot N \cdot t_{pd}
$$

For $N = 3$ inverters (the minimum odd integer):

$$
T = 2 \cdot 3 \cdot 14\,\text{ns} = 84\,\text{ns}
$$

Step 4 — Compute the oscillation frequency.

$$
f_{osc} = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{84 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{s}} \approx \mathbf{11.9\,MHz}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Fan-Out Calculation Across Logic Families

**Problem:** A 74LS00 NAND gate output is required to drive a mix of loads: 5 inputs of 74LS00 (each drawing $I_{IL} = 0.4\,\text{mA}$ and $I_{IH} = 20\,\mu\text{A}$) and 2 inputs of 74HC00 (each drawing $I_{IL} = 1\,\mu\text{A}$ and $I_{IH} = 1\,\mu\text{A}$). The 74LS00 output specs are $I_{OL} = 8\,\text{mA}$ and $I_{OH} = -0.4\,\text{mA}$. Compute the effective fan-out.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Sum the LOW-state loading currents.

$$
I_{IL(\text{total})} = 5 \cdot 0.4\,\text{mA} + 2 \cdot 0.001\,\text{mA} = 2.0 + 0.002 = \mathbf{2.002\,mA}
$$

Step 2 — Sum the HIGH-state loading currents.

$$
I_{IH(\text{total})} = 5 \cdot 0.020\,\text{mA} + 2 \cdot 0.001\,\text{mA} = 0.1 + 0.002 = \mathbf{0.102\,mA}
$$

Step 3 — Compute the LOW-side ratio (output sink capability divided by total sink demand).

$$
\text{Fan-out}_L = \frac{\vert I_{OL} \vert}{\vert I_{IL(\text{total})} \vert} = \frac{8\,\text{mA}}{2.002\,\text{mA}} \approx \mathbf{3.99}
$$

Step 4 — Compute the HIGH-side ratio.

$$
\text{Fan-out}_H = \frac{\vert I_{OH} \vert}{\vert I_{IH(\text{total})} \vert} = \frac{0.4\,\text{mA}}{0.102\,\text{mA}} \approx \mathbf{3.92}
$$

Step 5 — Effective fan-out is the minimum.

$$
\text{Fan-out}_{\text{eff}} = \min(3.99,\, 3.92) = \mathbf{3}
$$

(Fan-out is an integer count of *guaranteed* load drives; we round down to the conservative value.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Although 74LS datasheets quote a fan-out of 20, this assumes *all* loads are 74LS. The moment you mix CMOS loads with TTL outputs, the HIGH-side fan-out collapses dramatically because CMOS inputs draw almost zero current but the TTL output still cannot source more than 0.4 mA in the HIGH state. This is a classic KTU viva question.

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Power Dissipation Calculation

**Problem:** A 74LS138 (3-to-8 line decoder) datasheet specifies $I_{CC(max)} = 10\,\text{mA}$ at $V_{CC} = 5\,\text{V}$. Compute the total static power dissipation and the per-gate power (assuming the IC contains the equivalent of 15 gates).

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute the total IC power.

$$
P_{IC} = V_{CC} \cdot I_{CC} = 5\,\text{V} \cdot 10\,\text{mA} = \mathbf{50\,mW}
$$

Step 2 — Compute the per-gate dissipation.

$$
P_{\text{gate}} = \frac{P_{IC}}{15} = \frac{50\,\text{mW}}{15} \approx \mathbf{3.33\,mW/gate}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Pre-Lab Workflow

The following block diagram maps the conceptual flow of the pre-lab exercise — from physical IC identification to electrical validation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STEP 1: PHYSICAL IC] --> B[READ TOP MARKING]
    B --> C{FAMILY PREFIX}
    C -->|74 OR 54| D[TTL FAMILY]
    C -->|40 OR 74HC| E[CMOS FAMILY]
    D --> F[FUNCTION NUMBER 00 02 04 08 32 86]
    E --> G[FUNCTION NUMBER 11 81 71 30 69]
    F --> H[CHECK PIN 1 VIA NOTCH OR DOT]
    G --> H
    H --> I[APPLY UNIVERSAL PINOUT RULE]
    I --> J[PIN 14 IS VCC OR VDD]
    I --> K[PIN 7 IS GND OR VSS]
    H --> L[READ DATASHEET PARAMETERS]
    L --> M[VIH VIL VOH VOL IIH IIL]
    L --> N[TPLH TPHL FANOUT PD]
    M --> O[CONSTRUCT TRUTH TABLE]
    N --> O
    O --> P[PRELAB RECORD READY]
```

## 4.2 Gate Identification Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[LOGIC GATE TO IDENTIFY] --> B{HAS SINGLE INPUT}
    B -->|YES| C[NOT GATE INVERTER]
    B -->|NO| D{IS OUTPUT INVERTED}
    D -->|NO| E{ANY INPUT HIGH GIVES HIGH OUTPUT}
    D -->|YES| F{ALL INPUTS LOW GIVE HIGH OUTPUT}
    E -->|YES| G[OR GATE]
    E -->|NO| H[AND GATE]
    F -->|YES| I[NOR GATE]
    F -->|NO| J[NAND GATE]
    A --> K{XOR FAMILY}
    K -->|OUTPUT HIGH ON DISAGREEMENT| L[XOR GATE]
    K -->|OUTPUT HIGH ON AGREEMENT| M[XNOR GATE]
```

## 4.3 Pinout Schematic of a 14-pin DIP (Top View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph L[LEFT SIDE PINS 1 TO 7 TOP TO BOTTOM]
        P1[PIN 1] --> P2[PIN 2] --> P3[PIN 3] --> P4[PIN 4] --> P5[PIN 5] --> P6[PIN 6] --> P7[PIN 7 GND OR VSS]
    end
    subgraph R[RIGHT SIDE PINS 8 TO 14 BOTTOM TO TOP]
        P8[PIN 8] --> P9[PIN 9] --> P10[PIN 10] --> P11[PIN 11] --> P12[PIN 12] --> P13[PIN 13] --> P14[PIN 14 VCC OR VDD]
    end
    L ~~~ R
```

## 4.4 TTL NAND Gate Internal Block Architecture (74LS00)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[INPUT A] --> M1[MULTI EMITTER BJT Q1]
    B1[INPUT B] --> M1
    M1 --> Q2[PHASE SPLITTER Q2]
    Q2 --> D1[DIODE D1 D2 LEVEL CLAMP]
    Q2 --> Q3[Q3 PULL UP NETWORK]
    Q2 --> Q4[Q4 PULL DOWN NETWORK]
    Q3 --> OUT[OUTPUT Y]
    Q4 --> OUT
    D1 --> GND[GROUND RAIL]
```

## 4.5 CMOS NAND Gate Internal Block Architecture (4011)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[INPUT A] --> P1[P CHANNEL MOSFET MP1]
    A --> N1[N CHANNEL MOSFET MN1]
    B[INPUT B] --> P2[P CHANNEL MOSFET MP2]
    B --> N2[N CHANNEL MOSFET MN2]
    P1 --> OUT[OUTPUT Y]
    P2 --> P1
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> GND[GROUND VSS]
    P2 --> VDD[SUPPLY VDD]
    P1 --> VDD
```

## 4.6 Bench Setup Architecture for Pre-Lab Validation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DC[5V DC REGULATED POWER SUPPLY] --> VCC[RED LEAD TO PIN 14 VCC]
    DC --> GND[BLACK LEAD TO PIN 7 GND]
    SW1[SPDT SWITCH A] --> IA[INPUT A TO PIN 1]
    SW2[SPDT SWITCH B] --> IB[INPUT B TO PIN 2]
    IC[LOGIC GATE IC ON TRAINER] --> O[OUTPUT LED OR METER]
    O --> V[VERIFY AGAINST TRUTH TABLE]
```
